- The True Meaning Of Life (Animated Cinematic): Kind: captions Language: en-GB - [Narrator] Every day, over 150,000 people die. That's over 6,000 people every hour, over 100 people every minute, and about two people every single second. That means since you started watching this video, about 20 people have already died. (gentle music) And some of these people got hit by cars, others fell off a cliff, others may have had a heart attack, and others may have simply passed away from natural causes. Death is one of those things that every single living thing on Earth has in common, and death does not discriminate against your skin color, or your political beliefs, or where you were born, or how much money you have. No one can escape the incurable disease that we all are blessed with from birth called life. And it's interesting because even though we all logically know this, very few of us have really internalized this. How many of you have really sat down with your eyes closed in an empty room and truly imagined what your funeral would look like? Like who would even be at your funeral? Who would be there out of pure obligation and politeness, and who would be there because they actually wanted to be there. What would people say about you? What would they remember? And it's weird because you'd think because we all have this terminal disease called life, that we all would be more sympathetic and loving with one another. I mean, this life disease really is the one thing that all human beings have in common. Because in 120 years from now, every single human being on Earth will be dead, and a brand-new set of humans will be alive. And then in another 120 years after that, another brand new set of people will be alive. And this is the cycle of life. It just goes on and on and on. And when you start thinking about life like this, you really start to realize how short our time here on Earth really is. And you also start to realize that we all are on this journey together as one. And it also makes you question the way most people interact with one another. Like why do so many people get so angry with each other? Or why do people argue with each other? Why do people physically harm one another? Why are there so many parents who yell at their kids or even beat their kids? Why do so many spouses who supposedly love each other fight all the time? Why are there siblings who don't speak to each other to this day, over some disagreement that took place years ago? Why do best friends talk badly about each other behind each other's back? Why do human beings behave like this? I think a part of it is because we really have not sat down to really think about what the true meaning of life is. And I also think that most people really have not come to terms with the fact that they are not going to be here forever. And to be honest, I don't really claim to know what the true meaning of life is, okay? I think it's something that our human brain can't fully logically understand. It would kind of be like trying to explain consciousness to a dog or a cat or something. (chuckles) They just can't understand it. However, what I will say is there are some things that I think everyone should know. And I think the first step to actually understanding a topic like the meaning of life is to first align ourselves with the four truths of the universe. And the first truth is the truth of cause and effect, which means that every single thing you do or say has some kind of effect on the world around you and yourself. (gentle music continues) For example, let's say a number of years ago, you lost your temper and you got really mad at a loved one in your life. This could be a family member or a friend, or even that random person on the street, that one single incident where you lost it will have a lingering effect on that person to this day. Now, with that being said, it doesn't have to be some huge effect. It could be a very mild and small effect, and it could be buried deep within their subconscious that isn't necessarily visible on the exterior. But make no mistake, the effect is there and it will always be there. And there is nothing that you can do to undo that. And the reason why most people hate hearing this is because it instantly makes them accountable for every single thing that they have ever done, and being held accountable is something that most human beings hate. Now, to be clear, I'm not saying that we should all dwell in the past and feel sorry for ourselves, but what it does mean is that going forward, you should be very conscious of the things that you say and the things that you do within this world. And if you feel like you behaved in a way, or you really lost it on someone, you can always apologize to that person because it is never, ever too late to say I'm sorry. And the second truth of the universe is the law of impermanence, which means that every single thing here on Earth is temporary. Like our bodies, our minds, our relationships, even our brains. Even the video that you are watching right now, this video will probably be gone one day because the sun could explode and every single thing could be gone within a second. And funny enough, this is actually a good thing, and it shouldn't really make you feel bad or sad because it's none of those things. What it should make you do is really cherish each and every single moment that you have here on Earth. And to be clear, they don't have to be these big, special moments. They can be any moment, like that one time where you laughed so hard that tears fell from your eyes, or maybe it was that time where you simply were sitting alone and you were just gazing at the sunset. Because all we really have in this lifetime is the present moment. And the third truth is the law of suffering and attachment. Any time that we become attached to something or someone, you are basically making a contract with yourself to be unhappy, because like we just discussed, nothing here on Earth is permanent, which means that you will eventually lose that loved one, or you will eventually lose that watch, or that car, or that family member. Even your own body, you are going to lose. And the fourth truth is that we all are going to die one day. And the death truth is typically the toughest one for most people to accept because it often makes people really sad. But death does not have to be this horribly bad thing, or even a sad thing, because without death, the moments with our friends or our family would not really be that special or deep. Without death, everything would eventually become dull and meek and gray. And there would be no incentive to really love and cherish the moments that we have with one another. There would be no incentive to really laugh deeply, or love deeply, or even to be present. And these are the things that truly make life rich and worth living. And on top of that, death is one of those things that truly does connect all of us. Because it makes you realize that you and the seven other billion people here on Earth really are all on the same team, because this is our time. This is our time to collectively live life to the fullest as one. So the next time that you feel the need to yell at that loved one, or to yell at that person who cut you off in traffic, or to get revenge on that person who wronged you in the past, just remember that you are choosing to actively hurt your very own teammate. Make every moment count. Love deeply, laugh deeply, and be present to the moment, because at the end of the day, all we really have in life So don't let it go to waste. If you wanna learn more about life and motivation, then go ahead and click the video on the screen right now. I promise that you will love it. Thanks for watching, and I'll see you all very soon.

- 74 and Still Can’t Retire: The Painful Truth About Growing Old Broke: Kind: captions Language: en At 69 years old, he still wakes up before sunrise. Not because he wants to, not because he loves the quiet of the morning, but because when you've spent your whole life worrying about money, your body forgets how to rest. His knees hurt before his feet even touch the floor. His back tightens as he sits up in bed. The room is small, quiet, and colder than it should be. There's no luxury here, no comfort, just a worn blanket, a weak cup of coffee, and another day that feels heavier than the one before. He worked all his life. That's the part nobody understands. This isn't a man who wasted his money chasing fantasies. He didn't spend decades gambling, drinking away paychecks, or making reckless choices. He did what America told him to do. He worked. He showed up. He stayed loyal. He kept going when he was tired, when he was sick, when he wanted to quit. He believed that if he gave his best years to the job, someday the job, the system, the country, something would give a little back. But now he's 69, and there's nothing left. No real savings, no paid-off house, no retirement that can carry him, no soft place to land, just bills, rent, medication, groceries, and a fear so constant it feels like background noise. He doesn't talk about it much. Men from his generation usually don't. They were taught to keep moving, keep providing, keep their problems to themselves. So, when the money started running out, he didn't tell anyone. When the credit card balances crept up, he kept smiling. When the doctor added another prescription, he nodded and said, "I'll figure it out." That phrase, "I'll figure it out," has carried millions of older Americans farther than it ever should have. Because the truth is, he already figured it out. He figured out that working hard doesn't guarantee security. He figured out that inflation doesn't care how old you are. He figured out that the same paycheck that once supported a family can now disappear in a week. And he figured out something even harder. You can do everything right and still end up with nothing. For decades, he believed retirement was a finish line, not a glamorous one. He wasn't dreaming about cruise ships or beach houses. He just imagined peace. Maybe a small porch. Maybe mornings without an alarm clock. Maybe enough money to buy groceries without checking the account first. Maybe enough left over to help the grandkids once in a while. That was the dream. Not wealth, not luxury, just dignity. But dignity has gotten expensive in America. Now even basic survival feels like a full-time job. Rent rises. Food costs more. Insurance goes up. Utilities climb. And every unexpected expense feels like a direct threat. A car repair. A dental bill. A trip to urgent care. One bad month can tear apart what little stability a person has left. And at 69, there is no magic recovery plan. There's no plenty of time to rebuild. There's no second act waiting around the corner. There's just the quiet panic of realizing that the years ahead may be smaller, harder, and more uncertain than all the years behind you. He remembers being young and hearing older people talk about struggle. Back then, he thought old age meant slowing down. He thought after 40 or 50 years of work, a person would at least earn the right to breathe. He never imagined he'd be this age, still doing mental math in the grocery store, still deciding which bill can wait, still wondering what happens if his body gives out before the bills due. That's the cruelest part. Aging is hard enough. Aging while broke is something else entirely. Because poverty in old age doesn't just take money. It takes choices. It takes freedom. It takes pride. It turns simple decisions into daily stress. Should he fill the prescription or pay the electric bill? Should he buy decent food or stretch cheap meals for another week? Should he ask for help or protect what little pride he has left? Most people never see this part. They see an older man moving slowly through a store. They see gray hair, worn clothes, tired eyes. What they don't see is the invisible math running through his head every second. They don't see the lifetime of labor in those hands. They don't see the disappointment, the betrayal, the exhaustion of realizing that after all those years of doing what he was told, he somehow arrived at the end with less than he started with. And the question keeps coming back over and over. How does a man work his whole life and still have nothing left? That question is bigger than one person. Because this story is not rare. It's not shocking because it's unusual. It's shocking because it's becoming normal. All across the country, older Americans are facing the same quiet collapse people who played by the rules, trusted the process, and are now staring at a future built on scarcity instead of rest. In this video, we're going to talk about what really happens when a lifetime of work no longer leads to security, and why so many people are reaching old age only to discover that survival never stopped. Let's be honest about something people don't like to say out loud. For a lot of Americans, retirement was never truly a promise. It was a story. A story told to working people so they would keep going, keep clocking in, keep sacrificing, keep believing that one day all the strain would make sense. Work hard, stay loyal, save what you can, and eventually you'll be okay. But what happens when okay never comes? What happens when a man spends 40, 50 years working with his hands, wearing out his body, missing family time, taking overtime, skipping vacations, saying yes to every extra shift he can handle, and still ends up staring at a bank account that can't carry him through the month? That's where this story turns from sad to brutal. Because by 69, it's not just about money anymore. It's about time. And time is the one thing he cannot earn back. When he was younger, every problem seemed temporary. If the car broke down, he could work more hours. If rent went up, he could pick up a weekend shift. If Christmas got expensive, he could grind a little harder and recover later. Later. That word used to feel comforting. Later, he'd catch up. Later, he'd save more. Later, things would settle down. Later, life would finally get easier. But later arrived, and it brought another bill, then another one, then another year. Somewhere along the way, later stopped being hope and became a lie. He didn't notice it at first. Most people don't. Financial ruin in old age rarely arrives with one dramatic moment. It shows up slowly, quietly. It hides in ordinary decisions. It's the years of earning just enough to survive, but never enough to build. It's the medical deductible that wipes out a tax refund, the emergency expense that goes on a credit card, the rent increase that quietly kills the little breathing room you had, the paycheck that covers today, but leaves nothing for tomorrow. And that's how a lifetime disappears. Not in one giant failure, but in thousands of small losses. A little here, a little there, a little every year, until one day you wake up nearly 70 years old and realize the system took your strength, your youth, your energy, and in return it That realization changes a person. It makes them quieter. He doesn't dream the same way anymore. He doesn't think in terms of 5-year plans. He thinks in weeks, in due dates, in whether the old car will make it another month, in whether the pain in his chest is stress or something worse, in whether he can afford to find out. And maybe the hardest part is this. He's embarrassed. Not because he did something shameful, but because this country has taught people to treat financial hardship like a personal failure. So, instead of saying, "I worked in a broken system," he says, "Maybe I should have done more. Maybe I should have saved more. Maybe I should have invested earlier. Maybe I should have taken a different job. Maybe I should have known." But that kind of guilt is cruel. Because it rewrites history with information he didn't have when he was living it. When you're raising kids, paying rent, putting food on the table, helping family, dealing with layoffs, illness, inflation, debt, and everyday survival, you're not sitting around building some perfect retirement strategy. You're trying to get through the week. That's what so many people miss. Poverty in old age does not always come from irresponsibility. Sometimes it comes from responsibility. From spending your life taking care of everyone else first. From choosing the practical path again and again, until there's nothing left for you. He paid for school clothes. He paid for gas. He paid for repairs. He paid for birthdays, holidays, medicine, groceries, and emergencies. He paid for life, and life kept sending invoices. Now the people around him may assume he's fine. After all, he's still standing, still showing up, still saying, "I'm okay." But I'm okay can mean many things when you're older. It can mean, "I skipped that meal, but I'm okay. I'm behind on the bill, but I'm okay. My body hurts every day, but I'm okay. I'm terrified about the future, but I'm okay. That phrase becomes a kind of armor. Thin armor, but armor nonetheless. Because what's the alternative? Tell your children you're drowning? Tell your friends you can't afford the basics? Admit that after all these years, you're one emergency away from collapse? A lot of older people would rather suffer in silence than feel like a burden. And that silence is everywhere. It lives in cheap apartments and old mobile homes, in grocery store aisles and pharmacy lines, in senior discount days and overdue notices, in tired faces that smile politely while carrying a level of stress no one their age should still be carrying. This is not the retirement they imagined. This is endurance. This is survival in slow motion. And what makes it even worse is watching the world move on as if none of this is happening. Advice keeps coming from people who don't understand the weight of being old and broke. Just budget better. Just downsize. Just get a part-time job. As if a 69-year-old body can simply absorb more labor. As if the answer to a lifetime of exhaustion is more exhaustion. There comes a point where hustle is no longer heroic. It's heartbreaking. A man should not have to spend the final stretch of his life panicking over rent, counting prescription pills, or choosing between pride and survival. But for millions, that is exactly what old age has become. And once you see it clearly, you can't unsee it. Because this isn't only about one man losing money. It's about what he lost trying to earn it. His strength, his time, his peace of mind, his chance to rest. And now, with fewer years ahead than behind, he is left asking the kind of question that keeps people awake at night. If a lifetime of work still ends like this, what exactly was all that work for? There is a special kind of loneliness that comes with getting older and realizing your struggle is invisible, not hidden, and visible. People can see that you're aging. They can see the wrinkles, the slower walk, the tired posture, but they cannot see what it feels like to carry decades of effort inside a body that has almost nothing to show for it. At 69, he is not just tired. He is worn down in a way that younger people rarely understand. He knows what it means to keep going when you have no real option. He knows what it means to smile through bad news because breaking down won't pay the bill. He knows what it means to hear people say, "These should be your golden years." And wonder what gold they're talking about because there is nothing golden about fear. There is nothing golden about checking your bank balance before buying bread. There is nothing golden about pretending not to need help. There is nothing golden about sitting alone at night wondering how many more years your body can keep carrying a burden your heart is already tired of holding. And yet, this is where so many older Americans end up not retired, not secure, not comfortable, just surviving one more month and hoping nothing goes wrong. That phrase matters. Hoping nothing goes wrong. Because when you're young, mistakes can hurt you. When you're old and broke, mistakes can destroy you. A missed payment can spiral fast. A health problem can become a financial crisis overnight. A small setback can open the door to something far worse. Eviction, debt, dependency, humiliation. That is the emotional reality of late-life poverty. It is not just about lacking money. It is about living with almost no margin for error, no cushion, no safety net that feels real. Just a fragile arrangement held together by discipline, denial, and luck. And luck is a dangerous thing to depend on when you're 69. Maybe that's why he keeps replaying the past in his mind. Not because he enjoys it, but because when the future feels unstable, people start searching the past for answers. He thinks about the years he worked through pain, the times he said yes when his body was begging for no, the weekends he lost, the family moments he missed, the opportunities he passed up because the practical choice always seemed safer than the risky one. He remembers trusting employers who promised stability. He remembers believing that loyalty mattered. He remembers years when a raise felt meaningful, when groceries didn't feel like punishment, when the future still seemed reachable. And maybe what hurts most is not that he has little money now. Maybe what hurts most is the feeling that he was useful for so long, and now the world has no use for his sacrifice. That kind of pain is hard to describe. A man can spend his whole life being needed, needed at work, needed at home, needed by children, by family, by everyone who relied on him to stay strong. Then one day he gets older, slower, more expensive to insure, less able to keep up. And suddenly the same world that demanded everything from him begins to look through him, as if his struggle is outdated, as if his hardship is his own private failure, as if decades of labor can simply be erased by one painful fact. He is old, and he does not have enough. That's when shame starts trying to write the story. Shame says he should have planned better. Shame says he should have been smarter. Shame says if he ended up here, he must have done something wrong. But shame lies because the truth is millions of people are reaching this stage of life exhausted, underfunded, and deeply afraid not because they were lazy, but because they lived in a system where wages lagged behind reality, where emergencies swallowed savings, where healthcare costs punished the vulnerable, and where simply staying alive kept getting more expensive. He is not an exception. He is a warning. A warning about what happens when work becomes a lifetime offering and security never arrives. A warning about what happens when people are praised for resilience while being denied stability. A warning about a culture that celebrates hard work, but too often abandons the people who gave their hardest years. And still, every morning he gets up. That might be the most heartbreaking part of all. He still gets up. He still folds the same clothes, drinks the same coffee, checks the same numbers, and tries to stretch what cannot be stretched. He still speaks carefully, spends carefully, hopes carefully. He still protects other people from the truth of how scared he is. Because even now, after everything, some part of him still believes it is his job to carry the weight quietly. That is what his generation learned. Don't complain. Don't burden others. Handle it. Push through. Stay strong. But strength, when abused for decades, starts to look a lot like suffering. And suffering, when normalized, becomes easy for society to ignore. That is why stories like this matter. Because they force us to look directly at something many people would rather avoid. The possibility that an honest life can still end in instability. That a good person can still grow old in fear. That decades of effort can still be met with emptiness. 69 years of work, nothing left. That line sounds dramatic until you realize for many people, it is not drama. It is a summary. A summary of sacrifice without security, of discipline without reward, of survival without peace. And if that doesn't shake something in us, it should. Because no one who spent a lifetime working should arrive at old age feeling disposable. No one should have to wonder whether one bad month will erase what little dignity they still have. No one should spend their final chapters apologizing for needing what they already spent a lifetime earning. In the last part, we're going to talk about the deeper lesson behind this story and why this quiet crisis says something painful, not just about one man's life, but about the kind of society we've built. The hardest truth in this story is not that one man is struggling at 69. The hardest truth is that he did what he was supposed to do. He worked. He stayed responsible. He kept showing up. He carried his share. And still, here he is older, tired, and staring at the possibility that the very years meant for rest may become the years of greatest fear. That should disturb all of us. Because when a society can take decades of labor from someone and still leave them anxious about food, housing, medicine, and survival, that is not just personal tragedy. That is moral failure on a larger scale. We like stories with clear villains. We like to believe there was one bad choice, one reckless decision, one obvious mistake that explains how a person ends up in pain. But life is rarely that simple. More often, people arrive here through slow erosion, through years of doing their best in conditions that never gave them enough room to build real safety. And that's what makes this story so powerful. It forces us to ask a question many people spend their whole lives avoiding. What does hard work actually guarantee anymore? For some people, work still creates comfort. For others, it only delays disaster. And for far too many older Americans, it becomes a long road that leads not to peace, but to exhaustion. That is why 69 years of work, nothing left hits so hard. Because it is not only about money. It is about expectation. It is about betrayal. It is about the quiet heartbreak of realizing that the contract you believed in was never truly written for you. He gave his labor in good faith. He gave his time, his strength, his youth. He trusted that the future would hold some reward for discipline, some shelter for sacrifice, some dignity for endurance. Instead, he got older. The bills got bigger. The safety net got thinner. And the dream got pushed further away. By now, he no longer needs motivation. He no longer needs slogans about grinding harder or thinking more positively. What he needed was a fairer chance, a system that did not demand everything while returning so little. A life where growing old did not feel like slowly falling through the cracks. And maybe that is the real lesson here. Not that people should be afraid, but that people should wake up. Wake up to how fragile security really is. Wake up to how many older people are suffering in silence. Wake up to the reality that millions are one crisis away from the same ending. Because once you understand that, this story stops being about him and starts becoming about all of us. About our parents, our neighbors, our future selves. None of us stay young forever. None of us outrun time. And one day, all the things we are postponing, all the peace we think we will enjoy later, will be tested by reality. That is why this subject matters so much. It is not just sad content. It is not just another emotional headline. It is a mirror. A mirror showing what happens when a lifetime of usefulness is not matched by a lifetime of protection. A mirror showing how easily dignity can become unaffordable. A mirror asking whether a person should really have to earn rest over and over again, right up until the end. And still, even in all this pain, there is something deeply human about him. He keeps going. That does not mean he is not broken in places. It does not mean he is not scared. It does not mean he has accepted what happened. It means that somewhere beneath the disappointment, beneath the fatigue, beneath the years of being let down, there is still a stubborn part of him that refuses to disappear. That part deserves respect. Not because struggle is beautiful. Not because hardship makes people noble. But because surviving this kind of life takes a strength that most people will never fully understand. So, when you hear a story like this, don't reduce it to pity. See it clearly. See the decades behind the wrinkles. See the labor behind the silence. See the fear behind the phrase, "I'm fine." See the reality behind the headline. 69 years of work. Nothing left. For some, it sounds unbelievable. For others, it sounds familiar. And that is exactly why we need to talk about it. Because no person should spend a lifetime building a world they cannot afford to grow old in. No one should work themselves into old age only to discover that survival never ended. No one should reach 69 and feel like the reward for endurance is simply more endurance. If this story moved you, let it stay with you. Let it challenge the easy assumptions. Let it remind you that many older people are carrying burdens they never speak about. And let it force one final question to the surface, the question at the heart of everything we talked about today. If a man can work nearly his entire life and still have nothing left, what does that say about the world that took everything from him?

- The STRESS of Being RICH | Why Do Millionaires Still Have Anxiety?: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Private jets, luxury cars, the world's largest mansions—all of these things make one think life would be perfect if one had so much money. But that's not true. Millionaires have money, but they do n't get rid of anxiety and stress. Why? When money solves everything, why do rich people still worry? What is the biggest dream? Financial freedom. No money worries, just luxury and comfort. But the truth is, as wealth increases, so do financial responsibilities and expectations. Another big factor is the fear of losing it all. When money is low, you feel you need more, but when you have a lot of money, you fear losing it all. Market fluctuations, bad investments, legal troubles—all of these are part of a millionaire's life. When the stakes are high, the risks increase even more. And then comes social pressure. When you're rich, people 's expectations of you are also high. You always have to maintain a certain image. Family, friends, and society all expect you to live the best lifestyle, donate the most, and flaunt your wealth. All of these things put pressure on a millionaire. Even after becoming rich, people's eyes are on you. You have to maintain your status and fulfill people's expectations. This social expectation becomes a major stressor. Another thing rich people face is isolation. With money comes a sense of loneliness because you never know who your true friend is and who is just there for your money. Having more money also increases trust issues. Are your friends and family really there for you or just for your bank balance? Last but not least, a big reason is the chase. Once you become rich, you always want more. A race begins for more money, success, and power. But this chase never ends. Once one goal is achieved, you want the next, bigger goal. This constant race. It increases stress, so does money guarantee you happiness? Probably not. The more money a millionaire has, the more unique and bigger their stressors become. At the end of the day, whether you earn an average income or are a millionaire, stress is a part of everyone's life. Real wealth is how you maintain your inner peace and balance. [Prashaas]

- Psychology of People Who Fear Big Money | Inside the Human Mind: Kind: captions Language: en Big money doesn't scare everyone until it shows up in your world. The second you picture a bigger paycheck or a real investment, your stomach tightens for no clear reason. You might call it nothing, but it changes what you do. So, you delay the offer, you underpric the project, or you keep your dreams safe by staying small. Here's what usually happens. Someone offers you a serious deal, and instead of feeling relief, you start hunting for problems. timelines that are too short, terms that are probably risky, partners who might not understand, and then the cause and effect becomes obvious. You say no, or you negotiate yourself down, and a week later, you tell yourself it was the smart move. But your future looks the same as before. You end up building a life that matches your fear, not your capacity. That's the real problem with the fear of big money. It doesn't just protect you from losing. It also protects you from growing. Right at the moment when you most need to choose one, the moment big money feels dangerous. Banks don't usually scare people because of money itself. They get worried when it starts behaving like it can't be trusted. Big money starts to feel dangerous the moment someone realizes the deal is moving faster than the paperwork and the promises don't match what's actually written down. A concrete example is a landlord offering a quick payoff to fix a problem that's already documented as unsafe. The check shows up before inspection, but the condition still hasn't been corrected. And then the landlord asks the tenant to sign a release right away. That combination, money arriving first, accountability arriving later, turns excitement into suspicion fast. People feel it in their body because they can't locate the risk in any normal place like a misstep, a missing signature or a clear chain of responsibility. Another moment comes when large sums start showing up through intermediaries instead of directly. Someone closes a deal through a consultant who insists that payments must go to a separate account. Sometimes even in a different name than the contracting company. That's when the cause and effect becomes obvious. If the money is real, the payment path should be straightforward. If the path is confusing, it makes the money feel like a cover. Even if the number on the screen looks legitimate, the friction around who receives it and who controls it makes the whole transaction feel unstable. This tension also shows up in everyday consumer finance, not just high stakes investing. A person gets contacted about a guaranteed return tied to a wealthy sponsor, and suddenly the language shifts toward urgency and secrecy. They're told not to verify details with their own bank, or not to discuss terms with anyone inside the space. The moment they try to slow down and request plain documentation, who's underwriting it, what the contract says, what happens if things go wrong, the sponsor stops answering, or offers a different story. When communication breaks, exactly when verification becomes necessary, fear has a reason to form. It gets even sharper when big money changes the incentives around you. At a workplace, a vendor might offer a large contract, but the wind depends on cutting compliance steps that protect the company and the public. When employees notice that the vendor staff avoids written records and prefers verbal approvals, the danger doesn't feel theoretical anymore. The money is buying access, and access without records is where liability usually lands later. That's the moment big money feels dangerous. Not because it's big, but because it shows up with pressure, opacity, and a pattern of avoiding accountability. Two, how fear forms past signals and present assumptions. Big money often doesn't arrive like a headline. It shows up as a pattern people have already learned to distrust. When someone repeatedly sees wealthy backers pull strings, their brain starts linking bigger checks with fewer choices. That link can form without anyone making an argument just from lived examples that feel connected. A new opportunity then gets judged through the same lens, even if the details are totally different. A past signal can be as ordinary as a job offer that comes with invisible pressure. Someone gets hired because a client is funding the project and then decisions start shifting in ways that never get written down. Meetings that used to be collaborative become shorter. Feedback becomes narrower and certain topics simply don't get discussed. After that experience, the mind predicts the next funding stream will behave the same way because the outcome felt real and the warning felt immediate. Those predictions usually harden into present assumptions the moment big money appears on the scene. If a fundraiser becomes fast, the assumption becomes that it's not about support, it's about control. If an investor asks more questions than usual, the assumption becomes that they're searching for leverage rather than clarity. Even the language can trigger it. Phrases like alignment, synergy, or strategy can start sounding like signals that other voices will be filtered out. Sometimes the fear is driven by how people remember incentives, not just the events themselves. A person recalls that when a wealthy sponsor joined, budgets got redirected and some teams were quietly deprioritized. They don't always remember the specific contract terms, but they remember the effect on daily work, fewer resources for maintenance, more resources for visible deliverables, and a calendar that suddenly has to serve someone else's timeline. That effect becomes a template. So the next time a large donation is announced, the mind tries to place everyone into those same roles. There's also a subtle cause and effect loop in the way fear changes behavior, which then seems to confirm fear. When someone anticipates control, they withhold ideas, speak more cautiously, or avoid asking for details. That makes collaboration slower and reduces transparency. So observers can interpret the hesitation as evidence that things are already off limits. Then the same person thinks. See, big money makes people secretive even though their own caution helped create the secrecy. Three, what fear looks like in real choices and behaviors. Big money fear shows up fast in everyday decisions, especially when people expect a large sum to change everything. The moment money feels big, choices get smaller and more careful. You can see it in how someone suddenly delays a clear next step, even when the numbers are already known. A common pattern is overchecking and avoidance right after a promising opportunity appears. Someone might receive an offer letter or a payout estimate, then spend weeks asking the same questions instead of signing, transferring oruling. The cause and effect is simple. They imagine one mistake will be irreversible. So they protect themselves by creating delay. In practice, that delay costs them leverage because the best terms often depend on quick action. Another real behavior is moral bargaining, where people try to prove they're not after money while still fearing it deeply. They'll say they want impact, but they choose roles based on how little financial exposure they can manage. Someone could turn down a higher paying position because the salary makes them feel like they'll become a different person. Then they pick a lower risk path that keeps them feeling safe, even if it means staying stuck where the work is less aligned. You'll also notice defensive spending decisions that look responsible but are actually anxiety in disguise. If a person is afraid of big money, they might refuse upgrades that would remove friction, like a reliable car repair, better child care options, or stable housing support. The effect is that life stays harder on purpose because difficulty feels controllable and small. For example, instead of setting a realistic budget that includes one clear improvement, they keep their finances tightly restricted and wait for permission that never comes. Relationships can change too because big money fear often brings suspicion and secrecy. One partner may avoid discussing income details, not because the details are private, but because talking about them feels dangerous. That silence creates uncertainty, and uncertainty turns into arguments about fairness, effort, and trust. You can end up with a couple where one person fears financial imbalance so much that they hide plans while the other person senses the gap and grows resentful. Risk behavior also flips in unexpected directions. Some people swing toward extreme conservatism, putting off investment decisions and keeping funds in the least dynamic place because big money feels fragile. Others do the opposite, chasing quick returns as a way to overpower fear, then pulling out when the outcome feels uncertain. Either way, the behavior tracks the same feeling. The size of the money makes them react to the idea of loss more intensely than to the plan itself. Even paperwork can become a battlefield. When fear is present, people get stuck on contract details, not necessarily because they don't understand them, but because signing feels like surrendering control. They might negotiate indefinitely, demand more documentation than the situation calls for, or involve extra gatekeepers until the deal becomes unworkable. The consequence is predictable. The opportunity cools off. Counterparties move on. And the fear gets confirmed through outcome instead of addressed through clarity. Four, the logic of risk. Why the fear doesn't match the outcome. Big money often triggers a risk story in people's heads, even when the real risk is smaller than they think. That reaction makes sense on the surface because large sums look hard to control, and uncertainty feels bigger when the numbers get bigger. Risk logic usually goes wrong at the point where people substitute scale for likelihood. If someone hears big investor money, they may assume the downside is automatically larger, but the actual downside depends on deal terms, enforcable protections, and how cash flows are structured. A person who fears losing control might picture a dramatic takeover scenario. Yet, a simple protective clause like limits on voting or required consent for major actions can reduce that specific risk a lot. Consider how many everyday decisions are managed with big ticket money already without panic. A household may put a large deposit on a home and still sign a contract with contingencies, escrow, inspection terms, and clear remedies if something goes wrong. The fear doesn't disappear because the money is smaller. It drops because the pathway for loss is defined and the process for recovery is spelled out. When the same clarity exists in a business or personal investment deal, the big money equals catastrophe assumption stops matching reality. In practice, fear tends to ignore the strongest factor in risk where the losses are bounded. If an investment is structured so the investor's loss is capped to the amount invested, the odds of a catastrophic outcome are limited to that defined exposure. Meanwhile, the fear often treats every outcome as if it could spiral without limit, like a vague threat hanging over everything. That's why two offers with the same headline size can feel worlds apart. One might include liquidation preferences or repayment schedules, and the other might be mostly promises with few protections. Another mismatch is that fear focuses on worst case imagination, while outcomes usually reflect the most likely path. When money is deployed in trenches tied to milestones, the next step doesn't happen until conditions are met. So, there's a natural break on bad momentum. People who dread big money pressure may expect constant renegotiation or sudden dominance. Yet, milestone-based releases can mean fewer surprises and fewer chances for conflict. Even something as simple as separating operational spending from equity issuance can change the emotional texture because the control points become concrete. You also see this in how people interpret access to resources. Money can reduce risk by extending runway, which gives time to fix problems and collect data, not just by fueling growth. A team that feared layoffs might get breathing room through a funding round that pays specific expenses first and that changes the perceived outcome from will be crushed to we can troubleshoot. When risk decreases because options widen, the fear often lags behind the actual mechanics and that gap is where the logic breaks. Five, a practical way to move through the fear without pretending. Big money anxiety often shows up as a bodily pause. Your mind goes blank, your shoulders tighten, and suddenly you can't hear what you're being offered. That's a real signal, not a flaw in your character. So, the first move is to notice the exact moment it hits. When you feel that tightness, you can name it privately. Fear is here. And the point isn't to cheer yourself up. It's to stop treating the feeling like an emergency. A practical way to move through it without pretending is to shrink the situation until your next action is small enough to be doable. If you're worried about an investing conversation, for example, you don't have to force a confident personality. You just choose one concrete thing. Ask what the fees are or ask how the risk is described or ask for a plain language summary of the plan. People often avoid those questions because they feel beneath their fear. But those questions create traction and traction is what quiets the panic. Another cause and effect that works in real life is this. Fear grows when you try to suppress it and it softens when you give it a job. Instead of pretending you don't care about the money, you can use your concern as a checklist. If you're selling a service and a high offer makes you nervous, you can prepare a short verification routine, confirm scope in writing, confirm payment schedule, confirm what counts as done, and confirm timelines before the conversation even starts. Then during the call, you read your own checklist in your head. Not because you're cold, but because you're protecting clarity. It also helps to separate first feel afraid from first. must act like first I'm not afraid. When someone offers you something big and your stomach drops, you can respond with grounded realism rather than performance. For instance, you might say, "First, I'm interested, but first want to review the terms carefully." Or, "Can you walk me through the downside scenarios in plain language?" Those responses don't pretend the offer doesn't matter. Yet, they keep yo. You can feel it when the numbers get big and your stomach tightens. That fear shows up when a job offer feels too good, when a rent increase looks scary, or when an investment account goes from small to real. You start protecting yourself instead of making a move. So, you delay, you save, and you choose safe options that quietly cost you more over time. Then, something changes, sometimes from a simple conversation. A friend shows how they budgeted after their first real raise, or a partner breaks down the plan with real dates and real amounts. That's when the fear loses power because you can see the cause and effect. Clear expectations reduce anxiety and steady steps reduce the sense that everything could fall apart at once. The key points land like this. Big money isn't dangerous by itself, but fear turns it into a threat in your head. And that mental story can control your decisions. If any of this sounds familiar, you're not behind. You're just human. Take one moment to notice where the fear shows up next time money gets big for you. From there, choose a small action you can actually repeat, like reviewing one bill, setting one automatic transfer, or asking one question before you say yes or no. And when you do, you'll probably feel that shift, less panic, more clarity, and more room to breathe. In the next video, we'll look at what to do when your first plan fails and you still want to move forward. We'll talk through real examples, what people changed, what they stopped doing, and how they built confidence step by

  20. @saofelixdeminas: Life is not meant to depend on the flow of money !

- Why $15,000 Is Enough to Escape Financial Anxiety Forever: Kind: captions Language: en $15,000 has the power to disappear me from financial anxiety for good. Not literally vanish. I don't mean forging a new identity or fleeing the country. I mean something far quieter and far more powerful. I mean becoming invisible to the pressure that traps most people in lives they never chose. Most people will never accept this idea. Their thinking runs in the opposite direction. They believe freedom requires millions. They believe it depends on finding the perfect investment, landing a six-figure job, or uncovering a secret that wealthy people guard from everyone else. I believe that once, too. None of it is true. Real financial independence doesn't come from brilliance, luck, or insider knowledge. It comes from avoiding a handful of obvious mistakes. Mistakes so visible that the only people who miss them are the ones actively making them. When I say disappear, I'm talking about fading from the paycheck to paycheck dread that chains most people to jobs they tolerate at best and resent at worst. The anxiety that makes you open your banking app with a knot in your stomach. The mental math at the grocery store. The quiet fear that one bad month could undo everything. People who escape this life differently. They don't check balances compulsively. They don't panic when bills arrive. Their decisions come from preference, not necessity. And here's the part that surprised me most. Their advantage isn't intelligence. It isn't opportunity. It isn't even income. It's understanding a few principles that most people consciously ignore. One of the most successful investors in history summarized it in a single sentence. Spend less than you earn. Save consistently. Put the money somewhere sensible. Wait. That's it. No mention of stock picking. No obsession with salary. No genius required. Spend less than you make. Put it away. Give it time. The reason this advice feels unsatisfying is because it is simple. And the reason people reject it is because they want it to be complicated. We crave shortcuts. We want a hack that lets us skip the waiting. There isn't one. The gap between financial stress and financial freedom isn't knowledge. It's behavior. So instead of asking how to succeed, I asked a different question. What would guarantee that I fail? This approach is called inversion. Instead of asking what to do, you ask what to avoid. Because once you know what guarantees failure, simply refusing to do those things dramatically increases your odds of success. There's an old saying I love. All I want to know is where I'm going to die, so I'll never go there. So that's where I'm going now. I'm walking through the graveyard of financial independence. And I'm reading the tombstones. Tombstone one, consumer debt. Carrying credit card balances at 18, 20, or 24% interest is like trying to run a race with your shoes tied together. The math is brutal. At 20% interest, debt doubles roughly every three and a half years. That means $3,000 becomes $6,000, then $12,000, then suddenly you're buried. You cannot out earn this. You cannot outinvest it. You cannot outsmart it. The only solution is to never take it on or to eliminate it with the urgency you'd use to put out a house fire. Tombstone two lifestyle inflation. This one is quieter. A raise arrives. 3 months later, your expenses have risen to match it. A better apartment, a nicer car, slightly pricier restaurants, a few extra subscriptions you barely notice. By the end of the year, you're earning more than ever and saving nothing. I once heard an observation that stuck with me. Extremely large houses don't make people happier. They often make them less happy. A modest home serves you. A lavish one serves your ego and drains your future. One investor I admire lived in the same simple house for decades. No upgrades, no excess. When asked why, he said he didn't want to live a life that made him look royal. Character mattered more. Tombstone 3: The Three Destroyers. There's an old saying about financial ruin. Three things reliably destroy people who would otherwise succeed. The phrase used to be ladies, liquor, and leverage. Today, I'd translate it this way. Costly romantic entanglements that cloud judgment. Substance abuse that erodess decision-making. Excessive debt that magnifies every mistake. Any one of these can erase $15,000. Two can trap you for decades. All three together. and recovery may never come. Tombstone four, get rich quick thinking. There's an uncomfortable arithmetic lesson here. If someone offers me an investment that returns 30% per year and I give them $5,000. The math looks incredible. In 10 years, that becomes nearly $70,000. In 20 years, over a million. In 30 years, tens of millions. If this were real, if it were reliable and accessible, everyone would already be doing it. The math would be common knowledge by now. The returns promised by get-richqu schemes are carefully engineered, high enough to overwhelm skepticism, low enough to avoid sounding absurd. They are designed by people who understand my psychology better than I do, which leads to something deeply uncomfortable to admit. I am not a rational being who occasionally makes emotional decisions. I am an emotional being who occasionally makes rational decisions. My brain evolved to survive predators, not to manage long-term investments. The instincts that once kept my ancestors alive are now quietly dismantling financial futures. When researchers studied why people make terrible decisions, patterns emerged. Not one or two, dozens, but one factor dominated more than any other. Denial. Denial explains why people avoid checking their balances, why they refuse to calculate monthly spending, why unused subscriptions quietly drain accounts year after year. The truth hurts. So the brain pretends it doesn't exist. The cure is simple and brutal. Look at the number. Whatever number I've been avoiding, debt, savings rate, spending, I have to write it down. Reality can't be changed until it's faced. Another bias is even more dangerous. I automatically think and act the way people around me do. That's how bubbles form. People buy houses they can't afford because everyone else is doing it. They lease cars that cost more than their monthly savings because it feels normal. But here's the inversion question. If most people are financially stressed, what happens when I copy their behavior? The answer is obvious and rarely accepted. One investor put it perfectly. It's not greed that drives the world. It's envy. Another expanded on that idea by saying envy is the only sin that offers no pleasure at all, only pain. It makes no logical sense to want what someone else has simply because they have it. I learned to look at my last few major purchases and ask one question. Did I need this or did I want to keep up? The honest answer to that question is often the most valuable financial analysis I can perform. Loss aversion is another silent killer. Losing something hurts roughly twice as much as gaining the same thing feels good. That's why people cling to bad investments. Selling would mean admitting loss, so they hold on and pretend it isn't real. It's why people stay in miserable jobs. Leaving feels like losing even when staying is slowly destroying them. Then there's overconfidence. Most people believe they are above average with money, just like most drivers believe they're above average drivers. That's mathematically impossible, but psychologically universal. Believing I'm the exception is one of the most expensive beliefs I can hold. The market doesn't care how smart I feel. My brain also hates uncertainty. It craves closure so intensely that it will accept a bad answer over no answer. That's why people make impulsive financial decisions. Doing something feels better than doing nothing, even when nothing would be wiser. Learning to say, "I don't know yet," is a financial skill. The most dangerous situation is when these biases combine. They don't add. They multiply. Social proof, overconfidence, denial, envy, fear of uncertainty. Each one alone might cause a small error. Together, they cause disasters that feel completely rational to the person making them. This is how intelligent, well-intentioned people lose everything. Not through one massive mistake, but through many small ones compounding. And that brings me back to $15,000. Because $15,000 can change a life. Not through luck, not through speculation, but through simple mathematics that almost no one consistently applies. Here's the core insight I had to internalize. Saving $10 is not saving $10. It's saving the future value of $10. At a 7% real return, $10 becomes roughly $20 in 10 years, $40 in 20, and $80 in 30. That sounds trivial until scale enters the picture. Saving $500 a month at that rate becomes over half a million dollar in 30 years. $1,000 a month becomes well over a million. The $15,000 matters because it's not just $15,000. It's a head start. Years of work already done. The hardest part, the beginning, is already behind me. There's a sentence that sounds harsh, but ends up being strangely liberating. The first meaningful amount of money is brutally hard to build, and you must build it. For some people, that number is $100,000. For me, the real psychological shift happened much earlier. It happened at $15,000 because before that point, progress feels invisible. Every dollar saved seems to disappear into nothing. You're pushing a boulder that doesn't move. After that point, something changes. Not overnight, not dramatically, but measurably. Money begins to respond. Starting from zero and saving consistently is mathematically exhausting. At a modest return, it can take more than a decade to reach six figures. But the second phase moves faster and the third faster. Still, money starts earning money. Then that money earns more money. The snowball doesn't look impressive at first. That's why most people quit before it matters. Having $15,000 puts me years ahead of someone starting from nothing. More importantly, it moves me past the point where most people give up because they can't see progress yet. One of the most honest confessions I've ever read about building wealth went like this. I wasn't brave. I wasn't bold. I wasn't extraordinary. I was a cautious little squirrel, saving more nuts than I needed and rarely touching the pile. That line rewired how I thought about money. Everything I'd been told suggested wealth came from bold moves, clever risks, and fearless bets. But the truth was quieter. Be the squirrel. Save more than I think I need. Don't dig too deeply into what I've saved. Don't confuse activity with progress. The same person later reflected on his early career. For over a decade, his total income averaged about $20,000 per year. From that he bought a home, owned two cars, and accumulated a substantial investment portfolio. Most people would have spent that income without noticing. He didn't. Saving felt as natural to him as breathing. His advantage wasn't income. It was restraint. This isn't glamorous. No one makes viral videos about slowly accumulating money over years. There's nothing exciting about consistency. And yet, this is how freedom actually happens for people who weren't born rich. didn't win the lottery, which leads to a deeply uncomfortable truth about expectations. The first rule of a happy life is low expectations. If I expect to get rich quickly, I'll make desperate choices. If I expect the process to be slow and boring, I'll actually follow it. Financial freedom works the same way. Expect slowness, expect boredom, expect years where nothing seems to happen. That expectation is not pessimism. It's alignment with reality. There's a paradox I had to accept. The smarter I think I am, the more likely I am to overestimate my ability to outsmart the system. And that confidence pushes people into risks that erase their advantage. One of the wisest observations I've ever heard goes like this. It's remarkable how much long-term advantage people gain by trying to be consistently not stupid instead of trying to be very intelligent. Strong swimmers drown because they venture into waters that kill anyone, skill or not. Average swimmers survive because they stay where survival is possible. Finance works the same way. People who believe they're clever take speculative risks that destroy them. People who respect their limits stick to simple methods that quietly work. With $15,000, my goal isn't to be smart. My goal is to avoid being idiotic. That means not picking individual stocks I don't understand, not using leverage, not trying to time the market, not paying high fees, not reacting emotionally to short-term noise. It means putting money into diversified lowcost investments and leaving them alone for years. This is so boring most people can't tolerate it. And their inability to tolerate boredom is exactly why they never build wealth. Something I learned far later than I should have is this. Everyone giving financial advice is being paid by someone and that someone is usually not me. This single realization changed how I listen. Financial adviserss who earn commissions recommend products that pay commissions. Fund managers who charge a percentage of assets promote strategies that keep assets under management. Financial media survives on attention, so it amplifies fear and greed because those emotions drive clicks. None of these people are evil. They're behaving rationally in response to their incentives. The problem is that their incentives are rarely aligned with my outcomes. That's why the most important financial skill isn't analysis. It's skepticism. Before following advice, I ask one question, how is this person being paid? And then another, what would they recommend if they had no financial stake in my decision? That question alone filters out most bad advice. I once encountered a book that explained this better than any spreadsheet ever could. It wasn't about money. It was about influence. How decisions are shaped, nudged, engineered, often without us noticing. One investor valued that understanding so highly that he gave the author a gift worth more than most people earn in a year. Because understanding how I'm influenced saves far more money over a lifetime than any single investment ever could. Avoiding mistakes compounds just like returns do. This brings me to mental models, not complicated theories. Simple frameworks that prevent expensive errors. The first one is this. Every dollar I spend isn't just a dollar gone. It's the future value of that dollar gone forever. Buying something unnecessary for $200 isn't really spending $200. It's spending what that $200 would have become over the next 20 or 30 years depending on time. That's $400 or $800 or more. This doesn't mean never spending money. It means understanding the true trade. Another model comes from engineering. Bridges aren't built to barely hold the expected load. They're built to handle several times that load. That difference is called margin of safety. My finances need the same buffer. If I calculate that I need $25,000 to survive a job loss, I aim for $40,000. If a 4% withdrawal rate seems safe, I plan on 3%. This isn't pessimism. It's acknowledging that my estimates are wrong and life is unpredictable. Then there's the idea of the circle of competence. There are things I truly understand and things I don't. The boundary matters. If I don't understand cryptocurrency, I don't invest in it. If I don't understand options, I don't trade them. If I don't understand private equity, I stay away. The honest answer to do I really understand this is usually no. Staying inside that circle protects me from errors that no amount of intelligence can undo. Another model changed how I evaluate decisions. First order thinking asks, "What happens if I do this?" Second order thinking asks, "What happens after that happens?" A higherp paying job increases income. But what follows? More stress, more spending, less health, less time. Sometimes the second order consequences erase the first order gain. Most financial mistakes come from stopping too early in the chain of consequences. Wisdom comes from following the chain all the way to the end. Here's the part most people don't want to hear. The timeline for turning $15,000 into real financial independence is measured in decades, not months. That's where most people fail. Not because they choose the wrong path, but because they can't stay on the right one long enough. It's easy to become well off slowly. It's almost impossible to get rich quickly. The difference isn't intelligence, it's patience. There's a truth about money that sounds simple, but changes everything once it sinks in. Time is not just a factor in building wealth. Time is the advantage. At ordinary market returns, someone who invests consistently for 25 years ends up with dramatically more than someone who invests the same amount for 15, not a little more, often twice as much or more. Starting earlier with less beats starting later with more. Persisting when progress feels invisible beats perfect strategy. Patience isn't just a virtue in finance. It's the skill that separates the free from the trapped. I once read about someone who reached financial independence in his late 30s. No windfall, no genius investment. Just 13 years of steady saving and an understanding of compounding. The arithmetic was boring. The outcome wasn't. But money alone isn't the full story. Character has a direct financial cost. There was a commencement speech about how to guarantee a miserable life. The first instruction was simple. Be unreliable. If I don't do what I say, I'll do. Nothing else matters. Unreliability compounds negatively. Missed payments lead to fees and higher interest. Broken commitments erase income and references. Lost trust closes doors permanently. Reliability, on the other hand, compounds quietly. Each kept promise builds reputation. Each broken one erodess it. Over decades, the difference becomes enormous. The same speech listed four behaviors that guarantee misery. Be unreliable. Learn only from my own mistakes. quit after setbacks. Refuse to question my own beliefs. Three of those aren't skills. They're character traits. And character shapes financial outcomes more than intelligence ever will. There's another pattern I couldn't ignore. Every truly wise person I've encountered shared one habit. They read constantly, not to find stock tips, not to chase opportunities. They read to understand how the world actually works. Reading exposes me to mistakes I haven't made yet so I can avoid them. It builds mental models. It reveals patterns before they turn into problems. One investor joked that his kids thought he was a book with legs. That image stuck with me. So what do I actually do with $15,000? I don't try to be clever. I try to be durable. I avoid debt. I avoid leverage. I avoid complexity I don't understand. I put the money into diversified lowcost investments. I automate contributions. I don't check balances obsessively. I let boredom do the heavy lifting. I build a margin of safety. I keep my lifestyle simple. I protect my time. And most importantly, I stay consistent when nothing feels like it's happening. Because here's the quiet truth. It's not dramatic decisions that change a financial life. It's the refusal to make catastrophic ones. $15,000 isn't wealth. It's distance. distance from panic, distance from desperation, distance from needing to make bad choices under pressure. And that distance compounds slowly, quietly, relentlessly. That's how I disappear from financial worry. Not by being brilliant, by being patient.

Why doesn't the anxiety beast get out of our way? We look for distractions or a way out of the quandry. Is there a drink I can have or a recreational drug I can take to make it all go away, at least for the moment? Can I busy myself with work or by obsessively reading and posting on social media? These strategies tend to just make anxiety worse. However, if we can harness the power of anxiety, we can thrive in ways we never thought were possible. We should not sidestep anxiety or back up and deny that it is there, but rather stop and evaluate the situation. What is our anxiety trying to tell us? The answer is that we need to dig deep down into ourselves, although doing so makes us uncomfortable. We need to experience anxiety in order to transcend it. But looking at it from a scientific standpoint, you know, I think a lot of people associate anxiety, with something that's kind of necessary, needed for survival, you know, especially right now. And that's something that I dove into a lot in my book, because I've been really interested with this idea, you know, there's this whole idea of performance anxiety, and I actually haven't found any evidence to support it. So, let's bookmark that as well, and talk about some of these origins here. So, think of fear as a survival mechanism, okay? We've talked before about habits, and setting up habits for survival, right? To remember where food is, and to remember where danger is. So, these learning mechanisms go way back, you know, evolutionarily conserved all the way to the sea slug. Like this is the oldest learning mechanism known in science, you know, positive and negative reinforcement. So, if you zoom in on fear as a negatively reinforced behavior, fear helps us survive, right? So, think of our ancient ancestors out on the savanna, they are forging for food, right? But they don't know if it's dangerous. So, they're moving from their safe zone, their cave, out into more of an uncertain space, the savanna. So, their brain naturally goes on high alert, to start to learn things, like, "Oh, there's food, go there again. "There's danger, don't go there again," okay? So, fear helps us learn where things are safe and where things aren't safe, so we can avoid the unsafe places. That fear mechanism is this old part of the brain, and then layered on top of it, is a thinking and planning part of the brain, the neocortex, or the prefrontal cortex in particular. And this is interesting, because it helps us survive in a different way. It helps us survive through thinking and planning, yet it needs information, and preferably accurate information, so in this day and age there's a lot of misinformation which gets in the way, but it also is helpful for it to have precedent. So, when it's going into unchartered territory, it's really hard to think and plan, you know? Like, "Oh, let's, let's go explore Saturn." Well, you know, we've never done that before, so you gotta think of a bunch of things, and try to approximate, but it'd be much easier if somebody else had explored Saturn and wrote a book about it, said, "Oh do this, don't do that," okay? So, the prefrontal cortex, think of fear, helps us survive. The prefrontal cortex helps us survive, but if you pair fear with a lack of certainty, right, which is what the prefrontal cortex is trying to help us do, it's trying to help us predict the future, based on past experiences, if there is no precedent, if there's a lack of certainty, that fear, plus uncertainty, leads to anxiety. And people think, "Oh, anxiety it's gonna help me survive." No, it doesn't. (laughs) There is no evidence for it helping us survive. It makes our thinking and planning brain go offline. And if you think of the extreme form of anxiety, panic, right, which is wildly unthinking behavior, that's that far into the spectrum of anxiety. Right, it's an interesting, but subtle and important distinction, in that it is the uncertainty, that's driving the irrationality, right? You can be afraid, when you have a certain set of predictable parameters to deal with, but when you don't know what you're venturing into, that's what provokes the anxiety. So, to kind of extend that, what's fascinating about that, is it's not the dire consequence that creates the anxiety, it's the lack of certainty, around whether that consequence is going to be dire, or not so dire Yeah, absolutely. And just to be super clear for your listeners, it's not that fear is a problem, right? Fear helps us learn, in new situations in particular. But fear doesn't have the same neurochemical reaction in the brain as anxiety. Like, neurochemically, like how do these two things distinguish themselves? Yeah, I don't think all of the neurochemistry has been worked out, but I think one way to think about this, is on a temporal scale. So, if you look at the timescales, you can actually differentiate them pretty well. I'll use an example, of let's say stepping out into the street. So, let's say in this day and age, when everybody is distracted by their, what does Cornell Welch say, "Our weapons of mass distraction," you know, everybody's looking at their phone. So, if somebody steps out in the street, and doesn't see the bus coming, you know, bearing down on them, they instinctively jump back onto the safety of the sidewalk. And I say instinctively, because this is much faster than our thinking brain. You know, imagine you look up to the bus, and go, "Hmm, is that really going fast enough, you know? "Is it going to veer," splat. You know? We don't have time for that. So, we jump back onto the safety of the sidewalk, and then we have a fear response. (air whooshing) All of our, you know, basically our fight or flight response kicks in, and says, "Wow, that was crazy." And I, to be more precise, that fight or flight response, is where we get these catecholamines, we get all these, basically the adrenaline surge, that says, "Hey, you gotta run if you need to, "if you're not safe at this point, okay?" But that also helps us have this fear response, that says, "Wow, you could have almost gotten killed. "You should learn from this, look both ways," you know, "Relearn what you learned as a kid." The problem is, so think of that as super rapid, is that instinctual response, the rapid response is that fear response, but then ideally that drains out of our system, you know, and we move on when we've learned. So, this is what differentiates that from anxiety, is with the anxiety, maybe we keep replaying it in our head, "Oh, I could have gotten killed, I'm an idiot. "I shouldn't have done that." That is just kind of keeping that fear response going, chronically. Wow. And that could happen for hours, days, weeks, years, you know, and this is where people, you know, it's not like we need a lifetime of psychotherapy for a fear response. Right, right. But what we do need, is the ability to see the difference between a helpful fear response, and us literally getting spun out of control, because our mind's going out of control, making us continue to think about it. And I think we're all experiencing, you know, some variation on those two things, over the past year, whether it's fear or unhealthy anxiety, you know, amidst a global pandemic, and our weapons of mass distraction, that are feeding us conflicting information, about X, Y and Z. I would suspect that this has created an unbelievably robust Petri dish, for you to really immerse yourself in the subject matter, in which you are an expert. And I know that early in the pandemic, like last May, you were writing pretty extensively about anxiety, and how we were grappling with how to manage this crazy shift in all of our lifestyles. But here we are almost a year later. It would seem to follow that there's less uncertainty now, perhaps the same amount of fear, but have you seen any kind of differentiation in how your patients or or the population at large, is kind of coping with COVID? So, I would say there are two main things that I'm noticing both in my clinic and then just at large. One is that there is that big spike of, "Wow, this is crazy. "This is really gonna be a pandemic. "Is it, wow, this really is a pandemic," you know? And then "How dangerous is this?" You know, "How infectious is this?" All of that, that uncertainty has gone down a little bit. And then they crash and burn. Or the feds, who are trying to figure out how to prop up the economy, without throwing us into whatever wild inflation or whatever. That's not my lane, so I don't know. Whether it's drinking, you know, drinking has gone up, actually it's interesting, drinking has gone up in a significant part of the population, and some people have just basically cut out their drinking, probably due to lack of social resources, and the the usual places that they do.
