We start by understanding the root of the fear of spending. This often stems from past financial hardships or witnessing family members struggle. Tracking net worth may also contribute to the fear as spending can appear as a financial setback. Now let's discuss powerful strategies to overcome the fear of spending. One, set clear financial goals. Define your aspirations. Be it early retirement or home ownership. This gives meaning to saving and spending. Two, create a budget. Track your income and expenses. A budget provides control enabling informed spending decisions. Three, separate accounts. Use dedicated spending accounts for discretionary expenses which minimizes anxiety. Four, challenge your beliefs. Spending isn't negative. Evaluate purchases for their true impact on your well-being. Five, explore alternatives. Consider all options before large purchases. Sometimes used items can be more practical. Six, adjust your perspective. Think of spending as part of your balanced financial plan. While saving is essential, enjoying the present is equally important. Let's see what you've learned so far. Here is a question. What is a key strategy to manage the fear of spending? Option A, create strict saving plans. Option B, challenge your beliefs about spending. Option C, avoid spending entirely. Option D, focus only on increasing net worth. You may pause the video now to think about the answer. We will discuss the answer in the next section of the video. The correct answer is to challenge your beliefs about spending. This helps in fostering healthier spending habits without the guilt. Here are some vital insights to remember. Focus more on achieving your financial goals rather than just growing your net worth. Use budgeting as a powerful tool to manage your finances effectively. Remember, spending money should not cause unnecessary anxiety. To sum up, understand the underlying causes of your fear regarding spending. Adopt strategies to control your anxiety and focus on achieving a financial balance that brings freedom.   19. @BehavioralCapital-c1m: In this episode, Lewis Howes and Jay Shetty discuss the emotional and psychological side of wealth, emphasizing that financial freedom is impossible without healing your internal relationship with money.
Core Themes:
1. Awareness is Step One: You must analyze your "money story"—the beliefs and attachment styles you inherited from your parents—and observe how your body physically reacts (stress, shallow breathing) when you receive or spend money.
2. Beliefs Dictate Behavior: If you believe "money is bad" or "rich people are dishonest," you will subconsciously sabotage your own success to remain a "good person."
3. From Helplessness to Helpfulness: When you are broke or stuck, stop trying to "take" from others to survive. Instead, flip the script to a mindset of service; adding value to others is the fastest way to build the connections and opportunities that lead to wealth.
4. Generosity as a Currency: Wealth isn't just a bank balance; it’s the energy you bring to the world. Being generous with your time, wisdom, and passion—even when you have no money—creates a "rich" internal state that attracts abundance.
5. Stop Chasing "Easy Money": Avoid the trap of "get-rich-quick" schemes (crypto hacks, gambling). True financial success comes from "making money easy" through the slow, consistent development of skills over a decade or more.
6. Respect the "Guest": Treat money as if it were a person you love. If you gossip about money, avoid looking at your accounts, and abuse your finances, the "energy" of money will not stay in your life.
7. Invest in Yourself: If you have limited funds, do not gamble them on speculative assets. Invest them in courage-building activities (coaching, training) that develop skills, which are the most reliable assets you will ever own.
8. The Trap of High Net Worth: Having a high net worth does not equate to being "free." Many wealthy people remain trapped in scarcity and anxiety because they never did the internal work to heal their self-worth.
9. Give Where it’s Uncomfortable: True abundance is unlocked by giving—whether money, time, or kindness—especially when it feels slightly uncomfortable. This breaks the cycle of ego and hoarding.
10. True Wealth is Internal: A rich life is defined by deep, meaningful relationships and feeling free in every moment, regardless of the number in your bank account.

- The Psychology of Money What Daniel Kahneman Teaches Us About Wealth, Fear and Decision Making: Kind: captions Language: en How Daniel Conorman explains why people buy. The psychology behind every decision you make. Today we embark on a 15-minute journey into your own mind. Hi everyone, it's Michael here. Today I want to take you deeper than ever before. Not just into psychology or sales, but into human nature itself. Because once you understand Daniel Conorman, you don't just understand buying behavior. You understand the hidden mechanisms of power. Think about the last time you made a big purchase, a car, a course, an investment. At the time, it felt like a rational decision. You had reasons. You had explanations. But did you decide first and then justify? Or did you truly calculate everything logically? Conorman showed us the truth. We are not rational creatures who sometimes get emotional. We are emotional creatures who sometimes use logic. Classical economics assumed homoeconomicus, the rational human who always maximizes benefit. But in the real world, we are far from perfect. People hold losing stocks too long. We buy things we don't need. We fall for marketing tricks. Conoran discovered these weren't random mistakes. They were predictable, which means they could be studied and used. This is where sales, marketing, and power enter the picture. The two systems that control you. System one is fast, automatic, and intuitive. System two is slow, analytical, and deliberate. System one runs most of your life. It's effortless, and emotional. It's the pilot that never sleeps, making thousands of choices every hour. System two is lazy. It requires focus and burns glucose. Your brain is designed to conserve energy, so it defaults to shortcuts. Biases. Why spend $10,000 on a watch when a $200 one tells time just as well? Because it's not about time, it's about identity, status, belonging. System one decides this feels powerful. System two then steps in to justify it. It's an investment, but the emotion always comes first. Let's talk about anchoring. Conorman showed that irrelevant numbers can pull our decisions like a spin of a wheel before a serious estimate. In real estate, a 3 million house makes a 1.8 million one feel reasonable. The house didn't change. Your mental anchor did. Apple does this perfectly. Launch a $2,000 laptop and suddenly the $2,300 model feels like a bargain. Anchors redefine normal. Now, the most powerful force, loss aversion. Conorman discovered that losses hurt twice as much as equivalent gains feel good. This is why scarcity works. Avoiding loss is a stronger motivator than acquiring gain. It's what drives panic sales and insurance markets. Prospect theory explains that when we are losing, we become gamblers. We double down because accepting the loss feels unbearable. The availability heruristic makes us fear plane crashes more than car accidents because they are more visible. Visibility creates your reality. Framing is the art of influence. The same data framed differently produces different behavior. Language activates emotion instantly. Apple sells identity. Tesla sells innovation. Nike sells self-belief. They all speak directly to your system. One, emotion leads the way. Decision fatigue kills action. Clarity increases conversion. The best businesses make the decision easy for you. Simplicity is power. To protect yourself, pause. Sleep on it. Activate system two deliberately because rationality requires effort and awareness is your rarest asset. You are not broken. You are human. Self-awareness is the highest form of leverage. I'm Michael. Stay with me to master the psychology of wealth.

- This is the Richest Old Money Family in America...Even Presidents Fear Them.: Kind: captions Language: en Forget the names you see flashing across your news feed every day. Forget the tech titans with their ambitions of colonizing other planets, their sprawling social media empires, and their very loud, very public, and often very fleeting displays of wealth. Their fortunes, while undeniably immense, are new. They're volatile, rising and falling with the latest market trends or the whims of a mercurial CEO. But there's another kind of money in America. An older, quieter, and far more entrenched kind of power. This is wealth measured not in fiscal years, but in generations. This is old money. These are the dynasties, the families whose fortunes were forged in the steel, oil, and railroads of the 19th century or carefully pieced together through retail and manufacturing long before the internet was even a flicker of an idea. They don't chase fame. In fact, most of them actively run from it, treating privacy as their most valuable asset. They don't need to cultivate a personal brand because their power isn't built on personality. It's woven into the very fabric of American life. They own the food you eat for breakfast, the candy you buy at the checkout counter, the stores you shop in for groceries, and the gas that fuels your car. You interact with their vast empires every single day, often without a second thought. Their influence isn't shouted from the rooftops. It's wielded discreetly in private boardrooms, from the seclusion of sprawling family estates, and through political networks that operate deep in the shadows. So, who are these families? How did they manage to build empires so durable that they've weathered world wars, the Great Depression, technological revolutions, and the constant, crushing pressures of time? What are the closely guarded secrets to their incredible staying power? And what does their story truly reveal about the nature of wealth and power in America? Today, we're pulling back the curtain and counting down the top 10 richest old money families in America. This isn't just a list of names and numbers. It's a journey inside the fortress of real generational power. A look at how it was painstakingly built, how it's ferociously protected, and the immense controversies it has created along the way. Kicking off our list at number 10 is a family that has quietly yet profoundly shaped what Americans read, watch, and how they communicate for over 125 years. The Cox family, with a fortune estimated at a formidable 26.8 billion. Their story doesn't begin in a glittering boardroom on Wall Street, but in a humble newspaper office in 1898. A man named James M. Cox, armed with little more than ambition and a deep understanding of the public word, borrowed some money to purchase the Dayton Evening News. Cox wasn't just another businessman. He was a former school teacher and reporter who viscerally understood the power of the media to shape opinion and influence society. That foundational insight would become the family's enduring trademark. His ambition couldn't be contained by publishing alone. He went on to become a three-term governor of Ohio and even ran for president of the United States in 1920 with a young charismatic Franklin D. Roosevelt as his running mate. He lost the election, but that defeat didn't slow him down one bit. He simply channeled that immense political drive back into his growing media empire. What began with a single Ohio newspaper grew into the sprawling privately held giant known as Cox Enterprises. The family brilliantly navigated the turbulent technological revolutions of the 20th century, a landscape littered with the corpses of companies that failed to adapt. When radio emerged as the new frontier, they bought stations. When television captured the nation's attention, they launched TV stations and methodically built one of the country's largest cable companies, Cox Communications. They understood a key immutable rule of generational wealth. You must adapt or you will be finished. Today, the third and fourth generations of the family, led by chairman and CEO Alex Taylor, run a massive and remarkably private company. While they shrewdely sold off many of their legacy media assets in recent years, they pivoted with incredible foresight and timing into a completely different gold mine, the automotive industry. If you've ever used Autotrader to find a used car or checked a vehicle's value on Kelly Blue Book, you've interacted with the modern Cox family empire. Those digital powerhouses are now the very backbone of their wealth, pulling in over $22 billion a year in revenue. By deliberately staying private, the Cox family affords themselves the ultimate luxury, the ability to think in decades, not in frantic fiscal quarters. They are a masterclass in how an old media fortune can be completely and masterfully reinvented for a new era, securing their spot among America's most powerful and enduring dynasties. At number nine, we find a name so deeply ingrained in American life that it's almost certainly hiding under your kitchen sink or inside your cleaning closet right now. The SC Johnson family with an estimated net worth of around $30 billion proves that the path to unimaginable wealth can be paved not with gold or silicon but with everyday household products. This is not a story of Wall Street finance or Silicon Valley disruption. It begins in the heartland in 1886 in Rasine, Wisconsin. A salesman for a local flooring company, Samuel Curtis Johnson made a bold move. He bought the business outright. He wasn't content just selling flooring. He soon started developing his own specialized floor wax to sell directly to his customers. That single product, Johnson's prepared paste wax, was the humble seed from which a global empire would grow. The company's motto was simple and direct, a family company. For five generations, through immense social and economic change, they've held true to that promise. While countless other family businesses eventually sold out to corporations or went public, the Johnson's held on tight. This fierce commitment to privacy gave them the invaluable freedom to make long-term patient plans and to cultivate a unique company culture far from the relentless short-sighted demands of Wall Street investors. This autonomy allowed them to pour vast sums of money into research and development, creating a dynasty of iconic brands that are now household names. Windex, Glade, Ziplock, Raid, and Pledge. Remarkably, leadership has been passed down directly from father to son for four generations. And now the fifth generation, led by chairman and CEO HF Fisk Johnson, is in charge. This kind of smooth, stable succession is exceedingly rare in the corporate world and is a huge component of their lasting success. The family has also meticulously built a reputation as one of the best and most ethical places to work in America. But the family's legacy isn't just about profits. The SC Johnson family was decades ahead of the curve on the concept of corporate responsibility. They were offering paid vacations to their employees way back in the 1930s during the depths of the Great Depression. In the 1970s, they voluntarily and unilaterally banned chlorofluocarbons or CFC's from all of their aerosol cans years before the government mandated it because they knew the chemicals were damaging the ozone layer. Still headquartered in their founding city of Rasine after more than 130 years, they represent a different, quieter kind of dynasty. one built not on ruthless tactics and hostile takeovers, but on quality products and an enduring belief that a business is fundamentally more than just a balance sheet. The family at number eight built their massive $30 billion fortune on something you can't see or touch, but that our entire modern world absolutely depends on. The vast hidden network of pipelines crisscrossing the nation like a great steel circulatory system. This is the Duncan family, heirs to an energy empire that began with a shockingly simple idea and a dose of pure Texas grit. The founder Dan Duncan was the living embodiment of the classic rags to rich's story. Born into profound poverty in rural Texas, his early life was marked by hardship. But he possessed an incredible unyielding work ethic and a sharp eye for opportunity where others saw none. After serving in the army, he got his start in the oil and gas fields and then struck out on his own in 1968. With just $10,000 to his name, some of it borrowed. He founded a company called Enterprise Products Partners. His core idea was simple yet utterly brilliant. At a time when the energy industry was fragmented and inefficient, Duncan saw the future in creating a consolidated one-stop shop for natural gas. He began methodically buying up pipelines, patiently building a comprehensive network that could gather, process, and deliver energy all under one roof. Enterprise products became the undisputed king of the midstream. that crucial and incredibly lucrative link between the drillers who pull resources from the ground and the refiners who turn it into usable products. Today, the company is an absolute giant. It controls over 50,000 m of pipelines carrying natural gas, oil, and prochemicals all across the country. It's like owning a toll road business on a colossal national scale. Every single time energy flows through their system, the Duncan family fortune grows. But Dan Duncan's story has one final unbelievable twist that secures his family's place in history. He passed away in 2010 during a unique one-year window where the federal estate tax had been temporarily repealed by Congress. It was a bizarre legal anomaly. If he had died just a few months earlier or a few months later, his estate would have faced a massive tax bill, potentially as high as 45%. Instead, his four children inherited the multi-billion dollar empire completely, 100% tax-free, marking one of the largest and most astonishing transfers of generational wealth in all of US history. Today, those four children share the immense fortune with his daughter Randa Williams serving as chairman of the board, quietly continuing the incredibly rich legacy their father built from literally nothing. Our seventh wealthiest dynasty didn't get rich from oil, media, or finance, but from one of America's most iconic comfort foods, the humble chicken sandwich. With a net worth of $33.6 $6 billion of the Kathy family, the founders and owners of Chick-fil-A, have built one of the most successful, most beloved, and most controversial fast food empires in the nation. The story begins not in a corporate high-rise, but in a small diner in Hapville, Georgia. In 1946, a man named Samuel Truit Cathy opened a restaurant called The Dwarf Grill. For years, he painstakingly tinkered with his recipe for a boneless chicken breast seasoned to perfection, pressure cooked in peanut oil for a juicy interior and served on a simple buttered bun with two pickles. In 1967, he opened the very first restaurant named Chick-fil-A and a cultural phenomenon was born. So, what sets the Cathy family and their business apart in the hyperco competitive world of fast food? an unwavering almost defiant commitment to a unique business philosophy rooted in their faith. Truet Cathy, a devout Southern Baptist, made it a non-negotiable core policy that all Chick-fil-A restaurants must close on Sundays. He believed his employees deserved a guaranteed day for rest, family, and worship, regardless of the financial cost. Business analysts estimate the company forgoes over a billion dollars in sales every single year because of this rule. And yet Chick-fil-A is a financial juggernaut. Its restaurants generate more money per location than any other fast food chain in the United States, even while being open one less day a week than all of their competitors. That phenomenal success is built on a legendary obsession with customer service, the my pleasure culture, and a unique franchise model that attracts highly motivated hands-on operators. But the family's deeply held conservative Christian values have also repeatedly thrust them into the center of national controversy. Public comments by family members on traditional marriage and millions in corporate donations to organizations with anti-LGBTQ stances sparked widespread protests and boycots throughout the 2010s. And yet through it all, the brand's intensely loyal customer base only seemed to grow stronger. The Cathy family story is a fascinating American paradox. a company that built a multi-billion dollar fortune on a foundation of southern hospitality while simultaneously becoming a lightning rod in the nation's ongoing culture wars. At number six, with a sprawling fortune of 41.6 billion, we find a family whose name you probably associate with luxury hotels, but whose true influence runs much, much deeper. The Pritsker family. This Chicago based dynasty is a masterclass in how to strategically leverage one successful business to build a diversified multi-generational empire of staggering complexity and reach. The architects of this empire were two brothers, Jay and Donald Pritsker. In 1957, Jay made a seemingly modest purchase, a motel called the Hyatt House, located near the Los Angeles airport. It was a small beginning, but the brothers shared a huge ambitious vision for a new kind of upscale hotel that catered specifically to business travelers and conventions. That single motel became the seed for what would grow into the global Hyatt Hotels Corporation. But the Pritskers were far too shrewd to put all their eggs in one hospitality basket. They were brilliant and often famously aggressive investors. Operating quietly through their family holding firm, the Marmon Group. They began buying up a vast and eclectic portfolio of companies. They bought everything from industrial manufacturers of railroad cars and construction equipment to famously TransUnion, the credit reporting agency. For decades, they operated almost completely under the radar, managing their immense and growing wealth through a complex labyrinthine web of over a thousand trusts. A financial strategy that not only protected their assets, but also made them pioneers in the art of sophisticated tax avoidance. However, that carefully guarded wall of privacy came crashing down in the early 2000s. After Jay Pritsker, the patriarch, passed away, a bitter and acrimonious family feud exploded into the public eye. Younger family members filed lawsuits against the elders, alleging that their trust funds had been plundered and mismanaged. The ensuing legal battle was vicious and threatened to tear the entire Pritska Empire apart from the inside. In the end, facing a potential catastrophe, the family agreed to a radical and unprecedented solution. They would systematically divide the entire $15 billion fortune among 11 different cousins. This massive restructuring known within financial circles as the Pritsker workout had a remarkable and unforeseen effect. It unleashed a new generation of 11 independently superw wealthy Pritskars into the world. Each with billions of dollars to their name and free to pursue their own ambitions. Penny Pritsker went on to serve as the US Secretary of Commerce under President Obama. Her brother JB Pritsker is the billionaire governor of Illinois. Gigi Pritsker has become a successful Hollywood film producer. From one simple motel near LAX, the Pritsker dynasty has multiplied, proving that even a fractured and embattled empire can remain an undeniable and powerful force in American life. Entering our top five is a dynasty that doesn't deal in tangible goods like candy or cars. They deal in the most abstract and powerful commodity of all, money itself. Trillions and trillions of dollars of it. The Johnson family of Boston, with a net worth of 44.8 billion, controls one of the largest and most influential financial institutions on the planet, Fidelity Investments. The story begins in 1946 when a Boston lawyer named Edward C. Johnson II took over a small unassuming mutual fund known as the Fidelity Fund. At a time when investing in the stock market was a privilege reserved almost exclusively for the super rich, Johnson had a profoundly revolutionary idea to make the market accessible to the average American middle class. He became a champion of the mutual fund, a vehicle that allowed everyday people, teachers, factory workers, small business owners to pull their money and have it managed by a seasoned professional. It was financial democratization on a scale never seen before. Under his guidance, Fidelity became an engine of innovation. He introduced money market funds that came with checkwriting privileges, a radical convenience at the time. His company was one of the very first to sell retirement plans like IAS directly to individuals, bypassing the traditional gatekeepers. He wasn't just managing money. He was fundamentally changing how Americans saved for college, for a new home, and for their retirement. His son, Edward Ned Johnson III, took the helm in 1977. Ned was known as an eccentric but brilliant leader who saw the future in technology long before his competitors. He poured billions into state-of-the-art computer systems and created the first telephone-based stock trading platform, allowing customers to make trades from their own homes. He transformed Fidelity into a true financial supermarket, offering everything from discount brokerage services to managing the 401k plans for millions of employees at major corporations. By insisting on keeping the company private, he could ignore the short-term pressures of quarterly earnings reports and focus entirely on long-term growth and technological dominance. Today, the third generation is firmly in charge. Ned's daughter, Abigail Johnson, now serves as the chairman and CEO of Fidelity. She is widely considered one of the most powerful women in the world of finance. The family still owns an estimated 49% of the company, giving them near total control over this financial behemoth. From a single mutual fund, they built a colossal fortune by giving millions of ordinary people a chance to build their own. At number four, we have a family that is the very definition of old money. They are sprawling, they are powerful, and they are so intensely secretive that they make other private families on this list look like reality TV stars. With a staggering collective net worth of $60.6 billion, the Cargill McMillan family owns the largest private company in the entire United States. A global agrous giant so deeply embedded in the world's food supply that you have almost certainly eaten its products today without ever knowing it. This dynasty's story begins in the immediate aftermath of the Civil War. In 1865, a man named William Wallace Cargill started a small single warehouse grain storage business in Iowa. As the railroads relentlessly expanded westward, opening up the American heartland, Cargill followed. He strategically set up grain elevators along the new routes, becoming a vital middleman in the nation's booming agricultural economy. He would buy, store, and trade grain, a simple but essential business. When his son-in-law, John McMillan, joined the business, the two family names became inextricably locked together, forging a dynasty. For over 150 years, Cargill Inc. has grown almost entirely in the shadows. Today, it is an agrous titan on a scale that is difficult to even comprehend. It operates in 70 countries and has its hands in nearly every part of the global food chain, trading massive quantities of grain, producing the chocolate used by other candy makers, manufacturing animal feed for livestock, and processing beef and poultry. The company's annual revenues are often larger than those of public giants like Coca-Cola or Goldman Sachs. Yet, it files no public earnings reports. What's most astounding about the Cargill McMillan clan is their fierce, unwavering commitment to privacy and family control. An estimated 90 family members still collectively own around 88% of the company. This ownership structure has had a stunning side effect. It has created more individual billionaires within a single family than almost any other in the world, a whopping 21 by one recent count. They are famous for living modestly, aggressively avoiding the press, and almost never making headlines. They are the absolute antithesis of the celebrity billionaire. Their long-term view allows them to make decisions not for the next fiscal quarter, but for the next generation. This strategy has enabled them to survive global conflicts, economic depressions, and commodity market crashes that would have crushed a bowam publicly traded company. The Cargill McMillan family operates like a hidden aristocracy. Their immense power flowing not from a famous brand name, but from their silent, essential, and near total control over the intricate global system that feeds the world. We've just crossed the $60 billion mark and the air is getting thin. We're about to enter the absolute stratosphere of American wealth, where fortunes are measured in the hundreds of billions. The top three families on our list have amassed fortunes so vast they are hard to truly comprehend. Before we reveal them, if you're finding these stories about the nature of generational power as fascinating as we do, take a moment to hit that subscribe button and ring the notification bell. We release deep dive videos like this every week, and you won't want to miss what's coming next. Now, let's get back to the countdown. On to number three. Cracking our top three is a family whose very name is as synonymous with industrial might as it is with intense political controversy. The Koch family with a massive net worth of $116 billion runs America's second largest private company, a sprawling and diversified conglomerate that has made them one of the most powerful and deeply polarizing forces in the entire country. The empire's foundation was laid by one man, Fred Coke, in the 1920s. He was a brilliant and innovative chemical engineer who invented a new, more efficient method for refining heavy crude oil into gasoline. However, his breakthrough was met with fierce resistance in the US where he was blocked by a barrage of lawsuits from the big established oil companies. So he took his invention overseas, finding an unlikely client, Joseph Stalin's Soviet Union. He spent years building refineries for the communist state, but grew to despise the ideology after witnessing its brutality and inefficiency firsthand. He returned to America with a renewed fervent anti-communist zeal and founded what would eventually become Coke Industries in 1940. When Fred died in 1967, ownership of the company passed to his four sons, Frederick, Charles, David, and William. What followed was not a smooth transition, but a Shakespearean battle of brother against brother. A nasty public and prolonged legal war erupted in the 1980s when William and Frederick attempted to stage a hostile takeover and forced Charles out of the company. In the end, Charles and his brother, David, emerged victorious, buying out their other two brothers shares for over a billion dollars. A move that over time would make Charles and David two of the richest men in the world. Under Charles's relentless and visionary leadership, Ko Industries transformed from a midsized oil company into a diversified industrial beast. They went on an acquisition spree, buying company after company. Today, Coke Industries is involved in a dizzying array of sectors from oil pipelines and fertilizer to brrawy paper towels, Dixie cups, the stain resistant carpeting in your office, and the high-tech electronic components inside your smartphone. Its annual revenues now top an incredible $125 billion. But the Ko's influence extends far beyond the boardroom. Driven by a staunch libertarian philosophy, Charles and David used their immense fortune to build one of the most sophisticated and powerful political influence networks in American history. They have poured hundreds of millions of dollars into a vast web of think tanks, advocacy groups, and political campaigns that push for free market policies, tax cuts, and deregulation. This has made them heroes to many conservatives and free market advocates and arch villains to liberals who accuse them of using their dark money network to bend the American political system to serve their corporate interests. After David's death in 2019, Charles continues to lead both the company and their political network, preparing the next generation to take the reigns of this powerful and controversial dynasty. At number two, we find a family that built a mind-blowing $17 billion fortune on two of life's simplest and most universal pleasures, candy and pets. The Mars family, the sole owners of the global empire Mars Incorporated, are perhaps the most obsessively secretive dynasty on this entire list. For over a century, they have operated in almost total anonymity while simultaneously creating some of the world's most famous and beloved brands. The story begins in 1911 when Frank C. Mars began making and selling buttercream candy from his own kitchen in Tacoma, Washington. The business struggled for years until his ambitious son, Forest Senior, joined him. Together they developed the Milky Way Bar, a confection with a new God center that became a massive national hit. But Forest was intensely driven and notoriously difficult. He constantly clashed with his father over the direction of the company and eventually left in a bitter feud, taking the Milky Way formula with him to England. There, with his father's blessing, but on his own terms, he built his own separate candy empire from scratch. It was during a trip to Spain during its brutal civil war that Forest had his next billion-dollar idea. He observed soldiers eating small pellet-sized pieces of chocolate coated in a hard sugar shell that prevented them from melting in the heat. He immediately recognized the genius of it, brought the idea back to the United States and in 1941 patented M&M's, a product that would become a global sensation and a cornerstone of the family fortune. He was a tyrannical boss obsessed with quality control and ruthless efficiency. After his father died, he returned to the US and staged a reverse takeover of the family company, merging his own successful business with the one he had abandoned years before to create the modern Mars Inky. But Forest Senor's greatest and most preient move was still to come. He astutely saw the emerging market for commercial pet food and diversified aggressively, launching brands like Pedigree and Whiskers. Today, Mars's pet care division is just as large, if not larger, than its famous confectionary business. If you've ever bought IAMS or Royal Cananan for your dog, or taken your pet to a banfield or VCA animal hospital, you have directly contributed to the Mars family fortune. The family's cultlike commitment to privacy is legendary. Mars, Inc. is still 100% familyowned and family members are reportedly forbidden from speaking to the press or even from being photographed for public purposes. This impenetrable wall of secrecy has allowed them to build their global empire for over a century with almost no public scrutiny. Today, the third and fourth generations of the Mars family silently run this colossal empire, a quiet but utterly dominant force in the global economy. We have seen fortunes built on media, finance, pipelines, and candy. But the family at number one on our list dwarfs them all. Their wealth isn't just a fortune. It's a vast economic force that has completely and irrevocably changed the way the entire world shops. With a net worth of an almost unbelievable $267 billion, the Walton family is by a massive and almost incomprehensible margin the richest dynasty in America and the world. To put that number in perspective, their fortune is more than double the Mars families at number two. It is larger than the entire gross domestic product of many countries. That is the sheer worldaltering scale of the company they built, Walmart. The story begins with one man, Sam Walton. Born in Oklahoma during the Great Depression, he was a man defined by two unshakable traits: relentless, burning ambition and an almost pathological frugality. After serving in World War II, he bought a Ben Franklin variety store in Newport, Arkansas. He was a natural-born retailer, constantly experimenting and always looking for new ways to cut his costs and lower his prices for customers. His motto was devastatingly simple, always low prices. After finding great success, his landlord shockingly refused to renew his lease, forcing him to sell the successful store. He had to move his family to the small remote town of Bentonville, Arkansas, and start all over again from scratch. This time, he made sure he owned the building. From his new store, Walton's 5 and 10, he perfected the revolutionary strategy that would one day conquer the world. He focused on rural towns that were completely ignored by the big retail chains like Sears and Kmart. He invested heavily in logistics to create a hypereefficient supply chain, buying goods in massive volumes at rockbottom prices. And he passed those savings directly on to the customer. On July 2nd, 1962 in Rogers, Arkansas, he opened the first store to bear the name Walmart. The rest is the stuff of business legend. Walmart's revolutionary big box stores swept across America, offering a range of products and prices that local merchants simply couldn't match, hollowing out countless main streets in the process. The company went public in 1970, a move that provided the capital for its explosive growth. But the Walton family wisely kept a huge ownership stake, the very foundation of their incredible fortune. Today, Walmart is the world's largest retailer by revenue with nearly 10,500 stores in 19 countries and staggering annual revenues of $648 billion. The family, Sam's three living children, Rob, Jim, and Alice, along with the family of his late son, John, still owns about 45% of the company's stock. That massive stake pays them billions upon billions of dollars in dividends every single year. A fire hose of cash that constantly replenishes their colossal fortune. The family's legacy, however, is deeply complicated. On one hand, they are seen as champions of the consumer who have saved American families untold billions of dollars. But they are also fiercely criticized for their devastating impact on small businesses and for a long and controversial history of low wages, poor benefits, and aggressive anti-UN policies. The third generation of Waltons is now taking on bigger roles within the empire, ensuring the dynasty's control for decades to come. Their story is the ultimate paradoxical fulfillment of the American dream, demonstrating how one man's simple idea and relentless uncompromising drive can build an empire that not only creates the world's largest family fortune, but completely changes the very way we live. From the reinvented media empire of the Cox family at number 10 to the world dominating retail machine of the Waltons at number one, the wealth we've discussed today is of a magnitude that is almost beyond imagination. The combined fortunes of just these 10 families represent a level of concentrated financial power that can shape entire economies, influence the course of politics, and quietly direct the future of major industries for generations to come. Their stories, while unique, all share common, powerful themes. A visionary and often ruthless founder, an unyielding drive for growth, a keen eye for diversification, and most importantly, a carefully constructed plan to keep power and control within the family. Unlike the new money billionaires of the tech and finance worlds, these dynasties prove that the most enduring fortunes are often the quietest. They are patient, they are strategic, and above all, they are built to last. They demonstrate that real generational power isn't always about having the loudest voice or the most visible brand. Sometimes it's about owning the room itself and the building it's in for the next hundred years. What do you think of these powerful families? Was there a story that surprised you or a legacy you find particularly controversial? Let us know your thoughts in the comments below. And if you enjoyed this deep dive into the world of generational wealth, don't forget to give this video a like and subscribe for more explorations into the empires that shape our world. Thanks for watching.

- Why Wealthy People Think Differently (Psychology of the Rich): Kind: captions Language: en You work hard. You're smart. You make plans. Yet somehow, wealth feels like it's always just out of reach. Your neighbor, who seems less capable than you, just bought a new house. Your college friend who barely passed exams now runs a successful business. Meanwhile, you're reading articles, taking courses, trying to figure out what you're missing. What if I told you the problem isn't your intelligence or your effort? What if the real obstacle is hiding inside your own head, running quietly in the background like a computer program you never installed? Your brain makes thousands of financial decisions every single day. And most of them are working against you. Not because you're foolish, but because your mind operates on ancient rules that helped your ancestors survive in the wilderness, but now keep you trapped in patterns that destroy wealth. Today, we're going to examine the invisible mental barriers that separate rich people from everyone else, and more importantly, how you can recognize these barriers in yourself and finally break through them. Scientists who study wealth and psychology have discovered something surprising. The gap between rich and poor isn't really about money at all. It's about how different groups of people process information, make decisions, and respond to opportunities. When researchers compare brain activity between wealthy investors and struggling workers, they find distinct patterns in how these groups evaluate risk, delay gratification, and learn from mistakes. This isn't about being born with a special brain. It's about developing specific mental habits over time. Habits that either multiply your resources [music] or slowly drain them away. The fascinating part is that most people never realize these patterns exist. They go through life making the same financial mistakes repeatedly, wondering why nothing changes, never understanding that their own thoughts are the prison walls. Let's start with something that affects almost everyone. You probably hate losing money more than you enjoy making it. This sounds obvious, but [music] the implications are enormous. Research in behavioral economics shows that the pain of losing $100 [music] is roughly twice as intense as the pleasure of gaining $100. Your brain has a built-in negativity bias that made sense when humans lived in small tribes where one bad decision could mean death. But in the modern world, this same bias causes you to play it safe when you should be taking calculated risks. You keep money in savings accounts that barely keep up with inflation because at least you won't lose it. You sell investments the moment they show profit because you're terrified of watching gains disappear. You hold on to losing stocks for years, unable to accept the loss and move on. Every one of these choices feels rational in the moment. Your brain is protecting you from pain. But protection from short-term pain often means long-term poverty. If you are new to this channel, please subscribe to this channel. [music] This helps to spread the knowledge to others. Rich people have learned to override this instinct. They've trained themselves to think in probabilities rather than emotions. When they look at an investment, they don't ask how terrible they'll feel if it goes wrong. They calculate the likely outcomes, the potential upside, the realistic downside, and make decisions based on mathematical expectation rather than fear. This doesn't mean they never feel afraid. Fear is human. But they've developed the discipline to act despite fear when the numbers make sense. They understand something crucial that poor people miss. The biggest risk isn't losing money on a smart bet. The biggest risk is letting inflation and missed opportunities slowly erode your purchasing power while you hide from decisions. Playing it safe guarantees that you'll fall behind because the world keeps moving forward whether you participate or not. Another mental trap that destroys wealth is the desperate need to fit in with the crowd. Humans evolved in small groups where being different could get you expelled or killed. Your ancestors survived by watching what everyone else did and copying it. If the whole tribe started running, you ran [music] too. You didn't stop to analyze whether there was actually a tiger nearby. This instinct is still firing in your brain every single day. When you see everyone around you buying houses, your brain interprets their behavior as important information. If everyone's doing it, it must be safe. It must be smart. So, you buy, too. Even though prices have already climbed to dangerous levels. When you watch friends invest in cryptocurrency and brag about their gains, your brain screams that you're being left behind. You jump in, usually right before the crash, because you couldn't stand the psychological pain of being on the outside. Markets move in cycles specifically because of this herd behavior. Prices rise as more people pile in, creating the illusion that the trend will continue forever. Everyone develops a story about why this time is different, why the old rules don't apply. Then reality reasserts itself. Prices collapse. The crowd panics and sells everything. [music] You end up buying high and selling low, the perfect formula for destroying wealth. All because you couldn't resist the gravitational pull of social proof. Wealthy people have learned to recognize this pattern and resist it. When they see crowds getting excited about an investment, they get nervous. They know that mass enthusiasm usually signals a top, [music] not a beginning. When they see everyone running away from an asset class in fear, they start paying attention because that's often when real opportunities appear. This doesn't mean they're smarter. [music] It means they've developed the emotional strength to stand apart from the crowd and think independently even when being different feels uncomfortable. There's another way your brain sabotages your wealth that's even more subtle. You value today enormously more than tomorrow. $1,000 in your hand right now feels real, solid, valuable. $10,000 20 years from now feels like an abstract concept, almost imaginary. Psychologists call this present bias or temporal discounting. And it's one of the main reasons most people never build wealth. Your brain evolved to grab resources immediately [music] because our ancestors lived in an uncertain world where tomorrow might never come. If you found food, you ate it now. If you didn't, [music] someone else would or it would spoil. But in the modern economy, this instinct becomes toxic. Every time you're tempted to buy something you don't really need, your present focused brain lights up with anticipation. You can imagine the pleasure of owning it, using [music] it, showing it off. That pleasure feels real and immediate. Meanwhile, the abstract idea of having more money in the future barely registers emotionally. So, you spend and you keep spending and you wonder why you never get ahead. Your brain also has a powerful tendency to seek out information that confirms what you already believe and ignore everything that contradicts it. You probably think you're objective and open-minded. Almost everyone does. But research consistently shows that humans are remarkably good at filtering reality to support their existing views. If you believe you're a good investor, you'll remember your winning picks [music] and conveniently forget the losers. If you think real estate always goes up, you'll notice every article about rising prices and scroll past warnings about bubbles. Your brain isn't trying to deceive you. It's trying to protect your self-image and reduce the cognitive discomfort that comes from admitting you were wrong. But this protective mechanism keeps you trapped in failing strategies. You can't improve if you can't see your mistakes clearly. You can't learn if you only pay attention to information that makes you feel smart. Psychologist Daniel Conaman spent decades studying how people make decisions under uncertainty. He found that humans are consistently overconfident in their own judgments and abilities. Most people think they're above average drivers, above average investors, above average judges of character. Mathematically, [music] this is impossible. But our brains don't care about mathematics when self-image is at stake. This overconfidence is expensive. You read a few articles about stockpicking and convince yourself you have a talent for it. You make a few lucky picks that happen to work out. Your brain interprets luck as skill. Now you're confident. You invest more money, take bigger risks, expand into areas you don't understand. Eventually, reality catches up. Your luck runs out. [music] The losses come. But even then, your brain protects you from the truth by blaming external factors rather than admitting your analysis was flawed. Wealthy people combat this by keeping detailed records of their decisions and outcomes. Not mental records that can be revised and distorted over time, but written documentation that can't be changed. They write down what they believed, why they believed it, what they expected to happen, and then months or years later, they review what actually happened. This process is painful because it forces you to confront your errors directly. Your brain hates it, but it's also how you actually improve rather than just repeating the same mistakes with different details. They also actively search for people who disagree with them. Most people surround themselves with others who think similarly [music] because it feels good to have your views validated. Rich people deliberately seek out smart people who think they're wrong because they know their own brain is hiding important information from them. They need outside perspectives to see their blind spots. There's another mental trap that costs people enormous amounts of money. You get anchored to irrelevant numbers and can't let go. You bought a stock at $50 per share. It drops to $30. [music] You refuse to sell because in your mind you haven't really lost money until you sell. You're waiting for it to get back to 50, back to where you bought it. As if the market cares what price you paid. The market doesn't know you exist. The only question that matters is whether this stock is a good investment at $30 right now. Would you buy it today at this price if you didn't already own it? If the answer is no, you should sell. But most people can't think this clearly because they're anchored to that $50 price in their head. That number has become their reference point for reality, even though it's completely irrelevant to the stock's actual value. This happens everywhere in your financial life. [music] You see a car priced at $40,000. Then the dealer shows you one at 35,000. Your brain thinks you're getting a deal because it's $5,000 less than the anchor, but maybe that car is only worth 30,000. [music] You're overpaying, but you feel like you're winning because you beat the anchor. Salespeople understand this psychological principle and use it against you constantly. They set high anchors to make everything else seem reasonable by comparison. The defense is to completely ignore the first number you hear and do your own independent analysis. What is this actually worth? Not what are they asking, not what someone else paid, not what it cost last year. What is it worth today based on its actual characteristics and the current market? Another psychological pattern that keeps people poor is envy. Not greed, which at least motivates you to want more for yourself, but envy, [music] which makes you obsessed with what other people have. Your neighbor buys a new car. Suddenly, your car, which was perfectly fine yesterday, feels old and embarrassing. You feel a pressure to keep up, so you buy a new car you can't afford. Your coworker gets a promotion and starts talking about their higher salary. You feel inadequate, so you spend more on clothes and restaurants to feel successful. Your friend posts vacation photos from an expensive resort. You feel like you're missing out on life, so you book a trip you can't afford and put it on a credit card. None of these purchases made you happier. You didn't even want them until you saw someone else had them. But envy converted you into a spending machine, and it's draining your ability to build wealth. The insidious thing about envy is that it never ends. There's always someone with more. [music] You buy a nice car, but someone has a nicer one. You get promoted, but someone got a bigger promotion. You take a vacation, but someone went somewhere more exotic. You're running on a treadmill that speeds up every time you think you're catching up. [music] Meanwhile, all the money you're spending to keep pace with others is money that's not invested, not compounding, not working to build your actual future security. You're sacrificing real wealth for the appearance of wealth. And the appearance doesn't even make you feel better because comparison is endless. Research in psychology consistently shows that people who focus on relative status, on how they compare to others, are less happy than people who focus on their own absolute well-being and progress. The act of comparing yourself to others activates stress and dissatisfaction, regardless of how much you actually have. Wealthy people have learned to ignore social comparison. They define success on their own terms and live according to those definitions. They genuinely don't care what their neighbors think about their car or their [music] house or their clothes. This sounds easy, but it's extraordinarily difficult in practice. The social pressure to keep up is constant and intense. It takes real strength to drive a modest car when you could afford better. To live in a normal house when others expect you to upgrade, to wear regular clothes when people judge success by appearance. [music] But this freedom from comparison is what allows rich people to keep their money instead of spending it to impress strangers who don't actually care about them. Warren Buffett lived in the same modest house for decades. Not because he couldn't afford a mansion, [music] but because he didn't need external validation. He defined enough for himself and stopped caring about more once he reached it. Your brain also loves stories and patterns even when they don't exist. After any market movement, financial news creates a narrative to explain it. Stocks rose today on strong earnings reports. Stocks fell today despite strong earnings reports. The same data gets spun into opposite stories depending on what the market did. It all sounds logical and convincing, but most of it is just storytelling grafted onto random noise. The problem is your brain learns from these stories. It creates rules and patterns. When earnings are strong, stocks go up. Except sometimes strong earnings lead to sell-offs because they weren't strong enough relative to expectations. Your brain oversimplifies reality, creates false patterns, and then you make decisions based on those false patterns and lose money. This is why people who watch financial news constantly often perform worse than people who ignore it completely. They're filling their heads with convincing narratives that have no predictive power. Rich people learn to ignore stories and focus on fundamentals. They don't care what narrative the market is telling today. [music] They don't care what explanation journalists create for short-term price movements. They understand that most of what happens day-to-day is noise, random fluctuation that means nothing. What matters is the actual underlying value of businesses over years and decades, not the stories people tell about daily changes. They develop the discipline to tune out the constant chatter and focus on the small number of things that actually matter. This is harder than it sounds because your brain craves stories. Stories make sense of chaos. Stories provide comfort by suggesting the world is predictable and controllable. But in markets, most stories are illusions that cost you money if you believe them. Philosopher Aristotle observed over 2,000 years ago that people who have too much wealth often become unwilling and unable to submit to authority or accept limits. They grow up surrounded by luxury and compliance, never learning discipline or delayed gratification. This creates a particular psychology among the wealthy that's worth understanding. Some people who achieve wealth develop a sense that normal rules don't apply to them. They begin to see other human beings as tools to be used rather than people with their own dignity and worth. They surround themselves with employees, assistants, and advisers who agree with everything they say, creating an echo chamber that reinforces their belief in their own brilliance. This can lead to spectacular failures when reality eventually intrudes on their carefully constructed bubble. Not all wealthy people fall into this trap, but enough do that it represents a real psychological danger of wealth. The same mental habits that help you build money, confidence, decisiveness, willingness to go against the crowd can become toxic when taken too far. Confidence becomes arrogance. Decisiveness becomes impulsiveness. Independence becomes isolation. The key is maintaining what psychologists call intellectual humility. A clear understanding of what you know and what you don't know. The smartest investors are often the ones most willing to say, "I don't understand this well enough to have an opinion." They pass on opportunities that might have made them even richer because they know the limits of their competence. They understand that confidence outside your area of expertise is just arrogance wearing a disguise and arrogance is expensive. So, how do you actually change these patterns? Knowing about psychological biases doesn't automatically fix them. These tendencies are emotional, not intellectual. They happen fast, automatically, below conscious awareness. Your rational mind is slow and takes effort to engage. Most of the time, you're running on autopilot. And autopilot is programmed with all these wealth destroying biases. You need systems that force your rational brain to check your emotional brain before making important decisions. Start with this simple rule. Never make a financial decision in the moment. Never wait at least 24 hours. Sleep on it. Your emotional mind might be caught up in excitement or fear or a compelling story or social pressure. But after sleeping on it, your rational mind gets a chance to engage, to analyze, to check for biases, to think it through properly. Another practical tool is keeping a decision journal. Before making any significant financial choice, write down why you're doing it. [music] What do you believe? What are you assuming? What would have to be true for this to work? What could go wrong? Then months or years later, review what you wrote. Were you right? Were you wrong? Where was your thinking accurate and where was it flawed? This is how you get better over time. This is how you identify your personal biases and learn to correct for them. Most people never do this. They make decisions, forget what they were thinking at the time, and never learn from their mistakes. They repeat the same errors for 40 years with different details and wonder why their financial situation never improves. Create a checklist of questions to ask yourself before any major decision. Am I being influenced by what everyone else is doing? Am I anchored to some irrelevant number? Is this based on actual value or just a compelling story? Am I holding on because of sunk costs? Am I overconfident about something I don't fully understand? Am I doing this because of envy or comparison? These questions force your rational brain to engage. They interrupt your autopilot. You don't have to be perfect. You just have to catch yourself often enough that you stop making the biggest mistakes. Even catching yourself half the time [music] will dramatically improve your financial outcomes over a lifetime. Actively seek out people who disagree with you. Your brain naturally seeks confirmation. So, you have to artificially create contradiction. Before making a big decision, find someone smart who thinks you're wrong. Listen to them. Really [music] listen. Don't just wait for your turn to argue. Try to understand why they see it differently. What do they know that you don't? What are they seeing that you're missing? This is uncomfortable. Your brain hates it. But it's essential because your brain is showing you only what confirms your beliefs. You need someone else to show you the other side. The best investors surround themselves with people who challenge them constantly, [music] who point out flaws in their thinking, who aren't afraid to say when the emperor has no clothes. Start small with changing your psychology. Don't try to fix everything at once. Pick one bias, maybe loss aversion. For the next month, just notice every time you make a decision based on fear of loss rather than probability of gain. Just notice it. Don't even try to change it yet. Just become aware of when it's happening. Once you can see it operating in real time, then you can start to override it. But you have to see it first. Most people never see their own biases in action. They experience the emotions and make the decisions without ever connecting the dots. Awareness is the first step toward change. The fundamental insight is this. Getting rich is not about secrets or shortcuts. It's about thinking correctly. And thinking correctly is not natural. Your brain is wired in ways that made sense for survival in ancient environments but keep you poor in the modern world. Every single day, your psychology is working against your financial interests, making you follow crowds, fear losses, spend today, [music] believe stories, hold grudges against sunk costs, and compare yourself to others. These aren't character flaws. They're human nature. Everyone has these tendencies. The difference is that some people become aware of them and build systems to override them. [music] Those people accumulate wealth. Everyone else works hard and wonders why they stay broke. You now understand how your own mind has been sabotaging you. [music] You know the specific traps. You know why you make the financial mistakes you make. More importantly, [music] you know how to build systems to protect yourself from your own psychology. That puts you ahead of almost everyone because most people go through their entire lives being controlled by these biases without ever understanding what's happening. You have a choice now. You can ignore this information and keep operating on autopilot. Keep making the same emotional decisions that feel right but keep you poor. Or [music] you can do the hard work of building systems, overriding your instincts, and thinking like wealthy people think. Not because you're smarter than everyone else, but because you understand psychology and they don't. The difference between building wealth and staying broke comes down to this choice. Choose wisely. If you enjoyed the video, please like it and share your thoughts in the comment. [music] If you are new to this channel, please subscribe.

It whispers and it warns. But it only wins when you obey it. And every time you believe it, every time you shrink instead of step forward, you trade power for comfort. You give up growth for safety. You stall. You suffocate. You stand still. Not because you're weak, but because fear has convinced you that stillness is survival. But not today. It whispers that you're not ready, that you're not smart enough, talented enough, worthy enough, that if you try, you'll fail. That if you speak, they'll laugh. That if you chase that dream, you'll crash and burn. And because fear wears the voice of your own mind, you believe it. You accept it as reality, never realizing that it's just a story you've repeated so many times it became familiar. Not true. Just familiar. Fear thrives in imagination. It takes a moment of uncertainty and builds a movie around it. You don't just worry about a difficult conversation, you see it go horribly wrong. You don't just hesitate to try something new. You see yourself failing publicly and permanently. You don't just fear rejection. You imagine humiliation, abandonment, and proof that you never had what it takes in the first place. And that's how fear wins. Not with truth, but with repetition. The more you rehearse the worst case scenario in your mind, the more your nervous system believes it's real. And when your brain believes the threat is real, it floods your body with signals to retreat. Heart racing, breath shallow, muscles tense. Suddenly, you're not living in the present. You're imprisoned in a future that hasn't even happened. And in that state, even the smallest step forward feels impossible. But here's the breakthrough. If fear is built by repetition, so is confidence. If fear is a story, you can tell a better one. One where you speak and you're heard. One where you leap and you land. One where you fail and rise anyway. Because the truth is, the mind can't tell the difference between imagination and memory. If you can visualize defeat, you can visualize victory. If you can rehearse disaster, you can also rehearse domination. This is the battlefield. Not out there, but in here. And the moment you stop accepting fear script and start rewriting it, you take back control. You don't have to feel brave to act brave. You don't have to feel certain to move forward. It's your signal. It's the flag that says you're standing at the edge of your growth. And what happens next? That's up to you. Fear grows in the dark in silence in avoidance. The more you run from it, the bigger it gets. The more you try to outthink it, the louder it becomes. Because fear doesn't shrink with time, it shrinks with exposure. And this is where most people get it wrong. They wait for courage to arrive before they take the leap. They wait for the fear to fade before they take the first step. But courage doesn't come before action. It comes because of action. Imagine standing at the edge of a freezing pool.
