- 7 Signs Someone is Secretly Wealthy: Kind: captions Language: es-MX La mayoría de los millonarios y multimillonarios son completamente invisibles. Probablemente estén sentados junto a ti en Starbucks, y ni siquiera sabes que tienen siete, ocho o quizá hasta nueve cifras en el banco. En mi opinión, hay siete señales reveladoras que delatan si alguien es rico en secreto o no, así que si sabes qué buscar, podrás encontrarlos. Sinceramente creo que si logras adoptar algunos de sus hábitos, podría ser la clave para construir una verdadera riqueza por tu cuenta. La primera señal que vamos a ver es que la gente rica evita a toda costa hablar de las tres E. Ahora, estas tres E se refieren a ganar, a lo caro y al exceso. Por la primera E, que es ganar, nunca hablan del dinero que están ganando porque para ellos, si alguien siempre está hablando de dinero y de cuánto gana, demuestra que esa persona todavía está en la etapa de querer probar algo. Los verdaderamente ricos no tienen nada que probar porque en secreto ya saben que tienen el dinero y no hay necesidad de decírselo a nadie. La segunda E: no presumen su última compra cara. Mientras otros publican bolsas de diseñador o relojes de lujo en redes, los ricos en secreto callan sus adquisiciones. Porque si alguien habla constantemente de cuánto costó su carro, de lo caras que fueron sus vacaciones, es una clara señal de que estiraron su presupuesto para poder pagarlo y están tratando de maximizar cada dólar que gastaron obteniendo algo de fama en redes sociales o quizá contándoselo a sus amigos. Y la tercera E es que evitan vivir en exceso, al menos en cuanto a su imagen externa. La gente rica entiende que los verdaderos lujos en la vida son tener opciones, comodidad y libertad, y no tener las cosas más ostentosas. Si alguna vez te encuentras con una persona que habla de cuánto gana, cuál fue la compra más cara que ha hecho recientemente, o simplemente vive en exceso y lo presume, puedes apostar que esa persona probablemente no es rica. ¿Te crees mucho por tu Range Rover? Pues sí. Al menos yo tengo carro. ¿Tú siquiera tienes uno? Quédate aquí. ¿Quién es el plebeyo ahora? ¡¿Quién es el plebeyo ahora?! Eso pensé. Okay. Entonces, eso significa lo contrario. Los que son callados, son ricos en secreto, y creo que si puedes adoptar este tipo de actitud en tu vida, podría ayudarte a generar más riqueza. Porque la energía hipotética que gastarías en las apariencias, o en impresionar a otros, podría redirigirse a construir tu patrimonio neto real. La señal número dos de que alguien es rico en secreto: conduce un auto confiable, de cinco a más de diez años, ya pagado. No manejan autos ostentosos como BMW de alta gama, Porsches, Rolls-Royces o Lamborghinis. Eso es porque los ricos saben lo brutal de la depreciación, así que tirar quinientos a ochocientos dólares al mes en la mensualidad de un carro por algo que pierde valor cada día es un retorno de inversión muy negativo. Y si haces cuentas, podrías invertir de seis mil a nueve mil seiscientos dólares anuales en su lugar, y en diez años con rendimientos compuestos, eso bien podría valer más de cien mil. Además, un auto de cinco a más de diez años ya perdió la mayor parte de su valor por depreciación. Las curvas de depreciación de autos varían, pero se aplanan drásticamente tras cinco años. Esto significa que la mayor parte de la depreciación ya ha ocurrido, por lo que el valor se estabiliza. Si quieres ahorrar dinero en cuanto a carros, creo que tiene mucho sentido comprar un carro usado con unas treinta mil millas o quizá de tres a cinco años de uso. Ese suele ser el punto ideal entre valor y depreciación, pero si solo quieres ser una persona rica que pasa desapercibida, quizá optarías por algo más viejo. Pueden ver en esta gráfica que estos son los modelos de los carros, y el "aquí" que se indica en la gráfica es el punto de inflexión alrededor del año seis o siete, y es cuando la depreciación realmente se desacelera. Los ricos en secreto entienden que un auto es solo transporte. Prefieren ese dinero extra en inversión, evitando pagos de auto y destinándolo a activos que generen riqueza. En otras palabras, prefieren ser ricos que solo parecer ricos y al mismo tiempo deber un montón de dinero por un carro. La señal número tres de que alguien es rico en secreto es que el dinero que sí gastan lo invierten en cosas que la gente normal ni siquiera consideraría. gastan su dinero en cosas poco comunes, por ejemplo, para tener más tiempo, pueden tener un pasatiempo de coleccionables raros, o gastan dinero en pequeños lujos o comodidades de las que no te enterarías a menos que, por alguna razón, pudieras pasar veinticuatro/siete con ellos todos los días. Hablemos primero del tiempo. Una persona rica entiende que el único recurso finito en todo el mundo y en la vida es el tiempo. Por lo tanto, podrían comprar su tiempo de vuelta y están dispuestos a gastar una cantidad desproporcionada de dinero en las cosas que pueden devolverles ese tiempo. En viajes, podrían volar en privado, evitando seguridad, saliendo cuando quieran y saltándose el tedioso abordaje y desembarque. Para la comida, un chef privado les hace las compras y cocina saludable, ahorrándoles incontables horas semanales. Y las tareas del hogar, también las delegan. Para eso, seguro tienen mucha ayuda. Y en todos estos casos de ahorro de tiempo, realmente no tendrías idea de que alguien gasta dinero en estos servicios a menos que pudieras observar a esta persona por un período prolongado. La siguiente categoría de gasto en la que una persona rica podría derrochar, es en pasatiempos poco comunes o caros como coleccionar arte raro, vinos finos, recuerdos deportivos o quizá hasta tarjetas de Pokémon. En lugar de mostrar su riqueza como una persona normal, prefieren hacerlo con una apreciación por el lujo y artículos raros. Poseer algo que cientos de miles ya tienen no es lo suficientemente genial para ellos. Necesitan sentirse únicos; cuanto más raro el artículo, más codiciado. En mi opinión, es naturaleza humana. Cubiertas las necesidades básicas, los ricos ya no compiten con otros. Están tratando de diferenciarse por completo de todos los demás, y una de las formas en que lo hacen es coleccionando bienes raros o quizá comprando su tiempo de vuelta, que es su activo más... eh, valioso. Otra parte de la naturaleza humana es suscribirse a mi boletín gratuito llamado Hump Days. Ahí publicamos noticias de negocios dos veces por semana, los miércoles y domingos. Y si quieres ser rico en secreto algún día y disfrutas los videos de aquí, asegúrate de suscribirte. Es completamente gratis en la descripción de abajo. Perdón, tenía que meter el comercial sutilmente. Bueno, continuemos. La señal número cuatro de que alguien es rico en secreto, es que son conscientes de su imagen. Ya aludí un poco a esto en la primera señal, pero la gente rica entiende que, por lo general, la gente siente envidia de lo que no tiene. Incluso si tu mejor amigo está feliz por ti el noventa y nueve por ciento de las veces que tienes éxito, siempre habrá un pequeño pensamiento en su mente, quizá en el fondo, de "¿Cómo sería si mi vida fuera como la de ellos?" o "¿Por qué no pude ser yo?". Esa es la naturaleza humana. Así que, para no incomodar a nadie, evitan hablar de cualquier cosa que pueda llevar a alguien a entender, por deducción, cuánto dinero podrían tener o cuán exitosos son. Si le preguntas a una persona rica cuál es su patrimonio neto, es probable que diga alguna frase como: "Me ha ido bastante bien", o "Ahí la llevo", o quizá, "Ciertamente no soy la persona más rica que conozco". Nick, no puedes pagarlo. ¿Tu familia es realmente rica? Cómodamente. Eso es exactamente lo que diría una persona súper rica. Y de hecho, esta es una historia real que me pasó. Estaba comiendo con un individuo que vale más de nueve cifras, o sea, al menos cien millones de dólares de patrimonio neto. Y cuando hablaba de sus amigos en la comida, todos multimillonarios, soltó algo en la conversación muy casualmente. Fue algo como: "Soy pobre en comparación con todos mis amigos". Para mí, fue una locura escuchar eso porque me demostró que a medida que subes en la escalera de la riqueza, siempre habrá alguien más rico con quien compararte. Digo, eso es cierto para casi todos en el mundo a menos que seas Elon Musk, claro pero sigue siendo una locura pensar que alguien en el Top 400 de Forbes, por ejemplo, todavía podría compararse con personas que están más arriba en la lista. La comparación está tan arraigada en la naturaleza humana. Estaría dispuesto a apostar que hay al menos una persona en la lista del Top 400 de Forbes que piensa que no es lo suficientemente rica, aunque racionalmente, están perfectamente bien. La gente rica quiere ser cuidadosa con su imagen externa también por razones de seguridad. Si son constantemente ostentosos, o le dicen a la gente cuál es su patrimonio neto, solo están atrayendo atención negativa y resentimiento de otros, lo que podría comprometer su seguridad. La quinta señal de que una persona es rica en secreto es que hace preguntas detalladas sobre compras de menos de cien dólares, pero no pestañea ante gastos grandes y necesarios. Esto es bastante común. Podrías encontrar a una persona rica pasando de veinte a treinta minutos investigando la mejor batería portátil de veinte dólares en Amazon. Leen reseñas, se clavan viendo videos de YouTube, pero de repente, los verás soltar quince mil dólares en un techo nuevo sin pestañear. La diferencia es que la compra de la batería portátil es un gusto, mientras que el techo era una necesidad. Probablemente hay quince opciones diferentes de baterías portátiles que hacen lo mismo, así que la persona rica se toma su tiempo para encontrar el mejor valor por su dinero. El reemplazo del techo de quince mil dólares, por otro lado, es una necesidad, y especialmente si el reemplazo es urgente, simplemente gastarán el dinero para acelerar esa reparación. Personalmente, creo que esto es más un hábito arraigado de alguien que se hizo rico por su propio esfuerzo. Este pequeño hábito de no gastar de más en compras pequeñas a lo largo del año puede sumar un gran ahorro en el transcurso de trescientos sesenta y cinco días. Sin embargo, la gente rica entiende que los grandes gastos necesarios ocurrirán, y cuando esas cosas surgen, tienen que actuar rápida y decisivamente. La señal número seis de que alguien es rico en secreto: ha dominado la espera. Mientras los demás compran cosas al instante, la gente rica investigará una compra por meses, esperará la oferta perfecta o, a veces, decidirá que no necesita ese artículo. También entienden que la mayoría de las ofertas no son reales. Y si esperas lo suficiente, casi todo se pone en liquidación. Si te pones a pensar en lo que es una oferta, es literalmente un descuento inventado basado en un número inventado. ¿Y quién establece ese número? Generalmente es el proveedor o el vendedor de dicho artículo. Una persona rica en secreto podría ser muy frugal y orientada al valor, no le importará esperar, especialmente si eso significa obtener algo que realmente ama, en lugar de algo con lo que solo está conforme. Entonces, una buena lección aquí es que si estás pensando en una compra grande, esperes al menos de siete a treinta días, realmente tienes que hacer una pausa y preguntarte si de verdad seguirás queriendo ese artículo, digamos, dentro de un mes. Y quizá podrías pensar en artículos sustitutos que cumplan el mismo propósito y que no te cuesten un dineral. Esto es solo gratificación postergada, el concepto clave para volverte muy, muy rico más adelante en la vida. A veces, debes renunciar a lo que quieres ahora por una vida financiera más sólida y un mejor futuro. La mayoría no puede postergar la gratificación ni veinticuatro horas, mucho menos cinco o diez años, una disciplina clave para la riqueza. Los ricos en secreto son muy pacientes, esperan no solo ofertas, sino también otras cosas importantes en la vida. La señal número siete: viven en una casa modesta, muy por debajo de su capacidad de pago. Mientras la mayoría se estira con hipotecas apenas pagables, los ricos en secreto hacen lo opuesto. Viven en casas que parecen casi demasiado pequeñas o modestas para alguien con su nivel de ingresos, o simplemente son dueños de su casa y nunca te darían a entender que viven sin hipoteca. Si te compras una casa grande y lujosa que apenas puedes pagar, no solo tendrás más metros cuadrados que decorar, sino que probablemente necesitarás limpiadores, jardineros, y si tienes alberca, un alberquero. Y una vez que tienes una casa grande, no querrás una más pequeña. Tendrás la expectativa de mantener las apariencias para vecinos, amigos y familiares, lo que implica gastar más dinero para mantener tu estilo de vida. Hay muchos niveles de riqueza. Si el objetivo es vivir tu vida más plena, serás más feliz, en mi opinión, si simplemente te mantienes callado. Compartí este tuit en un video anterior, pero creo que resume muy bien los diferentes niveles de riqueza. Brandon Turner (Twitter/X) dice que hay tres niveles de riqueza. Nivel uno: cortas tu césped porque no tienes dinero. Nivel dos: pagas a alguien porque tienes algo de dinero y valoras tu tiempo. Nivel tres: cortas tu césped porque, ya retirado, tienes tanta libertad y tiempo que quieres hacerlo tú mismo, por puro placer. Esto es muy acertado, pues la persona en el nivel tres es probablemente la más rica, disfrutando su vida y cortando su césped por el simple gusto de hacerlo. ¡Gracias por ver el video! Suscríbanse a Hump Days si no lo han hecho. Si les interesa mi próximo video sobre cómo llegar al uno por ciento superior, tengo consejos geniales... ahí. Querrán verlo aquí. Los veo en ese video, o en otro. Gracias por acompañarme. Paz.

- How Wealth Inequality Spiraled Out of Control | Robert Reich: Kind: captions Language: en Elon Musk's wealth has surpassed $200&nbsp; billion. It would take the median U.S. worker OVER 4 MILLION YEARS to make that much. Wealth inequality is eating this country alive. We’re now in America’s second Gilded Age, just like the late 19th century when a handful&nbsp; of robber barons monopolized the economy kept wages down, and bribed lawmakers. While today’s robber barons take joy rides&nbsp; into space, the distance between their gargantuan wealth and the financial struggles&nbsp; of working Americans has never been clearer. During the first 19 months of the&nbsp; pandemic, U.S. billionaires added $2.1 TRILLION dollars to their collective wealth. And the rich have enough political power&nbsp;&nbsp; to cut their taxes to almost nothing&nbsp; — sometimes literally nothing. In fact, Jeff Bezos paid no federal&nbsp; income taxes in 2007 or in 2011. By 2018, the 400 richest Americans paid a&nbsp; lower overall tax rate than almost anyone else. But we can't solve this problem unless we&nbsp; know how it was created in the first place. Let’s start with the basics. Wealth inequality in America is&nbsp; far larger than income inequality. "Income" is what you earn each week or month or&nbsp; year. "Wealth" refers to the sum total of your assets — your car, home, art — anything else you own that’s valuable. Valuable not only because there’s&nbsp; a market for it — a price other people are willing to pay to buy it — but because wealth itself grows. As the population expands and the&nbsp; nation becomes more productive, the overall economy continues to expand. This&nbsp; expansion pushes up the values of stocks, bonds, rental property,&nbsp; homes, and most other assets. Of course recessions and occasional depressions&nbsp; can reduce the value of such assets. But over the long haul, the value&nbsp; of almost all wealth INCREASES. Next: personal wealth comes from two sources. The first source is the income you earn&nbsp; but don’t spend. That’s your savings. When you invest those savings in stocks,&nbsp; bonds, or real property or other assets, you create your personal wealth, which, as we’ve seen, grows over time. The second source of personal wealth is whatever&nbsp; is handed down to you from your parents, grandparents, and maybe even generations&nbsp; before them — in other words, what you INHERIT. The wealth gap between the richest&nbsp; Americans and everyone else is staggering. In the 1970s, the wealthiest 1% owned about&nbsp; 20% of the nation’s total household wealth. Now, they own OVER 35%. Much of their gains over the last 40 years have come from a dramatic&nbsp; increase in the value of shares of stock. For example, if someone invested $1,000 in 1978 in&nbsp; a broad index of stocks — say, the S&P 500 — they would have $31,823 today, adjusted for inflation. Who's benefited from this surge? The richest 1%, who now own&nbsp; HALF of the entire stock market. But the typical worker’s wages have&nbsp; barely grown. Most Americans haven’t earned nearly enough to save anything. Before the pandemic, when the economy appeared to be doing well, almost 80% were living paycheck to paycheck. So as income inequality has widened, the&nbsp; amount that the few high-earning households save — their wealth — has continued to grow. Their growing wealth has allowed them to pass on more and more wealth to their heirs.&nbsp; Take, for example, the Waltons — the family behind the Walmart empire — which has&nbsp;seven heirs on the Forbes billionaires list. Their children, and other rich&nbsp; millennials, will soon consolidate even more of the nation’s wealth. America is&nbsp; now on the cusp of the largest intergenerational transfer of wealth in history.&nbsp; As wealthy boomers pass on, somewhere between $30 to $70 TRILLION will go&nbsp; to their children over the next three decades. These children will be able to&nbsp; live off of this wealth, and then leave the bulk of it — which will continue&nbsp; growing — to their own children … tax-free. After a few generations of this,&nbsp; almost all of America’s wealth could be in the hands of a few thousand families. Concentrated wealth is already&nbsp; endangering our democracy. Wealth doesn’t just beget more&nbsp; wealth — it begets more POWER. Dynastic wealth concentrates power into&nbsp; the hands of fewer and fewer people, who can choose what nonprofits and charities&nbsp; to support, and which politicians to bankroll. This gives an unelected elite enormous sway&nbsp; over both our economy and our democracy. We might come to resemble the kind of dynasties&nbsp; common to European aristocracies in the seventeenth, eighteenth, and nineteenth centuries. Dynastic wealth makes a mockery of the idea that America is a meritocracy, where anyone can&nbsp; make it on the basis of their own efforts.&nbsp; It also runs counter to the basic economic ideas&nbsp; that people earn what they’re worth in the market,&nbsp;&nbsp; and that economic gains should go to those who deserve them. Finally, wealth concentration magnifies gender and&nbsp; race disparities because women and people of color tend to make less, save less, and inherit less. The typical single woman owns only 32 cents of&nbsp;&nbsp; wealth for every dollar of wealth owned by a&nbsp;man. The pandemic likely increased this gap.&nbsp; The racial wealth gap is even&nbsp; starker. The typical Black household&nbsp;&nbsp; owns just 13 cents of wealth for every dollar&nbsp; of wealth owned by the typical white household.&nbsp;&nbsp; The pandemic likely increased this gap, too. In all these ways, dynastic wealth creates a&nbsp;&nbsp; self-perpetuating aristocracy that runs&nbsp; counter to the ideals we claim to live by. The last time America faced anything&nbsp; comparable to the concentration of wealth&nbsp;&nbsp; we face today was at the turn of the 20th century.&nbsp; That was when President Teddy Roosevelt warned&nbsp; that “a small class of enormously wealthy and&nbsp;&nbsp; economically powerful men, whose chief&nbsp; object is to hold and increase their power,”&nbsp;&nbsp; could destroy American democracy. Roosevelt’s answer then was to tax wealth. Congress enacted two kinds of wealth&nbsp;taxes. The first, in 1916, was the estate&nbsp;&nbsp;tax — a tax on the wealth someone has&nbsp; accumulated during their lifetime,&nbsp;&nbsp; paid by the heirs who inherit that wealth. The second tax on wealth, enacted in 1922, was a capital gains tax — a tax on the increased&nbsp; value of those assets, paid when those assets are sold. But both of these wealth taxes have shrunk&nbsp; since then, or become so riddled with loopholes&nbsp;&nbsp; that they haven’t been able to prevent a&nbsp; new American aristocracy from emerging.&nbsp; The Trump Republican tax cut enabled individuals&nbsp; to exclude $11.18 million from their estate taxes.&nbsp;&nbsp; That means ONE COUPLE can pass on more&nbsp; than $22 million to their kids tax-free. Not to mention the very rich often find ways&nbsp; around this tax entirely. As Trump’s former White&nbsp;&nbsp; House National Economic Council director Gary&nbsp;Cohn put it, “Only morons pay the estate tax.” What about capital gains on the soaring&nbsp; values of wealthy people’s stocks,&nbsp;&nbsp; bonds, mansions, and works of art? Here, the biggest loophole is something called the&nbsp;&nbsp; stepped-up basis. If the wealthy hold on to their&nbsp; assets until they die, their heirs inherit them without paying any capital gains taxes whatsoever. All the increased value of those assets is simply erased, for tax purposes. This loophole&nbsp; saves heirs an estimated $40 billion a year. This means that huge accumulations of wealth&nbsp; in the hands of a relatively few households can be passed from generation to generation&nbsp; untaxed — growing along the way — generating comfortable incomes for rich descendants who&nbsp; will never have to work a day of their lives. That’s the dynastic class&nbsp; we’re creating right now. Why have these two wealth taxes eroded?&nbsp; Because, as America’s wealth has concentrated in fewer and fewer hands, the wealthy have more&nbsp; capacity to donate to political campaigns and public relations — and they’ve used that&nbsp; political power to reduce their taxes. It’s exactly what Teddy Roosevelt&nbsp; feared so many years ago. So what do we do? Follow the wisdom of Teddy&nbsp; Roosevelt and tax great accumulations of wealth.&nbsp; The ultra-rich have benefited from the American&nbsp; system — from laws that protect their wealth,&nbsp;&nbsp; and our economy that enabled them to&nbsp; build their fortunes in the first place.&nbsp;&nbsp; The majority of Americans, both&nbsp;&nbsp; Democrats and Republicans, believe the&nbsp; ultra-rich should pay higher taxes.&nbsp; There are many ways to make them do so:&nbsp;&nbsp; Closing the stepped up basis loophole, raising&nbsp; the capital gains tax, and fully funding the Internal Revenue Service so it can properly&nbsp; audit the wealthiest taxpayers, for starters. Beyond those fixes, we need a new wealth&nbsp; tax: a tax of just 2% a year on wealth&nbsp;&nbsp; in excess of $1 million. That’s hardly a drop in the bucket for&nbsp;&nbsp; centi-billionaires like Jeff Bezos and Elon Musk,&nbsp; but it would generate plenty of revenue to invest in&nbsp;&nbsp; healthcare and education so that millions&nbsp; of Americans have a fair shot at making it. One of the most important things you as an&nbsp; individual can do is take the time to understand the realities of wealth inequality in America&nbsp; and how the system has become rigged in favor of&nbsp;&nbsp; those at the top — and demand your political&nbsp; representatives take action to unrig it.&nbsp; Wealth inequality is worse&nbsp; than it has been in a century.&nbsp; We have to stop this vicious cycle — and demand an&nbsp; economy that works for the many, not one that&nbsp;&nbsp; concentrates more and more wealth&nbsp; in the hands of a privileged few.

- Levels of Wealth: Inside The Secret Lives of The Ultra-Rich: Kind: captions Language: en-GB If you earned $1 per second. Then to make 1 million dollars would take you about 11 and a half days. But to make 1 billion dollars, would take you over 31 and a half years. You see, it’s easy to think of wealth in terms of rich or poor, but broadly speaking, there are arguably 5 different levels of rich, and at each level, things change very drastically. The difference between what a millionaire can do, and a billionaire can do, is simply staggering. In this video we’ll be exploring what life is like at each of these different wealth levels, and delving into how the ultra-rich spend their money, including some of their most bizarre and extravagant purchases. But, as you’re watching, you’ll realise that one of the biggest changes that happens at each wealth level, is perspective. Because prices feel relative to how much you earn. So if you compare someone whose earning $40,000 a year with someone earning 400 million a year, it’s 10,000 times more. Which means: A lambo that costs $300,000, to them is the equivalent of spending $30. A $10 million piece of art? Feels like $1,000. A $50 million home in the Hamptons? Feels like $5,000. So whilst to us those purchases would feel so major, to a billionaire, it’s not even something they need to think about. And of course, when you’re that wealthy, it’s not just items and experiences you can buy. But we’ll get to that. Let’s instead start off, with the first level of rich: The Top 1% Even though this phrase is synonymous with being extremely rich, if your income is over about $200,000 a year, you would technically be part of the top 1% of worldwide earners. And so being in the top 1% may not make you feel quite as rich as you think. Because sure, you can typically afford a nice house and car, but with those things comes higher costs, and so you probably don’t feel very financially secure, and you’re likely still working a full time job. Which is why beyond this level, wealth starts being measured in net worth rather than income, as the more wealth you have, the more assets you have, and you’re probably not making your money from a salary. So the next level of rich is a Net Worth of $10 Million - $30 Million At this level, your needs are met. You can live very comfortably. You have a really nice house. You often fly first class internationally. If it’s a special occasion, you might book a $2,000 suite. You can afford any healthcare you need, and in fact no emergency financial situation can destroy your life. But you are still not "rich" in the way that money doesn't matter. You still have to be sensible and careful with big decisions. And in the banking world, you’re still not classified as ultra high net worth'. Which brings us to out next level: Net Worth of $30 Million - $100 Million At this point, you start playing in the big leagues. And most likely you run or have a controlling interest in a very large company. You always stay at 5 star hotels, and you have multiple residences all around the world you can stay at as well. You take holidays during prime time, for example going to Monaco for the Grand Prix, or Cannes for the film festival. And you won’t bat an eyelid when the place you’re staying at costs $10-$20,000 per NIGHT. You have personal assistants, and are starting to have ‘people’ that others have to talk to to get to you. You can buy pretty much anything that normal people think of as ‘stuff rich people buy. Like you can get any cars you want, the fanciest jewellery, and so on. Instead of flying first class, you fly private. Although you’d typically charter a flight or own a private jet fractionally through a service like Net Jets, instead of buying it outright and having to maintain it. But at this level, your social circle changes dramatically too. You probably socialize with Congressmen, Senators and community leaders. You are likely very well respected within some very high class circles. Interestingly though, since you’re likely surrounded by other ultra wealthy people, you may still feel you don’t have enough. Sure, you may have just bought a new Ferrari, but your friend has a limited edition version where only five of them were ever made. Sure, you just had dinner with a local governor, but your billionaire friend just had dinner with the President. Which brings us to our next level of wealth: Net Worth of $100 Million - $1 Billion To be at this level, you likely have ownership or control of a business that most of the public have heard of, even if they haven’t heard of you directly. At this point, you can likely socialize with movie stars, rock stars, and the corporate elite. You can go pretty much anywhere you want, and always in style. You have multiple residences with staff, all over the world, with elite cars at every residence. If it’s your thing, you might have a private island. And of course a private yacht to get you there. Almost any experience you can dream up, you can have. Want to play tennis with your favourite tennis star? Call his people. For a donation of 100k+ to his charity, you can probably play a match with him. Want your favourite band to come and play at your private party? For the right price, they will. Love art? Your people could arrange for the curator of the Louvre to show you around and show you some masterpieces they haven’t exhibited in years. If you can dream an experience up, you can likely have it - donations and fees can get you to anyone. Once you have many hundreds of millions of dollars, it’s the same when buying things - you may hire people to find you the most extravagant versions of items possible. For example, the Australian company Toilet Paper Man produces 22-carat gold toilet paper, for a cost of over 1.3 million. And if you are going to quite literally flush money down the toilet, why not get a gold toilet or entire gold bathroom to go with it? In fact, if you’re ultra-wealthy, you can literally have gold everything. How about a 24 karat gold water bottle, which in itself is a piece of designer art, and comes with spring water from France and Fiji, combined with glacier water from iceland. Or alternatively, how about the world’s most expensive iPhone has 24 carat gold casing, and more than 600 white and black diamonds, and sapphire glass for the screen. This certainly isn’t available to the public, and to buy one you’d need over 15 million dollars. At this level, it’s not just about buying the fanciest most luxurious items, it’s about getting the rarest and most exclusive things you possibly can. You could get a limited edition pen, that costs $160,000, has 4,810 diamonds inside, and only 8 are made each year. Or if you want to go a lot bigger than that, many billionaires turn to art. Not just rare paintings, but things like this dead shark art piece created by Damien Hurst that sold for $12 million dollars. Or let’s say you like piano? Well, here’s the same crystal piano that was used for the Beijing Olympic Games. It was purchased at auction by a private bidder for a record $3.22 million U.S. dollars! Your mind is the only real limit to what is available. But Our final level, $1 Billion Net Worth and Above. Before we get into what you can do as a billionaire - which is pretty insane - it’s worth noting that the number of billionaires on Forbes' annual rich list has significantly increased in recent years, and there’s now around 2,755 known billionaires. Of course, it’s possible there are more who have generational wealth that simply do a good job of staying very very private. Either way, Forbes estimated these 2,755 billionaires are worth a combined $13.1 trillion, and therefore have more wealth between them than the poorest 5 billion people on earth combined. In fact, Oxfam have argued that the very richest 26 billionaires have more wealth between them than the poorest 50% of the entire world. These statistics are overwhelming, but let’s actually visualise it to put things in perspective. This rice example really highlights that even within this billionaire category, the difference between 1 billion and the kind of wealth Jeff Bezos or Elon Musk has, is insanely vast. I’m a big fan of James Jani’s YouTube channel, and he also did a great job of visualising the difference between these large sums of money in a different way: So, now that we’ve got a better idea of the scale of a billion dollars, let’s look at what you can do with it. Because essentially, you can buy anything. And I do mean, anything. We’re no longer just talking about luxurious items and experiences. Firstly, you can buy access. You can ask your staff to contact almost anyone on the planet and get a call back. It’s amazing the level of access and respect a billion can get you, where within an hour you could ask your people to get in touch with someone, and they’d be able to do it. It’s not just celebrities either, it works the same with powerful people within politics too - especially because the billionaire may well be a donor to their political party. And so, if you’re a billionaire you may occasionally meet with heads-of-states and have conversations with them. Which brings us to the second thing a billion dollars can buy: Influence. As a billionaire, you have many ways to shape public policy and the public debate. This isn’t always done in some kind of evil way, like lobbying for lower taxes for rich people (although that obviously does happen). But because of your wealth and connections, you can absolutely influence big changes in the world, and not just on a political level. Because Another thing you can buy is IMPACT, through philanthropy and charity. Your money can literally change countless lives. The question is, how do you distribute it? Clean water for villages? Fund new hospitals? Try to save a dying species of animal? With money, you can impact almost anything of your choice. But one of the biggest differences with billionaires that is often overlooked, is the ability to essentially buy time. You quite literally don’t have wait for anything. No lines. No sitting around. Just think about it: You’ve got your own private jet, so you just show up at the airport whenever you want, sit down in the plane and 2 minutes later you’re in the sky and can go where you wish. When you arrive, a driver will have been arranged to pick you up and take you wherever you want to go. Your assistant will have already got you the best table at the restaurant you mentioned wanting to eat at. Or maybe your assistant arranged for a celebrity chef to come to one of your vacation homes instead, at whatever time you choose. However… believe it or not, there are some downsides to being this ultra wealthy. Firstly, it’s nearly impossible to have normal emotional relationships at this level. It can become increasingly difficult to have friends and family that love you for who you are. They exist, but it is pretty hard to know which ones they are. Because, your world is likely filled with yes men, and people looking to take advantage at every opportunity. Your trust and connections with people are undoubtedly considerably weakened. Dating also becomes an odd experience. On the one hand, some of the world’s most beautiful and intelligent women are all around you at parties and events, but again, finding someone you have a genuine emotional connection with is a different story… especially because you’re time is so valuable that you’re always busy and unable to invest in making deep connections with new people. And even if you do find someone, the relationship balanced is skewed in a strange way when you have essentially unlimited money - no matter whether you pay for everything or whether your partner makes their own money, either way something feels off. And finally, and most crucially, no level of money can give you everything. Death is the great equalizer, and losing loved ones, and losing your own health and facing your own mortality, are problems that no amount of money can solve. The reddit poster who inspired this video and deserves full credit, talks about how he met Sylvester Stallone at a party, who has obviously had a very successful career. He said he was such a great guy, had huge levels of wealth and luxury, a smart and beautiful wife. And yet, he also had a special needs son who died young. In his words, Nobody has it all. Nobody. In fact, if you were watching this video and feeling poor, I think it’s worth re-thinking. Because in this video we’ve looked at several levels of wealth, but the fact you are watching this means you are nowhere close to bottom of the total wealth pyramid. It’s easy to look up and feel frustrated that some people have so much more than us, but just look down the wealth pyramid for a moment. If you have internet access to watch this, plus some food in the fridge, clothes on your back, a roof over your head and a place to sleep… … then you are richer than 75 per cent of this world. In fact, even if you have $1 to your name right now, you are still richer than the majority of the world who are either in debt, or have absolutely nothing. What this video really highlights is that rich vs poor is so arbitrary. It’s not two groups, there are many many levels. And compared to most of the world, you might well be a lot richer than you think… If you enjoyed this video, please make sure you subscribe and turn on notifications for some future mini-documentaries that are on the way. Thank you for watching to the end, you are a legend, and I’ll see you next time. Cheers!

You disagree with that? >> I disagree with that. I waited a year before I told any friends or family what I was working on. And that's because I didn't want ego to have to get involved too early. When ego gets involved, you end up spending your time defending it and explaining it instead of pursuing it. So, I wanted to invest enough of my personal time in creating this crazy idea that I had. I worked at night and on the weekends on it until I knew I wouldn't turn back when I got doubt. And out of love, your loved ones will bring up things like my family was like, "Sarah, if it's such a good idea, why hasn't anybody already done it? You know, even if this is a good idea, the big guys will knock you off in 6 months and you've spent your life savings on it." Had I heard those things the moment that I had the idea, I would probably still be selling fax machines or something else cuz faxes aren't really around anymore. But it's very true because ideas are the most vulnerable in the moment you have them. And it's also people's instinct to share the idea in the most vulnerable stage because you get an idea and you turn to your co-worker, your husband, your wife, and you say, "Hey, I've got an idea." And out of love, they'll give you a list of like, "Hey, maybe you should consider this." And I didn't want that to happen. - Why Rich People Use The Internet Differently: Kind: captions Language: en It's the same thing as the Facebook or the same thing as moving off of Tinder. There's a certain elite group clustering in a certain area and when Claude becomes big enough, people are just going to hop to another thing. Social media platforms like Instagram are often framed as universal spaces where everyone can participate equally. But the ways people use and express themselves on these platforms are not equal. Differing user behavior, consumption patterns, and aesthetics often reflect deeper social and economic differences. Online trends like lowclass Instagram reveal how aesthetics, posting styles, and digital habits are inextricably linked to perceptions of class. Today, I'm joined by Adam Alexa, an etmologist and cultural commentator who has been covering these subtle internet divides. We're going to be breaking down the hallmarks of lowclass Instagram and discussing how these differences are not only about taste, but also about access to technology, cultural norms, and platform design. Hi Adam, welcome to Power User. >> Hi, excited to be back. >> So, there's this discussion that's been taking place on Instagram about lowclass Instagram. And I I thought of it a lot because Kyle Chica posted something last year about kind of like billionaire Instagram behavior. It was basically like the richest person you know has an Instagram that's private and has like 200 followers. And I think this is kind of weirdly so true. And that's what sort of woke me up to this idea of like there seems to be this way that like rich people use the internet verse the rest of us. And I'm curious kind of like how this discussion made its way to you. And how would you define this discussion around lowclass Instagram? Yeah, I think it's all something we've kind of known for a while, but recently there have been these internet memes and discussions happening about high income versus low-income Instagram. And there are certain posting behaviors that are associated with class that we could get conditioned into acting through. >> Yeah. And I want to start off also by saying like this is us kind of like describing these like trends that correlate with socioeconomic classes maybe or different like marginalized groups and the ways that they use it. Like these honestly transcend America. Like it's it's interesting to kind of like I think think about kind of like what that says and what these maybe like what these judgments that the public is making against these behaviors online where they stem from and kind of what they reveal about our culture. >> Right? I think it's something very important to examine why we think something is high class or low class and to understand that our elitism actually bleeds into everything including the online which we tend to think of as separate but it never really is. It's always a part of the whole. One thing that I feel like is sort of universally considered lowclass Instagram is the use of filters and sort of heavily filtered photos, heavily filtered videos. There was the joke about sort of like the Rio di Janeiro filter being put on everything as kind of like a meme. But I noticed this sometimes even just going into the comments and trying to sort of judge who someone is. If I click on their profile and they've got a lot of heavily filtered photos, I'm like this person I don't know like they seem maybe like they are lower class or that they are not as like maybe adept at using the internet. It feels almost kind of boomercoded as well. >> Yeah. Elitism which is tied to intellectualism is conditioned sociologically into behaving certain ways. And when you are in the group of elites, whether or not you've consciously thought about it, you will replicate behaviors that other elites are doing. One of those behaviors is to have a deep understanding of the platform and the medium and signal in an aesthetic way that coheres with that medium. So, some people may have thought about it explicitly and some people may have not, but nevertheless, there is this group that emerges of people who are presenting through the social media platform aesthetic in a way that kind of both is cohesive to identity and to the platform itself. And so, okay, what what I mean by that is we already know we're pretending online. If you put on a filter that's only like going beyond that to pretend like we are trying to look better. This is true. By posting online you already presenting a face of yourself that you want people to like you. The filter overd does it. So to elite scrutinizers it comes across as you're trying too hard to get people to like you. >> I think it's interesting because like we are all ultimately pretending online. And I think actually the way that a lot of people experience wealth through Instagram is actually people engaging in quote unquote like lowclass behavior where they're like posting their Ferraris or they're posting whatever. - A better way to tax the rich: Kind: captions Language: en That includes cars, houses, yachts, jewelry — and even cold, hard cash. It also includes things we owe other people, like student loans. So let's take a quick tour of American household wealth. We can start over here, where we see people who owe way more than they have. As we move further right, we can see — oh, that's me. And there's my doctor, who owns an expensive home. And there's former heavyweight boxer Mike Tyson! But when we get to the very richest people in the world — oh hey, it's Beyoncé — we can zoom out so far that this chart looks like a stick and we still can't see the top of their wealth. Because these people are so much wealthier than the rest of us. In other words, this chart doesn't just show It shows the staggering wealth inequality we have in America. But recently, politicians have been talking about an idea that's never been done before in America: In January, Senator Elizabeth Warren proposed something called a "wealth tax." This was a big deal. Because this doesn’t typically get taxed. Most of the taxes we pay only happen when money changes hands — like when we earn money or spend money. But according to Warren, here's the problem with that. This bar represents my wealth. And this is my friend's wealth. I think he owns a yacht. But because both of us have the same job and earn the same amount of money, our income gets taxed about the same. "Two people who may have the same income, but are in wildly different economic circumstances." So to fix it, Warren wants to tax the actual wealth people have, but only for the very, very rich. Here's her proposal: All the wealth above this line — 50 million dollars — would be taxed at 2 percent each year. All the wealth above this line — 1 billion dollars — would be taxed at 3 percent. So if you have 40 million dollars, you'd pay nothing. If you have 60 million dollars, you'd pay nothing on your first 50 million, but 2% on the 10 million after that, or $200 thousand. And if you have 2 billion dollars, you'd pay about $49 million. So what would happen if we imposed Senator Warren's tax? Only the richest 0.05 percent of Americans would pay this tax. But in 2016, the government could have raised about 200 billion dollars from it. That could pay for a plan that makes public college tuition free — three times over. It could pay for all federal food assistance programs, like food stamps, two times over. It could get close to cutting child poverty by 60 percent. These are massive programs that would help millions of people. And what makes that possible is the huge amount of wealth stuck here, at the very, very top with the ultra rich. A wealth tax hones in on that. We've started this paid membership program called Vox Video Lab. When you sign up, you support our journalism but you also get access to some really cool exclusive content, kind of behind the scenes of what we work on. So you can join at vox.com/join. - What I Saw as a Fake Billionaire | Fakes, Frauds and Scammers: Kind: captions Language: en-US As a real estate agent, you never really know who you’re dealing with. She could have been an actual billionaire. She could have been a scam artist, and I would never know the difference. This project is kind of like a Robin Hood project. I pretended to be a billionaire so I can get into the most exclusive apartments in New York. We're not just selling a building. The reason why people are living here is because of the lifestyle that we’re offering. Eighty-five million. Ten million. Sixteen million. These people are the 1 percent of the 1 percent. I think it’s really obscene that these buildings exist. Your maid would be getting ready with dinner while you’re just having one of the finest champagnes in a soaking tub with your husband. Imagine waking up every morning to these views. Gabriella... I really see you living here. [FAKE BILLIONAIRE] The cheapest property I’ve been to was 10 million, and the most expensive was 85 million. They manipulate the cities to the disadvantage of everyone else in the cities. My name is Andi Schmied, and in 2016, I spent three months in New York at an artist residency program. Basically, this whole idea started with the biggest cliché that I went up to the Empire State Building, and I saw the views. And I basically realized that there are lots of buildings that are as tall or even taller than the Empire State Building. And I just basically was really curious about what the view from those buildings looked like. That’s when I started to work on my book and my project. I chose 25 buildings, high, which were luxury towers. Ninety-three stories above the city, this is what $81 million gets you: a full penthouse, 8,400 square feet with floor to ceiling views of a metropolis. My goal was really to show those views that are considered to be the best views you can privately own in New York. First, I really just wanted to photograph the views for the book. As I was going deeper and deeper in this world, it became more and more obvious that it’s something totally bizarre and crazy. I was laying in bed, and really I was just thinking, “How could I get in those buildings?” I remember I talked about the project to the curator who was working at the residency, and she told me, “No way they would ever let you in without a credit check. The only way for that is to pretend to be a billionaire who is searching for an apartment.” From the moment that I started to work on the project, this was pretty much the only thing I could think about: write it down, check the address, go home, check if there’s any real estate available, call the agency, and usually, next day, go and see it and take a picture of the view. And so really, for three months, I was living in between this Gabriella, which was my billionaire persona, and myself, back and forth on the A line from Brooklyn to Manhattan. My name is Daniel Rosenstein. I've worked in real estate for four years. I met Andi two years ago when she was infiltrating New York’s most exclusive properties. I was doing Airbnb in my apartment in the Upper West Side. And first, I remember that he asked me, “What are you doing in New York?” And then he said he’s a real estate agent. I was actually a bit hesitant to tell him what I came here for. She told me that she was making a book. So I think at first, I was like, “Oh, is she mocking our profession? I’m just a pawn in your project here.” And then the more she was talking about it, the more I saw her point of view, which is almost nobody has access to these apartments. You have to go through a doorman. You have to reach out to a real estate agent. It’s not a normal real estate market, where you can show up to an open house. She asked a few questions, I think how to navigate getting into buildings. I told her to use the term, “We’re looking for a pied-à-terre or second home in the city.” A lot of really wealthy people look for second, third, fourth, fifth homes, and she at that point had essentially created her whole persona. Gabriella is my actual middle name because, at the beginning, I didn’t know if they would check my passport ever. And actually, at some of the properties, they did check it. And I remember, back then, I got really obsessed by many of these small details— that I need to do my nails, and I need to have proper shoes, proper bag. In order to do this project, I needed to bring my camera and photograph the view. This made me obviously very unusual in the eyes of the agents. But I also realized that the stranger I act, the more convincing it is that I’m actually a billionaire, and I just don’t give a shit. I was really, really nervous at this very first viewing. The agent started to ask me if I have a chef, if I have a nanny, if we have a private chauffeur. So I just randomly answered yes and no. And all of these answers kind of became part of what Gabriella was. Tell your husband every morning that this is really where you want to be. There are a few different types of agents. There are the ones who are very natural and just showing you, here is the kitchen, here is the bathroom, this is the price. And there is the complete other end of the spectrum. You will feel, Gabriella, it is like going on a journey from the sidewalks of 19th-century, old New York to the sidewalks of Florence. I actually might just prefer the 21st century-- Imagine your daughter running around saying words in Hungarian. It was really hard to keep a straight face in those viewings. I mean, it was really bizarre what they were telling me. Sit down, Gabriella. It really is a moment for you. Just tell your husband. He loves you, and he wants you to be happy. I’m sure he will not say no to you if you let him know this is really your first choice. Because this ultra-luxury segment of the real estate is actually now not really going that well, the newer the development, the crazier the amenities they have because there’s really this huge competition. One of the things that became an absolute standard for these buildings is the golf simulator room, Michelin-starred private restaurant in the building, theater, swimming pool, obviously. Equinox gym. They have a children’s playroom, a billiards room, a conference room, and everything in between. In one of the penthouses, it was that they would give you a yacht as a present when you buy the apartment. A yacht. So you mentioned the number of people who are actually living in poverty, according to the federal poverty numbers. But we all know, living in New York, it’s a whole different ballgame. The average rent across the city has returned to nearly pre-pandemic levels. We built fewer homes in the 2010s than in any decade going back to the 1960s. I think the way poverty is usually portrayed is from the bottom. So, talk about homelessness, or you talk about people who cannot afford housing. I think when you see it from this very top of the sphere, it somehow is also very telling. The buildings that you can see from every single corner of the city are these ultra-luxury real estate, which are not accessible for anyone, really. Apartments and buildings are standing 60 to 70 percent empty. Commercial and residential wonderland featuring some of the city’s tallest buildings and most expensive restaurants and condos. If you are looking to call Steinway Tower home, studios start at $7.7 million. Listen, not to be not sympathetic to the homeless, but if you live in One57, you’re paying millions of dollars to live there. Do you want a homeless shelter in your building? I don’t think so. We are not building enough housing for everybody who needs a place to live. Here’s what it’s like living in the smallest apartment in New York. Wherever your expectations are, lower them. So these buildings are kind of really soulless, in a sense. They are super standardized. The trends that I see in these properties are a lot of the same finishes and materials used. Some of the floor plans are the exact same. I think it’s a really challenging task to create something different when you’re working with essentially the same floor plan, which is a tall square or a rectangle in the sky. Oh, wow. Who was the interior designer? Deborah Berke. There is no one better in design at the moment. So you have always some very special type of marble in the apartment that the agents would always highlight. The marble is Siberian, which is now the best on the market. Then you would have the oak floor, which is always the best type of oak floor. And there’s really nothing that would make any of these buildings stand out, to be honest. But it’s not really surprising when you realize that it’s solely a form of investment, most of the cases. So it’s really not about living, and it’s not about having a soul. And it shows. It’s really obvious when you are there. One of the most striking things was how agents are trying to always go out of their way to tell me that no one else lives in this building, that I'm not going to have any neighbors. A lot of these Billionaires’ Row towers are investments for overseas and local people, so they’ll rent them out or they’ll just own them, have them sit empty for a number of years and resell them. It’s a safe investment. It holds value over time. But what that translates to is buildings sitting empty. There’s a trickle-down effect, so once the hyper-expensive properties become even more expensive, it raises the value of everything else beneath it. I get very often the comment that this project is kind of like a Robin Hood project, in the sense that I’m getting the views which other people or most people cannot see to the public. And it’s like being so high up, it’s cool. But then it didn’t feel good to be there, in a way. One of the very big problems of these buildings, they are casting sometimes hundreds and hundreds of meters of shadows, for example, over Central Park or over other people’s up until then sunny living rooms, or just over the streets. And so, somehow, these buildings that are not even lived in are taking away such natural things from the rest of the city as sunlight. There’s a lot of money in New York. And I think there will always continue to be a lot of money in New York. I do think it’s going to be more segregated. What I’ve learned from this project is really just how obscene differences are in society, I guess, and how this can translate into this very literal physical form in the city and how that is impacting everything around it.

- How Rich People Hide Their Money: Kind: captions Language: en - The US Department of Treasury estimates that the ultra wealthy in America are skipping out on more than $160 billion worth of taxes every year, - And we know that number is impossible to imagine. So compare that to the roughly 48 billion that Penn Wharton budget model estimates the US will be spending each year for the next decade on student debt relief. An extra 160 billion could go a long way. - So how did a rich get away with it? Well, most of us are taxed on our income. We go to work, we earn a paycheck. A percentage of each paycheck goes back to the government to spend on programs and infrastructure, and we use the rest to buy groceries, pay bills, and score Beyoncé tickets. - The ultra wealthy though, don't earn much of their income from a paycheck. Instead, they grow their wealth through investments, and it's only when they sell those investments that they actually owe taxes on them. - That is, if they don't find some clever loopholes first. (playful music) - One way that the rich hide their money is by literally hiding it. Secret Swiss bank accounts or shell companies in the Cayman Islands sound like the stuff of heist movies, but some wealthy people do use foreign accounts to shield their money from the IRS's irises. - These tax havens are attractive places to stash cash and maybe not tell the US government that it's there. First, a wealthy person or maybe their financial advisor will find a country that collects low or no taxes on foreign funds stored there. But foreign banks are still supposed to report to the US on foreign accounts held by individual US citizens. So instead of putting an account in their own name, a rich person could form a fake company that doesn't really do anything and open accounts in that company's name. Popular tax haven countries like Panama and Luxembourg, will look the other way, while these anonymous company owners move money in and out of their accounts without reporting it to the IRS, - But like we all learn from the Wolf of Wall Street, this is pretty illegal. US law requires that citizens report all of their assets, even if they're stored abroad. So if the IRS catches you keeping secret foreign investments, you could get in pretty big trouble. - Lucky for the rich, there are totally legal tax havens right here in the US. In addition to the taxes we pay to the federal government, the states, and even cities we live in, can collect taxes too. So a state might collect income taxes on the money we earn, capital gains taxes on the money we make from investments, sales taxes on things we buy and property taxes on the value of our homes. But all states decide this mix a little differently. - Some states, like Washington, don't collect an income tax at all, and I'm sure it's just a coincidence that billionaires like Bill Gates and Jeff Bezos have lived there for decades and eight states, including Nevada, Texas, and Florida, don't tax capital gains either. Wealthy people who want to pay the least amount of taxes may choose to live in a state that doesn't tax their income and investments. - In 2021, Washington State passed a law that allowed them to collect capital gains tax for the first time. Now, anyone who makes more than $250,000 selling investments, would owe the state 7% of anything they earn after that first 250 K. - So what did Jeff Bezos, who makes billions a year selling Amazon stock, do? He moved to Florida, where he'll keep $610 million in his pocket this year by avoiding Washington's tax on his investment gains. - For people who don't wanna play where in the world is Carmen San Diego with their dollars, there's another strategy that wealthy people use called buy, borrow die. Remember that we don't get taxed on our investments as they grow. It's only when we sell them that we get taxed, and that's why the ultra wealthy just don't sell. Instead, when they need to buy a new spaceship, they can take out loans using their investments as collateral. - Back in 2020 when we knew Elon Musk mostly as that eccentric electric car guy, he held $548 million in personal loans. That's the money he actually used to pay for utility bills, shop for groceries, or most likely pay other people to do those things for him, while the rest of his wealth was tied up in Tesla stock. - He did this again in 2022, securing $13 billion in loans that he used to buy Twitter. But because these billions are in loans, not income, Musk and other super rich investors don't have to pay taxes on that cash. - So the rich buy investments and let them grow, then borrow against those investments to fund their lifestyles. But what's the die part? - If you buy stock for $10, hold onto it for decades until it grows to be worth a thousand dollars, and then die without ever selling it, whoever you choose to inherit your estate can sell that investment, - But thanks to a tax rule called the step-up in basis, your heir doesn't owe taxes on the full $990 that your investments grew while you were alive. Instead, $1,000 becomes the new starting value. And if your heir later sells the stock for $1,200, she only pays taxes on the $200 it grew after you died. - By using the buy borrow die strategy, nobody has to pay taxes on all the capital gains that happen during your lifetime at all, which is how some of the wealthiest people in the country managed to amass billions while barely paying taxes on any of it. - But sometimes the rich do need to turn their mega investments into cash. They have some creative ways to hide that money too. Whenever they make money selling an investment that grew, the rich might also look for losses. That might mean selling off investments that are losing money at the same time. - Any money you lost gets subtracted from what you gained, and you only pay taxes on what is left over. If you have a lot of losses in one year, you can even wait to count those losses against income you have in the future. This strategy called tax loss harvesting helps rich people sell their investments at a profit, while making it look like they didn't really walk away with much. - Wealthy people can also find losses by structuring their fancy hobbies as businesses, whether it's training race horses, owning a sports team, developing luxury real estate, or buying a social media platform. The ultra wealthy can afford to start or buy businesses that they don't actually expect to live off of. So when those businesses lose money, it can actually be a good thing. - In the 1980s and nineties, when Donald Trump was just a wealthy real estate developer, he reported over a billion dollars of losses from his hotels, casinos, and apartment buildings in a single decade. - His tax returns dating back to 1985, showed that those business losses got subtracted from his income and investment gains, lowering the total he had to pay taxes on, sometimes to zero, all while getting to live in a gold-plated penthouse apartment. - ProPublica found that between 2014 and 2018, the 25 richest Americans paid only 3.4% in taxes on their total wealth increase. Meanwhile, the median American household pays about 14% of their total income. While this may seem crazy, strategies like buy, borrow, die, and tax loss harvesting are perfectly legal ways to hide your wealth, - There have been attempts to close some of these tax loopholes. In 2022, president Biden proposed that Americans with over $100 million in wealth would have to pay a 20% tax on their investment growth, even before those investments are sold. - [Julia] And senators like Bernie Sanders and Elizabeth Warren introduced a bill in 2021 that would've eliminated these step up in basis rule that allows people to inherit a lifetime of gains tax free. - Still, none of these plans have been enacted into law. Legislators are still debating how to close these tax loopholes or whether to close them at all, but the ultra wealthy don't seem too worried because the longer it takes us to figure it out, the more their hidden wealth continues to grow. - And that's our two cents. - [Katie] Thanks to our patrons for keeping Two Cents financially healthy. Click the link in the description to become a Two Cents patron. (soft music)

  2. @ScottEDsntKnow [1 likes]: 7:13 Almost got it past me, very sneaky. Listen closely and you will notice that she is actually comparing two completely different things, but presenting them as if they were an apples to apples comparison. In regards to the 25 richest Americans the 3.4% stat represents the % in taxes they paid on their “Overall Wealth Increase”, which she then decivingly compares this to what the  Median Household pays on their “Total Income” over the same time period. Strategically she does not also use their “Total wealth increase” as well over the same timeframe. Point being total income involves (earned income) from active work and (unearned income) ie- Profit from: selling investments, rental income, dividends, asset sales, etc. Total Wealth increase involves Earned income, unearned income. And pivotally- the increase in value of all of their held assets. ie-Stock share price appreciation, home value growth, gold/silver value increase etc. TLDR -Taxes paid in relation to Total Wealth Increase vs Total income. Conveniently paints a lopsided data illustration to drive s point of “Rich aren’t paying their share”

- Why Is America So Rich? | 5 Minute Video: Kind: captions Language: en The United States is the world’s most prosperous economy. It’s been that way for so long -- over a hundred years -- that we take it for granted. But how did it happen? There are many answers, of course. One is that the United States values the free market over government control of the economy. But here’s a point that is seldom made: It didn’t begin that way. Before the country placed its trust in the free market, it trusted the government to make important business decisions. Or to put it another way, only after the government failed repeatedly to promote economic growth and only after private enterprise succeeded where the government failed, did the United States start to develop a world beating economy. Let’s look at three telling examples: In 1808 John Jacob Astor formed the American Fur Company and marketed American furs around the world. Europeans adored beaver hats for their peerless warmth and durability. Astor gave them what they wanted. Instead of leaving the fur business to capable entrepreneurs like Astor, the government decided it wanted to be in on the action. So, it subsidized its own fur company run by a self-promoting government official named Thomas McKenney. McKenney should have won the competition. After all, he had the federal government backing him. But while Astor employed hundreds of people and still made a tidy profit, McKenney’s company lost money every year. Finally, Congress in 1822, came to its senses and ended the subsidies for McKenney and his associates. A similar situation developed in the 1840’s around the telegraph. The telegraph was the first step toward the instant communication we have today. Invented by Samuel Morse, the telegraph transmitted sound – as dots and dashes representing letters of the alphabet. Morse, more of an idealist than businessman, agreed to let the government own and operate the telegraph “in the national interest.” But the government steadily lost money each month it operated the telegraph. During 1845, expenditures for the telegraph exceeded revenue by six-to-one and sometimes by ten-to-one. Seeing no value in the invention, Congress turned the money-loser over to private enterprise. In the hands of entrepreneurs, the business took off. Telegraph promoters showed the press how it could instantly report stories occurring hundreds of miles away. Bankers, stock brokers and insurance companies saw how they could instantly monitor investments near and far. As the quality of service improved, telegraph lines were strung across the country – from 40 miles of wire in 1846 to 23,000 miles in 1852. By the 1860s, the U.S. had a transcontinental telegraph wire. And by the end of that decade entrepreneurs had strung a telegraph cable across the Atlantic Ocean. Why didn’t the US government profitably use what Morse had invented? Part of the answer is that the incentives for bureaucrats differ sharply from those of entrepreneurs. When government operated the telegraph, Washington bureaucrats received no profits from the messages they sent, and the cash they lost was the taxpayers’, not their own. So government officials had no incentive to improve service, to find new customers, or to expand to more cities. But entrepreneurs like Ezra Cornell, the founder of Western Union, did. Cheaper, better service meant more customers and more profits. Just fifteen years after Congress privatized the telegraph, both the costs of construction and the rates for service linking the major cities were as little as one-tenth of the original rates established by Washington. In the steamship business, we see the story repeated yet again. During the 1840s, regular steamship travel began between New York and England. The government placed its bets on ship owner Edward Collins, a man more skilled at political lobbying than at business. While Congress funded Collins, Cornelius Vanderbilt started his own steamship company. Vanderbilt cut the costs of travel, filled his ships with eager passengers, and built a fabulously successful business, soon leaving Collins in his wake. Collins failed because he didn’t feel a need to improve, or even provide safe and regular service (for example, two of his four ships sank, killing hundreds of passengers). If he lost money, there was always another politician to appeal to. Vanderbilt, in contrast, had to serve his customers or he would have lost his company. You’d think we would have learned our lesson by now: economic prosperity comes from free enterprise, not from government subsidies. But it's a lesson we have to learn every generation. I’m Burton Folsom, Professor of History at Hillsdale College, for Prager University.

- Germany: The discreet lives of the super rich | DW Documentary: Kind: captions Language: id Jerman super kaya. Tidak ada negara Eropa lain yang memiliki miliarder sebanyak Jerman. Dan sementara kekayaan mereka bertambah, semakin banyak orang Jerman hidup di bawah garis kemiskinan yang ditetapkan Organisasi untuk Kerjasama Ekonomi dan Pembangunan OECD. Pers sering melaporkan tingginya ketidaksetaraan pendapatan negara itu dan mobilitas sosial yang rendah. Tetapi sedikit yang diketahui tentang orang super kaya. Tentu uang menarik sukses dan sukses menarik uang. Saya percaya itu. Saya telah alami sendiri bagaimana Anda tiba-tiba bisa terhubung dengan orang-orang yang sebelumnya tidak terjangkau. Siapakah orang-orang super kaya Jerman? Bagaimana mereka hidup? Dan bagaimana mereka melihat negara tempat mereka tinggal? Cara orang-orang kaya di Jerman dibicarakan mengganggu saya, karena itu sensasional dan memalukan, dan sama sekali tidak mencerminkan apa yang telah dilakukan orang kaya, dan terus dilakukan, untuk negara ini. Ini menciptakan kesan orang kaya seperti Paman Gober Bebek. Bahwa mereka punya gudang uang tempat mereka berendam dalam koin mereka. Paman Gober ingin lebih, lebih, lebih banyak! Itu bukan dunia saya. Saya suka mendapatkan uang, tapi bukan berenang di dalamnya.
