To fully understand the role tycoons have played in the country's history, you have to return to the 19th century to the pioneers of US capitalism. The Robert Baronss were a group of men who ran the industrialization of the United States. In those days, there weren't many rules constraining what they could do. called the robber barons because of their ruthless behavior. They made their wealth from the new technology of the industrial age. The richest of them all was John D. Rockefeller who made his fortune from oil. John D. Rockefeller was president at the creation of the oil business and he rode that horse to become the biggest richest man in in the world in its time. Rockefeller's uh fortune was bigger than Bezos, bigger than Musk. It was enormous. In today's money, he is the tycoon who was the closest contender to being a trillionaire. In 1915, John D. Rockefeller could have paid off the national debt of the United States and still have been the richest man in the world. But like many US tycoons, Rockefeller started out with nothing. His father was a um a never do well was someone who was always was always hustling but not necessarily doing it in the most um legal of ways. Many billionaires are individuals who grew up on the wrong side of the tracks and that motivation is what leads them to actually become people who transform the system and make a lot of money for themselves in the process. Going from rags to riches is the essence of what's called the American dream. The American dream to me means the the ability to become wealthy. It provides the hope that drives people to work hard. You work your way up from nothing. You don't come from money. You don't come from family. And the idea that if you hustle enough, you can do anything. And one family from Arkansas defined the American dream. The Waltons are the richest family on Earth thanks to their stake in the retail chain Walmart. Founder Sam Walton's three surviving children have consistently been in the top 20 of Forb's rich list. And Alice Walton, his youngest child, is worth $65 billion alone. Alice Walton is the richest woman in the United States. She inherited most of her money, but she also invested her money. She remains involved in in many of the Walmart businesses. Sam Walton became the richest man in America in 1985, a position he held for 4 years. Sam Walton is the true rags to rich's story. Mostly it's a matter of of believing in our people, working with our people and really trying to do everything we can to for their best interest and they in turn come back and take care of our customers and take care of ourselves. Walmart now has around 11,000 stores worldwide and is the largest retailer across the globe. This retail giant started with a small shop in Arkansas and once again he made his fortune by using new technology. He was one of the first people who used computers um to track his inventory. What allowed Walmarts to become so extremely successful was very tight control of inventory. So they would buy it at the last minute, sell it as quickly as possible, keep the goods moving. Once Walmart became a a giant, they were able to push their um suppliers and force their suppliers to sell at at you know lower prices. He used glossy commercials to attract families. Walmart everyday low prices means everything. Everyday low prices really mean something at Walmart. Every single item in the store every day. Passing all savings on to consumers to make Walmart the cheapest place to shop was always Walton's aim. He also reinvested profits allowing the company to grow quickly. So Sam Walton became a cult figure. People saw Sam Walton as the embodiment of the American dream. And everyone in the company said, "Mr. Walton, you've got to perform. We did it. Now you do it." But when Sam Walton died in 1992, some saw it as a turning point. Walmart became more of a big empty corporation after Sam Walton's passing. Instead of helping their communities, it came to look like Walmart was just making money from their communities. Walmart also attracted criticism for its working practices. So, they tried in a first sense to make themselves more communityfriendly. They also tried to address a number of very damaging exposees about the mistreatment of their labor, the low wages they paid. Aware of this growing criticism, in 2008, Walmart decided to rebrand and give themselves a more caring image. They tried to raise their wages and do things to make Walmart a healthier, happier place for people to work. And they wanted to tell that story. The growth of Walmart keeps the Walton family the richest in the world, but their domination of the retail sector still provokes controversy. Fundamentally, when a Walmart comes to a town, Main Street dies. Who's going to compete with Walmart? The story of Walmart shows that size isn't everything. Tycoons getting too big and too powerful has been the great American dilemma since the time of robber barons. How to balance business success with the greater good. At its height in the early 20th century, Rockefeller's Standard Oil controlled 90% of America's oil business. Monopolies meant that a few people were getting wealthy from it, not the vast majority of Americans. And so they're not living the American dream because companies have gotten too big and too powerful. It's clear that something needs to be done to create some rules of the road. The Sherman Antirust Act back in 1890 allowed the government to dismantle monopolies. This included the break up of Rockefeller's Standard Oil. Governments continued to regulate tycoons throughout the 20th century. But in 1981, a new president decided to change everything and set the tycoons free. I, Ronald Reagan, do solemnly swear that I will faithfully execute the office of president of the United States. So help me God. Reagan thought things were being overregulated and that was stifling um economic opportunity and profitm. In the 1980s, Reagan undertook to repeal many of the limitations on wealth creation and business entrepreneurship in the United States. In this present crisis, government is not the solution to our problem. Government is the problem. Reagan cut taxes and deregulated markets, hoping the wealth created would improve everyone's standard of living. Reconomics did create an updated version of the American dream. Reagan's policies actually increased inequality in the US. However, over 16 million jobs were created and the booming economy made Americans feel good about themselves again. They were ready to buy houses, TVs, fridges, cars, and fast food. Oh, the 80s were great for billionaires. One of the truly great eras of prosperity in American history. It was a vision that Americans enthusiastically embraced. Reagan's policies also paved the way for a new type of tycoon. One who didn't actually make anything. Instead, he made his fortune by investing in other people's companies. Warren Buffett is an example of this new breed of tycoon who becomes immensely wealthy not by building things but by investing in things. By buying stakes in undervalued companies, Buffett for a time became the richest man in the world. Buffett is very good at reading kind of what sectors of this new market, this service economy, postindustrial economy, kind of what's going to grow and what's going to be profitable. Thanks to deregulation and a booming economy, Buffett made his first billions in the 80s. Instead of following the stock market, he studied financial reports from his office in Omaha, looking for undervalued businesses. I've never had a computer in there. I've never had a calculator in there. And uh and I've never had a stock ticker in there. He once spent a billion dollars buying Koch shares, which then quadrupled in value over four years. Warren Buffett epitomizes the 1980s because from his little office in Omaha, Nebraska, he's now able to move capital around without limitations. Before the 80s, it wasn't possible to move money that way. I'm having the time of my life. I mean, I get to do every day exactly what I like to do with people who I love and who seem to like me pretty well. And and it doesn't get any better than that. He now has thousands of followers for whom he invests money. They call him the Oracle of Omaha. Despite his huge wealth, his lifestyle remains modest in the extreme. Warren Buffett is interesting because he kind of bursts onto the scene at a time when the images of wealth are often flashy and, you know, fur coats dripping with jewels and and Warren Buffett's not that at all. He is a folksy Midwesterner from Nebraska. um lives, you know, not someone who flashes his wealth. Buffett still lives in the same house he bought more than 60 years ago. How would I improve my life by having 10 houses around the globe? I mean, if I'd wanted to become, you know, a superintendent of housing or something of the sort, you know, I could have gone into that as a profession, but I do not want to manage 10 houses, and I really don't even want somebody else doing it for me. And I don't know why the hell I'd be happier. Nothing really changed in the Buffett household as Warren Buffett got richer. his kids found out that they were wealthy because um they read about him in Forbes magazine. Now, as well as advocating for higher taxes on the rich, he's also sworn to give away most of his fortune before he dies. The 1980s was also the beginning of a new technological revolution, the digital age. It saw the birth of the tech tycoon. Just as in the 19th century, entirely new industries and sectors grew and you have the arrival of the commercially available personal computer. Computers make possible things that before were not possible and that includes the accumulation of enormous fortunes. Bill Gates saw the boom in home computers, but his genius was not to build them, but to write the software. Microsoft is the second most valuable company in the world and is worth around $2 trillion. Gates understood how technology would change our lives, foreseeing the impact of smartphones before most of us were even using a computer. - Where America's Richest Billionaires Live: The Top States for Extreme Wealth In 2025: Kind: captions Language: en Billionaires from all over America made the annual Forbes 400 ranking of the country's richest people in 2025. But the ultra wealthy aren't spread evenly across the US with more than half of the list living in the four most populous states, which account for nearly 2/3 of the record $6.6 trillion in total wealth on this year's ranking. Here are the five states with the most billionaires on the 2025 Forbes 400. Coming in at number five is Illinois. Home to 16 Forbes 400 members with a collective worth of $132.8 billion. One of the wealthiest people in the state also happens to run it. JB Pritsker with a net worth of $3.9 An heir to the Hyatt Hotel fortune. Pritskar has served as governor since 2018. The richest person of all though is Lucas Walton. The 38-year-old Walmart Air has a net worth of $39.8 billion. Next up is Texas with 43 Forbes 400 members having a collective worth of $1 trillion. After spending the first few months of 2025 in Washington DC steering President Trump's Doge initiative, Elon Musk is back home in the Lonear State and has a net worth of $428 billion. The Tesla and SpaceX founder left California for Texas in 2020, shifting Tesla's headquarters to Austin the following year. Since then, he steadily acquired land for SpaceX operations and founded his own town of Starbase near the Rio Grand. Texas is also home to the richest woman in America, Walmart Alice Walton. Florida now ranks third among states with the most billionaires, down from a tie with New York for second last year. It now has 49 Forbes 400 members, collectively worth $716.3 Leading the pack is Amazon founder Jeff Bezos with a net worth of $241 billion, who relocated from Seattle to Miami in 2023. Behind him sits Citadel founder Ken Griffin. Other prominent Florida billionaires include NBA legend Michael Jordan and Only Fans owner Leonid Rinsky. New York at number two remains a magnet for some of the world's most successful business people. It is home to 57 members of the Force for 100, collectively worth $690.9 billion. Roughly 2/3 of the state's billionaires made their fortunes in finance and investments, including the richest resident, Michael Bloomberg, who's worth a staggering $19 billion. Other major names from the Empire State include media mogul Rert Murdoch and fashion icon Ralph Lauren. And the number one state is California, boasting the highest number of Forbes 400 members for 2025 with 85 individuals contributing a combined net worth of $1.7 trillion. California's reputation as a hub for Hollywood talent and tech visionaries holds strong. The state leads a nation in billionaires with 39 of them building their fortunes in technology. Oracle founder Larry Ellison tops the list with 276 billion, overtaking Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg. Other leaders of industry include Nvidia CEO Jensen Wang and Google co-founder Larry Page. Entertainment mogul include George Lucas, Steven Spielberg, and David Geffin, who also call the Golden State home. [Music]

- How The Super Rich Live (Wealth Documentary) | Real Stories: Kind: captions Language: en Rolls-Royce, the epitome of luxury and exclusivity. Only a limited number are produced and each individual unit is made to order. They cost upwards of half a million euros, more than a house, more than the average European earns after 25 years of work. A luxury car that very few can afford, which makes the sales figures even more astonishing. Rolls-Royce has achieved a record profit last year and sold more cars than ever before in its 100-year history, all in the midst of the economic crisis. It seems the luxury industry knows no crisis. The factory at Goodwood in southern England finishes off their wealthy customers' desired car with lots of custom-made extras. The interior of a Rolls-Royce alone requires 450 hours of work to be completed, all by hand. Wood, fine leather, and stainless steel are all used, as well as gold and silver for the Spirit of Ecstasy, the famous winged ornament for the bonnet. And if you have money, then you have a lot more money now. So, that makes a lot of sense. Everything here was blocked off for around three months, just like a high-security prison. everything more than 10 million americans have chosen this way of life but the u.s isn't the only place where these gated communities exist the concept has even been around in france since the 19th - How the World’s Wealthiest People Travel: Kind: captions Language: en There are roughly 295,450 people with at least $30 million. Such individuals, referred to as ultra-high net worth, represent about 0.0037% of the human population, yet they hold about a third, $35 trillion, of the world’s wealth. Therefore, it’s no wonder why a massive, global industry exists, fully centered on supporting the nomadic lifestyles of the world’s wealthiest 295 thousand. As with any socioeconomic class, ultra high net worth individuals are more likely to socialize with other ultra high net worth individuals, yet intriguingly, the number of people earning and spending similarly to you goes down as your income goes up. Therefore, once you get to the top of the income scale, the scarcity of people like you stretches your social networks out to a global scale. Through the years, this led to the creation of the informal but remarkably systematized so-called “Billionaire’s Social Calendar.” The year often starts on St Barts—the traditional New Year’s Eve spot for the wealthiest few—then February might include the Superbowl, and March skiing in Aspen or St Moritz. Spring starts with the Masters Golf Tournament in Augusta, Georgia, while May includes the Cannes Film Festival and the conveniently close Monaco Grand Prix. June’s highlight is the Henley Royal Rowing Regatta in England, July’s is Wimbledon, August’s is the Venice Film Festival, while September often includes a return to Monaco for its yacht show. October’s the Frieze London Art Fair, November might include a trip down under for the Melbourne Cup, and finally, the last major event of the year, before St Barts, of course, is Art Basel Miami Beach. These events are hardly about the events themselves—most wealthy attendees to Monaco’s grand prix hardly care about the racing and its yacht show is far more than a sales event for the vessels—but rather they serve as an exclusive space made available only to wealthy people who want to socialize with other wealthy people. While few go to all, and each adapts the calendar to their preferences, ultra-wealthy individuals have often expressed a feeling of obligation to join the circuit in order to maintain their social standing. Such pressure may have motivated Jeff Bezos, for example, to start his 2022 as he did— on a yacht off St Barts with his partner, Lauren Sanchez. Thanks to publicly-available aircraft transponder data, however, we’re able to see what he did with the rest of his January. Bezos’ pair of G650 jets are too large for St Barts’ short runway, so they waited on neighboring Saint Martin while the billionaire rang in the New Year. Then, on the 1st, N271DV took off for the seven-hour flight to Los Angeles to drop Sanchez off, while the other, N758PB, flew the next day to take Bezos directly home to Seattle. Over the next two weeks, both jets regularly shuttled up and down the west coast between Seattle and Burbank, presumably to take Bezos and Sanchez to visit each other. On January 13th, however, one of the jets flew from Burbank to Maui while the other flew from Seattle and stopped briefly at LAX before continuing on to Maui, presumably carrying Sanchez and Bezos respectively to the billionaire’s new $78 million vacation home on the island, then each jet returned to the mainland four days later. Bezos appeared to visit Sanchez again on the 23rd, then four days later flew directly to Washington DC—Bezos owns the biggest home in the District; the largest newspaper in the city; and his company, Amazon, is constructing its second headquarters across the river in Crystal City, Virginia—so he was likely there for business. But finally, Bezos finished out the month by flying back to Burbank, once again—and he did all of this while threading the needle between highlights on the Billionaire’s social calendar, as just 11 days later he flew down to LA for the Superbowl.&nbsp;&nbsp; Across January, Bezos’ lifestyle called for his G650s to fly 29,000 miles or 46,000 kilometers in total, which is roughly equivalent to flying between Seattle and Sydney four times. To fuel this extent of travel, his jets used about 24,838 gallons or 94,022 liters of fuel, which could otherwise be used to propel the largest passenger aircraft in the world, an A380, across the Atlantic from DC to London with 500 passengers aboard.&nbsp; But Bezos’ travel is indicative of the way that market analysts think ultra-luxury travel is heading. You see, two phenomena are crashing head on into each other to throw the industry into turmoil. First, more people are getting wealthy than ever, as money is becoming increasingly consolidated at the top, and second, merely wealthy people are traveling less frequently than in years past, but spending more than ever. Data indicate that prior to COVID, Americans spent an average of $4,871 on a trip to Greece, but now, it’s all the way up to $6,561. Airline executives have also jubilantly noted that first and business class revenue is remarkably strong, despite the continued weakness of the traditionally lucrative business-travel segment, mostly thanks to leisure travelers opting to pay the premium for more luxurious travel at a greater rate than ever. In New York City, the ultra-luxury hotel market is said to be booming, with all-time high prices and occupancy rates and new super-premium properties opening faster than ever. Summer 2022 saw the opening of the highly-anticipated Aman New York, with the cheapest room starting at $2,600 a night, but more regularly pricing in the three or four thousands. Five years ago, the Aman’s sister property—the Utah desert resort known as Amangiri—started at $1,400 a night, but these days, that’s doubled to $2,800 a night, and even with dynamic pricing charging a hefty premium for the most attractive dates, plenty of nights are regularly sold out, even many months in advance. And of course, for those lucky enough to get a booking at resorts like the Amangiri, they need a way to get there so it’s no wonder that 2021 saw an all-time record-high number of private jet flights—3.3 million globally. This tremendous growth was in large part enabled by the propagation of the on-demand charter business model. Companies like Wheels Up, NetJets, and XO have revolutionized the industry by simplifying one-off charters of private jets, which makes the means of travel far more achievable for the twice-a-year wealthy leisure traveler. These private jets are serviced by fixed-based-operators, which essentially act as private terminals-for-hire, and these are also observing record traffic, leading to more FBOs being developed, which is pushing up prices for airport real-estate and hangars to an all-time-high as well. So, as the industry has grown as large as ever, it’s simultaneously more expensive than ever and more frustrating than ever to get access to a private jet. Essentially, across the ultra-luxury travel segment, there’s a capacity crunch. More people than ever are willing to pay more for a night in a top hotel than most do for a month of rent; or more for a two-hour private flight than most pay for their cars. The pace of industry growth is outmatched by the growth in demand for the segment. The incredible consequence is that the super wealthy have pushed the ultra-wealthy out from their traditional stomping grounds. There simply are not publicly-available travel experiences expensive and exclusive enough for the wealthiest of the wealthiest anymore, so they’re essentially turning to private everything. Yachts fulfill this need well—they allow the ultra-rich to travel on their own terms, bringing the quality of accommodations they expect to wherever they want to go, largely regardless of outside demand. But where they want to go is still largely dictated by that Billionaire’s calendar, so each fall, as the temperatures in the Med drop and the final summer events finish up, a maritime migration begins. Some of the superyachts push off the dock with pared-down crews to begin the lengthy transatlantic passage under their own power, and while most yachts are capable of taking on most ocean crossings, it’s an expensive, impractical, and dangerous endeavor. That’s why these exist—yacht-carrying ships. These ships work year-round connecting the world’s yachting hotspots, but in the fall and spring, it’s all about the transatlantic migration. So, at the end of the Med season, a deluge of vessels descend on yachting hubs like Genoa, Italy and Palma de Mallorca, Spain to load up on one of these vessels—their owners paying up to $100 thousand for the convenience. Upon arrival in the Caribbean, their owners can once again access their yachts, providing convenient access to winter events like Art Basel Miami and New Year’s on St Barts.&nbsp; As with every other segment of the industry, however, the superyacht industry is growing faster than ever, meaning what was previously a refuge from the constraints of land is finding itself increasingly stuck out at sea. Take, for instance, a berth at the Yacht Club de Monaco. First and foremost, it’ll cost you. During the summer season, a night will cost the shortest vessels $275; but if you’d like to dock the same boat during the Monaco Grand Prix, the price skyrockets to over $16,500 a night. To get one of the few spots that can fit the largest 60 or 70 meter vessels, it’ll cost $73,000 a night, but even then, the clubhouse building blocks the view of the track, so to actually see the race, this fee includes access for two to the building itself.&nbsp; But cost is hardly the issue here. Rather, it’s club membership, which requires that you were first sponsored by a member then approved by the admissions committee. Once in, to secure a spot during the Med Season, berth reservations have to be booked months in advance. But for the only event that parallels the Grand Prix in exclusivity, the yacht show, just getting a berth is only half the battle. When showing your superyacht off to other attendees, the best spot to dock is here, across the way, so onlookers can take in the full profile of the vessel rather than just its rear. But getting these spots requires first that the yacht is too long to fit in a traditional spot, and that you, or your captain, has a strong relationship with the event directors—relationships that take years to form.&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; Navigating these busy waters is an expensive pain. Superyacht ownership in general is an expensive Yachts don't actually get someone from one place to the next efficiently at all—on the open ocean they’re generally unpleasantly bumpy rides—and yearly costs generally average around 10% of the boat’s value. Yet, across the board, no matter the length, sales only continue to rise. Anecdotally, the deals cut on the decks of these behemoth boats, and the valuable relationships curated at yacht club bars are said to make the investment worth it. As one new yacht owner told the New Yorker “one deal secured on board will pay it all back many times over.”So, increasingly, the yacht is now perceived as a necessary component of the Billionaire’s social calendar, meaning the same way the wealthiest few are feeling about private aviation and ultra-luxury hotels now extends to the yachting world as well. In response, the ultrawealthy are getting more creative. Over the past five years, just as overall sales and superyacht builds have risen, the number of superyachts over 40 meters being refitted has nearly doubled. More often than not, refitting means a visit to a boat yard for minor alterations. Sometimes, though, these refits are major, which reflects a growing trend: the rise of the expedition yacht. With traditional waters busier than ever and the experiential economy as big as ever, billionaires are rebuilding and buying superyachts capable of taking on more adventurous trips, be it sojourns into the Arctic or lengthy, remote prowls across the Pacific. At the extreme end of this trend is a former Soviet icebreaker now rebuilt as an equally tough and luxurious expedition yacht called Legend. On the other, experiential yachting firms have popped up that create World War II battle simulations in the Pacific for bored kids or set up temporary restaurants on sandbars in the middle of the Maldives.&nbsp; The trend toward exploration and expedition isn’t just a superyacht thing either. At the same time that increasingly numerous millionaires are pushing the ultra wealthy away from their old stops, the interest in and social value of unique experiences is pulling billionaires to services offering one-of-a-kind adventures—from up-close gorilla safaris in Rwanda, to luxurious overland treks to the south pole. These luxpeditions, as they’re called, are individually curated by companies like Brown and Hudson to push their guests through Bear Grylls-like experiences in frigid winter conditions before they escape to tents with heated floors and Michelin star-worthy food waiting on them. Their goal is that the trip, in their words, is seared into the participant’s memory. And if the extreme outdoors isn’t of interest, Brown and Hudson offer the opportunity to travel like James Bond, to live a few days like someone else entirely, or go off on a globe-trotting Grand Tour. For the ultra-wealthy, the options are really endless. As long as it’s legal, said one representative of a referral-only travel service, they’ll do anything for a client. Bespoke traveling for the billionaire class is booming, and as long as the traveler gets a good story out of the experience or the ability to brag about visiting the Salar Uyani salt desert in Bolivia or the most rural reaches of Sudan before any of their friends, they’ll keep paying well into the hundreds of thousands for it. But of course, the true top of the market is what we don’t know about yet. Increasingly, wealthy people are traveling to be seen—either in person or on social media. In response, ultra wealthy people are increasingly traveling to disappear—as reflected in the record sales of secluded $30, $40, and $50 million vacation homes in places like Hawaii, Colorado, and the Caribbean. We don’t have a great sense of the ins and outs of the most expensive travel experiences, because the secrecy is part of the premium itself. By the time we do, the top of the wealth pyramid will have moved onto something else—always staying one step ahead of the merely wealthy.&nbsp; If you want to learn more about the yachting world, and specifically how builders are managing to design ever larger and more luxurious yachts, I’d recommend checking out this episode of the show Ultimate Processes on Curiosity Stream. Tens of thousands of Wendover viewers are already subscribed to CuriosityStream, but for those of you who aren’t yet, the good news is that it’s actually a really good deal when signing up through our link, because that also gets you access to Nebula for free. Nebula is a streaming site founded by myself and a bunch of the other creators that Wendover viewers likely already watch, and as a creator owned and operated platform, it was designed from the ground up to be the best home to the stuff we make. For example, by losing the ad-supported model, we’re able to make videos on more niche subjects that we know would appeal to our core audience, but potentially not to the size of audience that would make it profitable on YouTube. We’re also able to make higher-budget stuff, called Nebula Originals—we at Wendover have made all these, each of which is some of our best stuff. You’d probably be blown away by the price if it was just for that, but it’s not, because the cheapest way to get access to Nebula, as I mentioned, is through CuriosityStream. When you all go to CuriosityStream.com/Wendover and sign up for any of their subscriptions, they’ll throw in a Nebula subscription completely for free. That means you get access to all of the exclusive stuff we’ve made at Wendover, Half as Interesting, and Jet Lag: The Game; plus all the exclusive stuff the 150 other Nebula creators have made; plus the entire, massive, top-quality CuriosityStream catalog of non-fiction shows and documentaries. To get all of this, for a year, all you have to do basically is not eat one cheeseburger—or at least one overpriced cheeseburger. That’s because when you head to CuriosityStream.com/Wendover the annual subscription to CuriosityStream is just $14.79 a year right now, and again, that includes a free subscription to Nebula, which helps support us and loads of other independent creators. - Wealth, privilege, and responsibility - The world of the superrich | DW Documentary: Kind: captions Language: en Ah I forgot to say the most important thing during the interview… Wealth means… freedom. That's what really matters. Not having any constraints. And that's the really nice part… I can basically say whatever I want. You can just forget about everything else. What responsibilities do people who have to society, when they have much more than others? We're in Biarritz, France. In-season prices for a double room at the famous Hotel Du Palais start at 800 euros a night – breakfast not included. Georges Kern is the CEO of Swiss luxury watchmaker Breitling. His estimated fortune: 250 million euros. It's not something I really feel in my daily life: being wealthy. The money's just there, but I don't obsess about it. But what does having wealth mean? Do you feel that you have influence? Yes. Why? Because my clients do. I have a lot of rich clients. They're watch aficionados, but for them it's primarily about identifying with a particular image. The products we sell are completely redundant. Nobody needs a watch to tell the time anymore. We manufacture our own demand. It's not about responding to a need. We create that need. Here's one way Georges Kern creates that demand… His company, Breitling, has flown in 90 professional surfers, influencers and celebrities from around the world here to Biarritz. Thank you very much for being here. We’re going to introduce some cool stuff today. Georges Kern is presenting his new watch – starting price: 5,100 euros. After lunch, the guests will be invited to some high-end surfing lessons. In Georges Kern's case: from an 8-time world champion … although he's not keen on being filmed out on the water, in case he takes a tumble. Germany has lost that winning mentality – unfortunately. 'Winning mentality' meaning…? To win! I always want to win – and I don't feel awkward saying that. Even when I was playing ping pong with my 5-year-old son I'd show no mercy – so that he'd learn how to lose too. Because losing makes you want to win. Because who wants to be a loser? Hmm, So how do we go about finding the right images for a film about the societal responsibility of Germany’s rich? How about a trip to the archives? It starts like this: Being rich means… champagne… caviar… luxury sportscars, private jets, pristine beaches and… nice teeth. But joking aside: The media have always provided a clichéd portrayal of the wealthy. The story of the gulf between the rich and poor is as old as money itself. But who is rich in today's Germany? And how much does the average German actually own? Markus Grabka is a wealth researcher. Starting with the lower 50 percent, with the least wealth: Their assets tend to be things tied to basic consumption – a bank account, or maybe a car. Between 50 up to the top tenth of a percent you see this: Some home-owners, maybe they also have private insurances, perhaps savings bonds. And then with the top tenth of a percent: The most important component of their wealth are their business assets. In Germany, the poorer half of the population only owns an estimated 2.3 percent of the country's wealth… while the richest 0.1 percent own around 20 percent. US-based lecturer Katharina Pistor has done extensive research on those who profit the most. For the rich, wealth of course means freedom. And I think they should bear greater responsibility – because of that wealth and the capacity they have to help others. Josef Rick is a building contractor and developer based in Düsseldorf, Germany. His wealth comes from selling luxury apartments – in this case overlooking the city's upscale Königsallee. You can't get more central than this. This is where the buzz is, where the action is. A top location, this is obviously top-drawer. How much per square meter? Here we’re surely around 20 euros per square meter – which is to be expected in this kind of location and with the appropriate features. Living here is a luxury. Renting at 20 euros per square meter is twice the average going rate in major German cities. Josef Rick's business assets are his properties. And his business – building, modernizing and selling real estate – has already earned him a fortune of between 40 and 100 million euros. Back in his villa, he lets us take a look at his tax records from when he was a management consultant in the pre-euro era. It states here in this document that his income was almost one million deutschmarks – now half a million euros. Income tax: zero – thanks to a number of loopholes favoring the already privileged. I had a phase back then where I had good tax advisors helping me to optimize my accounting wherever it was possible. That was the objective, and it worked. And not just for that year. Back then wealthy people were able to bring their tax liability down to zero if they wanted to. And I largely still manage to do that today. It's not difficult. But that doesn't mean he finds such tax breaks fair. Most of the super-wealthy in Germany aren't mega-achievers. They've inherited their wealth. They haven't worked for it – their money works for them! So it's wrong to think of them as titans of industry. That sentiment applies to people who work hard and with dedication – and those who do just that are being heavily taxed in our country, which is just nonsense. We don’t have a meritocracy. We have a society where those that work hard are taxed and those who do little go practically tax-free. It's a theater of the absurd. Privleges that many believe are unearned. Josef Rick wants to see higher tax rates for the rich - even though he himself benefits from the current system. I'm still someone who uses these loopholes – which I find weird. I criticize the system, but because I live in this system, I use its advantages. And there are a lot of them. For example? Using depreciations in value, in real estate, for example. There are countless privileges available to me personally, too. And I don't feel guilty about using them – because it's what the law says. But I do want to talk about whether that law is sensible. Let’s go take a look at what the law actually states. Income from labor is subject to far higher taxation than increases in the value of assets. So people who sell their labor – from a certain level, at least – pay far higher rates than if you sit at home just watching your capital grow. Let’s look at some numbers. In Germany, all annual earned income over 67,000 euros is subject to a rate of 42 percent. Anyone earning over 278,000 pays an even higher rate: 45 percent. Individuals whose income comes from capital gains – so shares or business stakes, and in Germany, dividends and interest too – incur a tax rate of just 25 percent. …And even that's too high in the eyes of many of the super-rich. Moving profits abroad to tax havens has long been the name of the game, to reduce, or even eliminate taxes entirely. The red line is tax evasion. But the attempt to… circumvent or minimize your tax bill is not considered a red line. If I want to set up a firm but don't want certain parts of it to face German-style taxation, then I'll talk to a tax consultant about using legal constructs involving different baskets so-to-speak. One basket is in Germany and subject to German law, another one in a tax haven, and maybe one for my children. And what about children? In Germany, wealthy parents can pass along massive sums tax free. Here’s how the gift tax works in Germany: Let's say a wealthy couple have a baby. They can transfer 800,000 euros to their child's account, tax-free. And that isn’t just a one time thing, they can claim that tax-free allowance again and again, every ten years. So in our example: when that child turns 10 and again when they turn 20. And that adds up to a total of 2.4 million euros that the parents can transfer tax-free. Now let's take an average-earning full-time employee in the west of Germany. Say they save 10 percent of their income: a typical savings rate. That individual will need 670 years to reach that same figure of 2.4 million euros. That's the result of the 'birth lottery'. Simply being born into the 'right family' and circumstances gives you systematic financial advantages over people who don’t win that lottery. To find out more about those who are born into the right families, we went to Lake Constance on the southern border of Germany – and to a place where privilege and inequality are visible at a very early stage in life: in education. This is the elite boarding school Schloss Salem. The list of pupils who have passed through this gateway to a privileged start in life is an illustrious one. Prince Philip, husband to the late Queen Elizabeth the Second, Prince Ernst August of Hanover – as well as countless company owners and executives from the cream of German industry: from food empire Dr. Oetker to carmaker BMW. The school agreed to let us film on the premises, subject to its own terms, and was eager for us to see pupils learning about the topic of responsibility. Jackets off, please – and take your seats. We'll begin with a piece played by Philippa called The Swan from Carnival of the Animals. Thank you. While Germany's state schools suffer from an education crisis, due to chronic underfunding: Here, money is not an issue, thanks to the high tuition fees. The school's motto: building character through courage and trust. Salem is like a family: a place of acceptance where you can be how you are. Both the values and laws of Salem are an integral part of every pupil. These laws include responsibility and commitment. We all bear individual responsibility for this community. …responsibility that many of the pupils already know from home. Max's family runs a big German company. Great that we're able to meet up! Today he’s meeting with Janka Zöller. She advises families looking to register their children at boarding schools. It was her agency that got Max enrolled at Schloss Salem. You’re in your third year now? Yes, the third year is just starting. Salem was the right place for you, right? My mother said there was an agency that would arrange it. We looked at a couple of schools, like Birklehof. Then I looked at Salem and thought: if I'm going anywhere, then here. And what was it that won you over? The campus. And the people who showed me around were really nice. The other Pupils? Yes. The sense of community is really important. Today it's Max's turn to be the guide, together with a schoolmate. That's the girls' dormitory, with a balcony for each room. And here you have the tennis and basketball courts and football pitches. Which sports do you do? Tennis, volleyball and golf. I play hockey and also golf. That's the science building, where we have… All of the natural sciences. Probably my favorite building. We have our own lab, too. There was one at my old school, but this one is far more modern and better equipped. It is pretty exceptional compared to regular schools, wouldn't you say? I would say that it's a kind of privilege to attend Salem. And I'm hugely grateful for the opportunity to be here and be able to further my personal and academic education. It's kind of a shame that this kind of education, plus all the activities, isn’t available to everyone in Germany – and that it's only reserved to a privileged group of people. Max's father runs a well-known German business with a workforce of several hundred people. That's all we’re allowed to say. What we can't see on our little tour are the networks that develop here. If I need to do an internship, I might know someone whose uncle works at that company – so that's definitely a privilege. Having access to this network with the opportunities that come with it will definitely make things easier. Privileges that extend beyond the school's walls. Inside them, the 600 students benefit from small classes and individual tutoring – from fifth grade to their final year, before university. One in five pupils have a scholarship, but most pay fees of around 4,000 euros per month. A first-aid course at the school's own harbor on Lake Constance. There are also student committees, where they again learn about taking responsibility. Germany has several hundred thousand family-run businesses. And Max is among the future heirs who could imagine one day assuming a senior management position. I notice how much I enjoy taking on a kind of leadership role. And I can see myself doing something like that in the future: Even if it's only head of department or something. Family ties and networks provide a leg up – starting with the choice of school. We visit Janka Zöller at her pupil-placement agency. She herself was once a pupil at Salem. We work with at least 30 schools with a range of different characteristics. And of course including Salem, which is a very famous school. Parents who can spare the 50,000 euros in annual fees are among Germany's very top-earners, and are keen to give their children a privileged start for their future. A significant number of boarding school pupils will later join the family business. The boarding schools that we sell to people are a very niche and exclusive product… And basically it's like with any luxury good, in that they're only affordable to a very small group of people. It's not fair, of course. I'd like to see a lot more children having access to this kind of schooling. But the society we live in isn't fair. For me, if I had that money, then before buying a car or an expensive vacation or holiday home, I'd always say: if there's one thing worth investing in, it's my child's education. And just how niche is this product? One year's schooling at Salem costs an average of just under 51,000 euros. The average wage of a full-time employee in Germany is just under 54,000 – before taxes. 8.7 million children currently attend state schools in Germany. The number of pupils studying at boarding and other elite schools with fees of 40,000 euros and up… is 3,100. We met someone who attended Salem without having drawn a winning ticket in the birth lottery. First time, them all call me a bastard Then they tell me: get a proper haircut …a song written by musician Patrice Bart-Williams during his schooldays at Salem. Did he feel he was part of the elite? ‘Educational elite’ is maybe a bit over the top, because it's not about achievement, really. It's more about: can I afford that and do I want it? And of course it's about who can recommend you. It's basically a company of great tradition, with a lot of children from aristocratic backgrounds and big industrial families. So yes: the elite. It does have some highly gifted people – and some who they want to lend a hand to! The Cologne-born musician, who prefers to be known by his artist name: Simply 'Patrice', was admitted to Salem on a scholarship. I'm from a completely different background. I was into skating, and spent a lot of time outside. I didn't have the sheltered upbringing that a lot of the others had. We were really different in terms of life experience. Patrice says he was seen as a difficult student – but nonetheless, the school gave him plenty of encouragement and support. Without that school, I probably wouldn't have had the opportunity to start playing the guitar. They provided students the opportunity to develop their talents. They make sure nobody gets left behind. They take care to ensure that everyone's on board. And how would Patrice have fared without that opportunity? At a state school with big classes and without 24-hour support and supervision – suffering from underfunding. But where would the money for better public schooling come from?! The two words on a lot of people's lips: Germany's wealth tax was suspended in 1997 by then chancellor Helmut Kohl. It's anyone's guess exactly how much Germans own since that move – because nobody's been counting that closely. But estimates suggest that the suspension has cost the state around 380 billion euros in lost revenues. That’s money that’s desperately needed elsewhere – which is why Josef Rick is in favor of a wealth tax. "We have huge problem-areas that lack public funding – everywhere from crumbling schools to run-down local authority buildings, the police and fire departments. It doesn’t matter where you look, the hospitals too! There's a tangible lack of funding everywhere, while we choose not to collect these taxes that we could really use. It's scandalous. So we should eliminate these privileges of the super-rich? It wouldn't do any harm. It would mean people giving a little more who can in any case do that. I'd go even further and say you could generate some pride from making that contribution to the community. But making that change is the responsibility of the government. Period. Would it actually be that easy? Higher taxes and special levies on wealth and inherited assets? Königsallee is Düsseldorf's high-class shopping boulevard, where fabulous wealth is literally on display in every window. Among the jewelers selling their wares here back in the day was a certain René Kern. He was the father of Georges Kern, now head of luxury watchmaker Breitling. When not jet-setting around the world, he's here in the firm's Zurich offices. Does he feel any responsibility? The state takes in so much money – Germany is swimming in it! But their priorities are all wrong when it comes to spending it. They have massive infrastructure problems in Germany, no proper army, no decent school system, and a partly dilapidated healthcare system. The question is how you spend your money. He believes that tax breaks for business owners are fully justified. Those aren't privileges. The state should be glad it has businesspeople who take risks. Those people should be rewarded for their achievements – for their contribution. So: no changes needed? There's evidently a recurring theme here – the notion of business owners being the backbone of society. The people who bear the burden of taking risks – and should therefore be rewarded. But what exactly is being rewarded here? Financial inequality in Germany is more pronounced than in most other countries. 30 years ago, the top 10 percent had 50 times the wealth of the lower 50 percent. It's now double that proportion, with the gap continuing to widen. But why are the rich getting even richer? Because they're doing more? Or is it because their wealth is taxed with privileges – and then also passed onto the next generation with further fiscal advantages? This privileged treatment of business assets in Germany is a conscious political decision. I find the idea of entire estates being passed on to the next generation with tax privileges highly problematic. The sheer volume of assets waiting to be inherited changes the structure of a society. And once that structure is in place, it becomes entrenched. So what changes could be made at the political level? We met with Saskia Esken, Chairperson of Social Democrat party, the SPD. Her party is often in the governing coalition. And in every election campaign, has promised a fairer society. We want to see tax relief for 95 to 97 percent of incomes, with the difference offset by the highest-earners. And inheritance and wealth taxes would enable us to invest more where it's needed – to enable us to get out of the current crisis situation. People in Germany believe that the wealthy should contribute to society not just through charity but also via taxation. Sounds straightforward enough – so why isn't the ruling SPD party putting those demands into practice? Does the chancellor support these goals? Of course Olaf Scholz supports our party's resolutions. But the wealthy have a very strong lobby behind them. The politicians can’t or won’t change the status quo because of lobby groups? How trustworthy is the political system, really? We accompanied musician Patrice as he was preparing for a concert in Hamburg. From modest beginnings to the elite Salem boarding school, and later to German stardom, he’s now much like his school peers who don’t have any financial worries. My motivation was different, as was my purpose in life. It was never about making money and showing it off. I wanted to save the world! I said: I have to do something and do my part. That was my story. The privilege of the rich. Will this remain an issue that politicians only highlight while on the campaign trail? Does German society want to see an honest debate on the topic? At a time when the country is drifting further and further apart. A redistribution of wealth? And perhaps of responsibility… for the financially privileged.

In total, each month, they must spend at least $740, and the same goes for all 500 homeowners. So, each year, McDonald Highlands collects $4.5 million to look after everything. More than 10 million Americans have chosen this way of life. But the US isn't the only place where these gated communities exist. The concept has even been around in France since the 19th century. The first gated communities were created here in the Paris region. The oldest is the Parc de Montretout on Paris's higher land. Napoleon III was its first resident, a century before the Le Pen family. And now, an Oscar-winning actor. In the center of Paris, many industry tycoons and showbiz stars live in the famous Villa Montmorency in the 16th arrondissement, or in the newest gated community, the Villa Grenelle in the 7th arrondissement. But in France, you no longer need to be extremely rich to live behind fences. In the south of France, Marseille holds the record number of gated communities. Attractions like the Old Port, the Basilica, and the Velodrome make it a nice place to live. But the Mediterranean town is also one of serious violence, drug dealing, and is the capital of organized crime. In 40 years, more than 1,500 gated communities have been built in Marseille. In the 9th arrondissement, a residential area is Coin Joli. Although it's not a high-luxury community, 4 years ago, in a general meeting, the co-owners voted for the area to be closed off with eight fences. Now, there are around 100 homes there, which are often stylish, but far from the huge American-style mansions. However, here too, access is forbidden to strangers and to cameras as well. We attempt, nevertheless, to understand why these co-owners want to close off their Robberies, antisocial behavior. This is what must have pushed the residents to close themselves off. Marseille's 9th arrondissement, however, is not known for its crime. So, could there perhaps be other reasons which led them to put up fences? For Julien Dario, a geographical researcher who specializes in gated communities in Marseille, security could in fact just be an excuse. No more need to share parking spaces, calmer, less traffic. In short, improving life's comfort as well as security. But how were the Coin Joli residents able to close off an entire neighborhood? To understand, let's go to the Aix-Marseille University with Julien Dario, the researcher. After 5 years of fieldwork, he knows the town and its gated communities like the back of his hand. >> 1,500 gated communities in Marseille. That's almost 15% of the land. In 50 years, fenced-off areas have multiplied in Marseille, completely legally, for certain areas were already private, built since the '30s by developers at the town's request following a housing emergency. So, decades later, the residents are entitled to do this. However, the procedure is relatively simple, a request to the mayor, which is usually granted, since in France property rights are unrestricted. Lionel Royer-Perreaut is the mayor of the 9th and 10th arrondissement in Marseille. He tried to oppose fencing off the Coin Joli area, the community in the 9th But in reality, there's nothing he can do. And these closures could well structure The private road network must be maintained by the residents from now on. And that's not all. Every time an area is closed, the mayor must deal with it. In spite of these additional costs, residents in Coin Joli have kept the fences. However, not all of them are in favor. During the vote at the general meeting, six homeowners opposed it. Fabienne lives in this house in Coin Joli. Having lived here for 24 years, she has always been against setting up fences. First, a gate at the end of her road. And a few meters further, another completely sealed barrier. Fixed posts, anti-intrusion spikes, and no pedestrian access. A self-segregation that Fabienne does not support at all. The eight fences which surround her community have turned her daily life upside down. The children Fabienne is talking about are students from neighboring areas. They used to use these streets in the mornings and evenings to get to school. But since it's been closed off, access has become much more complicated. Sarah Lefebvre has been living in a house outside the gates and the closed community for 12 years. Five years ago, she found out about the construction of this gate at the end of her road. Since then, her day-to-day life has changed, especially when she walks her son, Sylvain, to school. For 7 years, Sarah cut through the Coin Joli neighborhood to drop her son off at school. Now, she has to go around Coin Joli and follow a rather unexpected route. Across this collapsed wall every morning. Frustrated residents. An impractical, dangerous path which has been left abandoned for years. The other side is the route which goes around the gated community. A pretty long way, but more importantly, it's even more dangerous. A few hours later, at the end of school, she agrees to show us the route she fears. And for good reason, it's a real source of stress for this mother. Sarah has felt excluded since the installment of these fences. They have become a headache, even for certain Coin Joli residents. We met up with Fabienne again inside the gated community. Today, she's invited friends round for a cup of tea at her home. Just four people, but it takes a lot of work. The first guest is her mother, and she has to open the gate for her. Fabienne goes out to greet her mother so as to avoid a tedious procedure. Something that Fabienne does not wish to inflict on her mother. The other guests won't enjoy this preferential treatment. They must use the intercom. And each time Fabienne has to re-explain the whole procedure. And open the gate remotely. The whole process is rather absurd, until finally, she can enjoy tea with her friends. And her guests don't understand this system, either. >> Living hidden away, protected, the gated community model is developing across the world. The trend has even spread to Eastern countries, and the market is proving to be very lucrative. Like in Bulgaria. After 40 years under communism, the country joined the European Union. In its capital, Sofia, are 1.4 million residents and Europe's lowest minimum wage at 235 euros per month. Although the town center definitely doesn't lack charm, the buildings are often worn down and degraded. Ivan, who's 45 years old, is the vice president of the Sofia Municipal Council. Every day, to go to work, he goes through Sofia and the area where he used to live. So, this is where we used to live. In this entrance, at the top, at the at the roof of the building. He and his wife lived right here in the center of town, in a small apartment with a balcony on the top floor of this building. The main issue is that it's a very busy street. So, it was packed with traffic, packed with cars. Now, it looks like a a highway. Noise, traffic jams, and the pavements here are far from the sanitized streets of gated communities. You see all those ugly pavements when you have a small kid or a or a stroller to to carry the baby, this is really not the perfect way you you you expect to have your pavement. It's funny to admit for this municipality official. Ivan has even opted for a change of environment, even if it means turning his back on his town. He has moved to 10 or so kilometers from the center of Sofia into this totally gated community in the American style, with fences, cameras, and security guards, exactly like in the United States. He and his wife, Svoboda, along with their son, Alexander, have bought their dream apartment, 196 m squared and a big garden, which is nearly 300 m squared. Finally, I feel a big relief because first of all, we were in living in much smaller apartments with all these toys and things, we were really, you know, full and packed with many stuff, so now it's really big relief to move in something something big. And it's a well-suited apartment for having friends over. Like this French couple who arrived the previous day. This morning, Ivan and Svoboda offered them a tour of their new paradise, and here is the most important part for them. Up to three secure accesses with barriers and guards at the gate, a security that Ivan appreciates even more now that he is a dad. If you don't have children and you don't have to worry about anything else, having a security with the barriers and live guards that actually watch around, they have cameras at night, they're watching the the perimeter. And that's not all. The residence is overflowing with facilities. Offices, a fitness center with a pool, football pitches, and basketball courts. But also, there is a nursery, a hair salon, a variety of shops and restaurants, and even a chapel, all within a gated community. It's a real man-made village. So, it's like living in a big hotel, in a way. It's like living in a small town in the big town. Yes. That's probably the perfect explanation. A town in which everyone has the same standard of living, a really quite comfortable one. People who have a very busy day life, people who have their profession and careers, most of the people are working in multinational companies, there are people working in the banks, in the insurance business, in the transportation, uh construction. And there's even a VIP living here. The house over there is probably the biggest house. He become very busy with his professional career, and then he become a minister and then a president. This man is Rosen Plevneliev, the president of Bulgaria from 2012 to 2017. Powerful and highly influential neighbors, security, shops. Ivan no longer wants to leave this life of comfort. This weekend, are you going outside of the gated community? >> Yes, in 1 hour we are going out. I don't know. I still have things to do at home. You don't want to leave No. However, to live here and benefit from the security and comfort, they must pay for it. Each month, Ivan and Svoboda pay around 300 euros. That's a huge amount in Bulgaria. It's more than the local minimum wage. And Svoboda is a bit uneasy about broaching the issue. Why we are talking about prices of apartments? We were talking all the time with that we are going to speak about lifestyle, we are going to speak about community. 300 euros in charges, that's twice her mother's pension. Svoboda struggles to accept it. In fact, for Ivan and Svoboda, it is tough to openly accept what they spend for a comfortable life. In this country, which has historically remained quite poor, this man knew how to make a profit from the newly rich. He's the owner of Residential Park. His offices look over the community. Georgi Ranchev is a property developer, a pioneer in gated communities in Bulgaria. However, he began with very different constructions. The Residential Park is just our second largest project. Where we started, more than 20 years ago, is the Business Park Sofia. Business Park, an entire area of offices made up of modern buildings for the time. The first building was the yellow one, actually, and uh from there it started, and then we developed building by building, adding more and more uh office space and more and more clients. As a result, hundreds of businesses moved there on the outskirts of Sofia. 15,000 people work there, but they are far from their homes and the center of town. Uh by talking to all the people and the business guys from the community, I came the idea that they would like to live nearby because of the traffic, because of the jams, because of the parking problems, and we started acquiring plots. This is how the idea for the gated community was born, a small town for the executives of these companies with housing, shops, and all the necessary comforts. Today, Georgi's company still owns the land, with the exception of the houses. So, he manages the residence like a small business. One of his tasks is maintaining the communal spaces. Today, with his associate, he wants to do up the community's shared spaces, beginning with the empty land next to the chapel. Because his success has created rivalry, and for several months, a competitor has been copying Georgi's idea. Just a few meters away from Residential park is a huge building site. Another community with offices, sports centers, restaurants, and even a school. So, behind the scenes, Georgi is preparing a counterattack. He has decided to build a whole new residence just a bit further from the center. Today, the businessman has met with his associates on the new land which he has bought 20 km from Sofia. He'd like to work out the appearance of the final project in more concrete terms. His architects give a tour. It has to be said that at the moment, it's rather difficult to picture a huge residence here on this vast land. It's a bit in the middle of nowhere. Yes, it is. More or less, yes. Oprah Winfrey went private back in 1991 after a fan asked her for a hug while waiting for a flight in Chicago's O'Hare airport. Her newest jet is a Gulfream G700 like the one we toured. Taylor Swift, Tiger Woods, and of course, Elon Musk all own personal luxury aircraft. Warren Buffett named his the indispensable after experiencing the convenience of traveling on his own time. And Donald Trump has owned private aircraft since the 1980s. While other public figures might try to play down their expensive purchase, Trump has flaunted it. His Boeing 757 is emlazed with his name in gold letters. He calls it Trump Force One, even though it's not his presidential plane. The US has two Air Force One planes, but they're more than 30 years old, having been used by six presidents dating back to President George HW Bush. So, they need to be updated. During his first term, Trump signed a nearly $4 billion deal with Boeing for two new jets. >> Well, the plane is totally out of control. It's going to be over $4 billion. It's for Air Force One program, and uh I think it's ridiculous. >> They were due to be delivered in 2024, but production delays have pushed Boeing's deadline to beyond Trump's second term. That's why he made other plans. In May 2025, the US accepted Qatar's gift of a luxury Boeing 747-8 jetliner. The plane is decked out with luxury fabrics, big leather seats, and shiny wood veneer. It has lounges and bedrooms, a private office, and of course, an overthe-top bathroom. probably what happened is President Trump got on the current Air Force One and found it rather, you know, quotidian. And then he saw the Qatari plane, which I'm sure has more, you know, gold lame than, I don't know, your standard disc attack in the 1970s, and that's what I want. But the US Constitution has an imoluments clause that prohibits accepting such an expensive gift from a foreign government unless it's approved by Congress. And this one wasn't. >> There is no such thing as a free palace in the sky. What do foreign countries want when they gift massive amounts of money and other gifts to the president? >> I could be a stupid person say no, we don't want a free, very expensive airplane. Uh, but it was I thought it was a great gesture. >> The White House said the aircraft was worth $400 million and would save taxpayers money, but experts say it's 13 years old and might only be worth a quarter of its original price. >> I don't think the Qataris quite know what to do with this jet. There's no residual market. There's no secondary market. So, I think they probably said, "Well, at least we can curry a bit of political favor." >> The upgrade could also take years to complete. Here's what needs to happen. First, the jet must be completely taken apart to check for any spying devices. Then, new security features approved by the Pentagon would need to be installed. Air Force One is designed to be a White House in the sky. It should be able to defend against surfaceto-air missiles and even survive a nuclear attack. It also has a very strategic layout with a surgical suite and two kitchens capable of preparing up to 100 meals at a time. It can even be refueled midair given the plane's unlimited range. >> Does Trump say, "I don't care about these systems. I just like the interior. Do we cut corners and fly around in a less capable, less safe Air Force One?" Here in the US, the rise of private flying is closely tied with the history of Air Force One. Franklin Roosevelt became the first president to fly in a private purpose-built plane in 1945 when he traveled to Crimea during World War II. Roosevelt's plane had a conference room, a special wheelchair lift, a bulletproof window, and a refrigerator, the epitome of luxury at the time. In 1953, President Dwight Eisenhower became the first to officially call his plane the Air Force One, when he upgraded to a jet, a Boeing 707. 10 years later, in 1963, a Swiss American aviation company, Learjet, introduced the first private aircraft with seats for six passengers and two pilots. Its price tag started at about $650,000. In the late '7s, Boeing began converting its popular 747 model for private luxury travel. Over the next two decades, charter services emerged, allowing people to easily explore and book private trips, ushering in a new era of flying. [Applause] Qatar has been building its own aviation empire since 1993 while using planes as a diplomacy tool. In 2000, the country sent a Boeing 747 to Iraq as an expression of solidarity. In 2013, when Turkish and Lebanese hostages were released from Syria, Qatar executive quickly offered jets to escort them home. And in 2018, Qatar's Amir gifted a Boeing 747 to Turkish President Arduan, citing his trust in Turkey. I don't think there's any question that they regard aviation as a kind of an integrated well fabric if you will, economics, diplomacy, uh, military power, so hard and soft power. They there's no way they could be spending this monster amount of money on jets without having a sense of what it all means. >> Becoming an aviation powerhouse has also helped Qatar secure its position in the Middle East. When tensions escalated between Qatar and its neighbors in 2017 over its ties to Iran, Egypt, the UAE, Bahrain, and Saudi Arabia imposed a massive blockade. They denied the tiny kingdom access to their airspace and seas and closed its only land border. Qatar relied on imports for survival and the country was facing a crisis. It quickly turned to its aviation superiority to flex some muscle. The airline flew thousands of dairy cows on Qatar Airways cargo planes from Germany, the Netherlands, the US, and Australia to Doha, where a new farm was waiting to help give the country more control over its food supply. >> Aviation was their lifeline. And whether economically or diplomatically, there was that high-profile story, I believe, of using Qatari jetliners to fly dairy cows into the country so they could have milk, you know. I mean, it was it was an exceptional moment in history. >> And Qatar's influence in aviation extends beyond just owning fancy planes. The Qatar Investment Authority, the country's $500 billion sovereign wealth fund, has bought shares in other airlines. In 2025, it scooped up a 25% stake in Virgin Australia after Australia wouldn't allow Qatar Airways to expand its flights. In 2020, Qatar secured its 25% stake in IA, which operates British Airways, Iberia, and Heirlingus among others. And back in 2012, the country bought a 20% stake in London's Heathro airport. It also owns part of two African airlines, Airlink and Rwanda Air. Qatar's ties to the United States also run deep. It shelled out $8 billion over the last 20 years to help develop and maintain the Al- Uid air base, the largest US military facility in the Middle East. It houses some 10,000 troops. In May 2025, Trump said Qatar pledged another $10 billion towards the base over the coming years. These closed ties benefit some US manufacturers as well, like Boeing. Qatar Airways has an order for 210 aircraft. And Qatar Executive flies 22 private jets made by another American manufacturer, Gulfream, with a few more scheduled for delivery next year. The private jet business is also growing worldwide. Today, there are about 23,000 private jets operating around the world, and about 65% of those are based in the US. That's up from 10,000 jets operating globally in 2000. Analysts say the global business jet market could reach nearly $70 billion by 2032. But owning a private jet isn't the only option. Some invest in fractional ownership, kind of like a time share for jets, where owners share costs such as maintenance, staff salaries, and hanger space. Others book and share chartered planes, or they use jet card programs that are like a prepaid card for flight hours. Vista Jet is one company that offers such a program. >> As long as the runway is long and safe enough, we'll make it happen. >> Vista Jet started with just two Lear jets in 2004. Now it has a fleet of more than 300 aircraft around the world. The company uses a subscription-based plan in which clients buy flight hours and it replicates ownership. But what it does is that it takes away all the ownership risks where the asset depreciation, the the management of the aircraft and the operation of the aircraft. >> It's a convenience clients are willing to pay for. The flight time costs anywhere from 11,000 to about 25,000 an hour depending on the type of plane. Members have to purchase a minimum of 25 flight hours a year with a 3-year commitment. And there's a separate fuel charge on top of the hourly rates. One flight from New York to London on this aircraft type could cost you $300,000 where our average client average net worth are well over half a billion dollars. members are guaranteed a plane will be available for their flight as long as they give 24 hours notice. >> It's like doing a jigsaw puzzle. How you make sure that you have enough availability, but at the same time, we can't have too much waste. >> To avoid empty seats, the company also sells individual chartered flights outside the membership program. And they're looking for ways to reduce their carbon footprint. Studies have found that private flights are up to 14 times more polluting per passenger than flying commercial as a carbon footprint is spread across fewer people. One study calculated that 2 hours of a private flight emits more carbon dioxide than an average person creates in a year. And because these planes fly higher, the emissions are released in more sensitive parts of the atmosphere where they linger, potentially contributing to more global warming. 85% of our customers opt in for our carbon emission program. They could give us, for example, a 7-day booking notice because it help us to organize our fleet to reduce that empty leg. like Qatar executive Vista Jet is competing to meet its customers high expectations for luxury when cooking on a private jet. Um it's very much the food has to be specifically engineered so that things don't dry out and that's what they really specialize on and getting the the food from worldrenowned chefs like Nou and things like this. On the 7,500 we have two ovens which are perfect for for this. With the the private airline, we can really cater to our clients specific needs. Um, we know exactly what our clients like. Um, and we take feedback after every single flight and we write back to our company. When it comes down to owning, sharing or chartering, experts say there's one simple principle. For those who travel a lot and can afford it, owning is perhaps the way to go.

background can achieve Success Through hard work and determination but how did this nation of dreamers and doers carve its path to Prosperity what were the pivotal moments the game-changing Innovations and the defining characteristics that propelled America to the Forefront of the global stage let's delve into the heart of this captivating narrative and uncover the forces that shaped ways to improve efficiency and productivity this Spirit of innovation wasn't confined to workshops and factories it permeated every aspect of American life from agriculture to Transportation the land of invention a place where dreams took shape and possibilities became realities America's geographical blessings extend far beyond its abundant natural resources its strategic location nestled between two vast oceans provided a natural Advantage for trade and commerce with access to both the Atlantic and Pacific America was positioned to become a global trading Hub connecting with markets in Europe Asia and Beyond furthermore the vastness of the American landscape stretching from the Atlantic to the Pacific fostered a spirit of self-reliance and entrepreneurialism the challenges of settling and connecting this vast territory spurred innovation in transportation communication and infrastructure the development of canal railroads and later highways created a vast interconnected Network that facilitated the movement of goods people and ideas this interconnectedness fueled economic growth by opening up new markets promoting specialization and fostering competition America's geography also played a role in shaping its political and social landscape the absence of rigid social hierarchies and the availability of land created a more egalitarian Society compared to many European countries attracting immigrants seeking a better life this influx of people with their diverse skills and perspectives would further contribute to America's economic vitality

On the x axis we have the income&nbsp;&nbsp; percentile of the global population The chart shows how much income has grown&nbsp;&nbsp; across the population in the 20 years up to 2008. The people around the 50th percentile of income,&nbsp;&nbsp; mostly people in Asia, saw big gains.
