But this doesn’t mean every American is rich. So far we’ve only been&nbsp; looking at country averages,&nbsp;&nbsp; but inside each of these bubbles there&nbsp; are both rich people and poor people. We need to look at the inequality within countries The bubbles now show how much income&nbsp; the top 10% earn in each country. In the U.S, the top 10% earn 47% of the income. The country with highest income&nbsp; inequality is South Africa,&nbsp;&nbsp; where the top 10% get 65% of the income. We’re talking about income, which&nbsp; tells you how much money someone earns. But we should also look at the wealth,&nbsp; the accumulated worth of everything you&nbsp;&nbsp; own. That includes your savings but&nbsp; also your house, your car and your&nbsp; investments, making it arguably&nbsp; a better indicator of inequality. If we switch the chart from income to&nbsp; wealth, the inequality becomes even bigger. South Africa also has the&nbsp; highest wealth inequality,&nbsp;&nbsp; with the top 10% holding&nbsp; almost 86% of the total wealth On the other end, in the Netherlands they own 45% Wealth inequality within countries has&nbsp; generally increased in the last decades. Each country has a different&nbsp; trend, but in the U.S,&nbsp;&nbsp; the top 10% owned 71% of the wealth&nbsp; in 2021, more than 25 years earlier. This number also went up in all&nbsp; of the world’s top 10 economies. This can have negative impacts and several&nbsp; economists argue that the more unequal a society,&nbsp;&nbsp; the weaker its institutions tend&nbsp; to become over time. The World Inequality Report argues that this&nbsp; was not inevitable, but a political choice. They argue that in the 80s, leaders like&nbsp; Ronald Reagan and Margaret Thatcher pushed&nbsp;&nbsp; a series of policies that made private wealth go up and public wealth go down So their argument here is that if governments own less, then in theory they are less capable of tackling inequality. But from now on, we’re not going&nbsp; to talk about countries anymore:&nbsp;&nbsp; we’re entering global wealth tiers. If your wealth is at least 1 million dollars then you’re part of the global 1%. This could mean having 1 million dollars&nbsp;in your bank account or just owning a&nbsp; an expensive house There are 82,000,000 people in this group&nbsp; and they own 38% of the world’s wealth. To make it to the top 0.01% you need&nbsp; a wealth of at least 21 million dollars There are about 800,000 people in this&nbsp; group and they own 11% of the world’s wealth It’s estimated that private jets are&nbsp; used by about 0.003% of the population,&nbsp;&nbsp; which would be about 30% of this group. If your wealth is at least 1 billion dollars&nbsp; then you’re considered a billionaire. There are roughly 3000 billionaires in the&nbsp; world and they own 3% of the world’s wealth. Over the years, the wealth&nbsp; of global billionaires has&nbsp;&nbsp; gone up and the same is true for&nbsp; the people in the previous tier. But one billionaire takes the&nbsp; number 1 position on Forbes As of the making of this video, Elon Musk is the&nbsp;&nbsp; wealthiest person in the world with a&nbsp; net worth of almost 400 billion dollars Between 2020 and 2024 Elon Musk’s wealth&nbsp; grew at an average rate of $1300/second. If Elon Musk saw a $100 dollar bill on&nbsp;&nbsp; the floor it would literally not&nbsp; be worth his time to pick it up. So now that we’ve reached the&nbsp; top let’s revisit question 2:&nbsp;&nbsp; Has global inequality gone up since 2000? Most of my audience said yes. And that makes sense We’ve seen the rise of billionaires and the top&nbsp; 0.01%, and increasing inequality within countries But the answer, to my own surprise, is C). Global inequality has actually gone down But how is that possible? to be very sort of blunt&nbsp; reducing inequality between&nbsp;&nbsp; Malawi and UK Is much more important than reducing inequality within the uk Although inequality within&nbsp; countries went up in many regions,&nbsp;&nbsp; the inequality between countries has gone down The global top 10% now own 74% of the&nbsp; wealth, which is less than before. And the bottom 50% own 2.1% of the wealth, which&nbsp; is not much, but still higher than in 2000. Developing countries are&nbsp; catching up If we go back to 1990, the bubble chart&nbsp; looked like this. There was a big gap&nbsp;&nbsp; between the rich and the poor and almost&nbsp; 40% of people lived in low income countries. There was a big gap between the rich and the poor. But as the years passed, billions climbed out of poverty and now most of the world&nbsp; lives in middle income countries. This doesn’t mean that people aren’t&nbsp; struggling or that we shouldn’t&nbsp;&nbsp; worry about rising inequality within&nbsp; countries. But personally I also think&nbsp;&nbsp; it’s important that we acknowledge&nbsp; the progress that has been made. Most people, myself included,&nbsp;&nbsp; failed these questions because we don’t&nbsp; have an accurate picture of the world. That's the video for today Thank you for watching! FAQs:
1. Why are the median income values shown so low? And why don't they add up to the GDP if you multiply them by 365?
A) the median income is the income available for the "typical" individual including children and retired people, not just workers. That means children, retirees, and unemployed adults are factored in, which lowers the median compared to the median salary, which only considers those in paid employment
B) the figures are shown in 2017 international dollars PPP, which means they are adjusted to the local cost of living to make it comparable with the other countries
C) GDP per capita is an average (which is skewed up by the people at the top of the curve). Additionally, it includes income from capital (e.g., dividends, rents) and government and corporate income.

Note: I wrongly referred to median income as 'median wage' as explained below

- Prince Salman's Daughters: The Richest Kids in the World: Kind: captions Language: en What do you get when your dad is one of the richest men alive? Private jets, goldplated playrooms, and billion-dollar luxury. That's the reality for Prince Salman's daughters. In this video, we're diving into the lifestyle of these young royals. So lavish even fairy tales can't compete. Trust me, you won't believe how they live. What it means to be MBS's daughters. After coming of age, Prince Salman, popularly known as NBS, assumed several positions in the inner workings of Saudi political affairs. He went from becoming the youngest defense minister in the world, crown prince, deputy prime minister, and de facto ruler of Saudi Arabia. Away from these roles and the heavy demands of being in power, MBS is a father of five children from his longtime marriage to Princess Sarah bint Mashure bin Abdulaziz al-Saud. MBS's children are Prince Salman, Prince Mashure, Princess Fda, Princess Nura, and Prince Abdulaziz. Prince Salman's daughters are pretty much shielded from the outside world, but are crucial individuals of Saudi Arabia's royal structure, though their lives remain private. Princesses Nora and FA have enjoyed an ultra-privileged life since their parents ushered them into the world. The young princesses belong to the bloodline of an oil rich nation known as one of the largest crude exporters in the world. They enjoy a lifestyle steeped in luxury, backed by the royal family's estimated $1.4 trillion wealth derived from oil, real estate, and investments. Additionally, they have access to their father's net worth of approximately $25 billion. Thanks to their father's elite taste in luxury, Nura and Foda have experienced some of the most coveted properties on Earth. One of them is the Chateau Louie 11th, a modern masterpiece built to reflect 17th century grandeur. The price, a jaw-dropping $320 million, the Chateau covers over 50,000 acres and is a short distance from the historic palace of Versailles, reflecting the prince's love for luxury and extravagance. The Chat Lou 11th is not just a home. It is a masterpiece of architecture and design. Its interiors are adorned with state-of-the-art technology and lavish amenities that redefine what it means to live in luxury. The estate features a wine celler capable of holding 3,000 bottles, a private movie theater, and two grand ballrooms. One of the chat's standout features is its underwater meditation room, a serene escape surrounded by koi and sturgeon in the castle's moat. This unique space offers a tranquil environment for peaceful contemplation, a luxury that only a trillionaire could afford. Above this, a Kurara marble statue of Louis the 14th stands guard, reminding everyone of the historical inspiration behind the estate. The grounds are equally impressive, featuring a complex that stretches over 1.2 mi with gilded statues and a garden designed to rival the majesty of Versailles. The Chateau Louie 11th is also a high-tech marvel, allowing control of lighting and water features with a tap on a smartphone. This blend of oldworld charm and modern convenience creates a living experience that is both luxurious and cutting edge. The estate is a perfect example of how Prince Salman seamlessly combines tradition with innovation, creating a space that is both timeless and contemporary. Beyond the shadow, Prince Salman's palatial residences in Saudi Arabia further exemplify his taste for luxury. The Alyamar Palace is a masterpiece of design, featuring Italian marble floors and intricately carved ceilings and walls. Every detail within the palace is meticulously crafted with furniture that is exceptionally comfortable and elegant, fit for royalty. The palace is a haven of luxury, offering a retreat where the prince can escape the demands of his public life. The Urka Palace is equally impressive, filled with highquality furnishings and luxurious details that enhance its elegant atmosphere. The artwork on the walls is carefully chosen to complement the palace's design, creating a harmonious blend of art and architecture. Even everyday items in these palaces, such as goldplated Kleenex dispensers, reflect a lifestyle where simplicity is transformed into luxury. Prince Salman's properties are not just confined to Saudi Arabia. His influence extends globally with luxury apartments in New York City, including a penthouse at 432 Park Avenue, one of the tallest residential buildings in the world. This penthouse is more than just a home. It is a symbol of his presence and influence on the global stage. Each property in his portfolio is a testament to his ability to blend luxury with functionality, creating spaces that are both beautiful and practical. Prince Muhammad bin Salman's lifestyle is woven with threads of art, culture, and unparalleled luxury. When your dad is one of the richest and most powerful men on the planet, your walls don't get the usual live, laugh, love poster from Teu. Nope, they get Da Vinci. Prince Muhammad bin Salman is not just about oil and politics. He also has a serious eye for art, the kind that comes with a price tag the size of a small country's budget. Take the famous Salvatore Mundi painting by Leonardo da Vinci, for example. The prince allegedly dropped over $450 million on it. That's not a typo. That's an entire blockbuster movie budget on a single canvas. The painting's acquisition was shrouded in mystery with fierce competition from the Qatari royals, hence the need for anonymity. Now, what does this mean for his daughters? Imagine growing up thinking Da Vinci is a family friend. Their version of hanging a painting is watching palace staff in gloves carefully mount centuries old masterpieces next to the flat screen. And instead of fingerpainting in art class, they're strolling past Monet originals on their way to breakfast. They don't just enjoy art, they live in it. Their rooms are probably curated like a mini Lou, only with better lighting and personalized gold name plates. And when they host parties, guests get appetizers, music, and a casual peek at a priceless sculpture chilling in the hallway like it's no big deal. The prince may be the one buying the art, but his daughters are clearly the ones enjoying the vibe. It's luxury, sure, but with a museum twist, one where the gift shop is a private jet, and the security guards are armed with more than walkie-talkies. Prince Salman's passion for art extends beyond the Salvatore Mundi. His collection includes a range of exquisite pieces that reflect his refined taste and appreciation for beauty. Each artwork in his collection is carefully curated, showcasing his ability to blend historical significance with contemporary relevance. This dedication to art is a testament to his desire to preserve and celebrate cultural heritage while also embracing modernity. Complimenting his art collection are Prince Salman's luxurious yachts, Pegasus and Serene, which redefine elegance on the high seas. If there's one thing richer than oil money, it's yacht money. They feature top-notch amenities, including helipads, indoor swimming pools, spas, and because why not, a submarine. Naturally, Nora and Far get first class access to these floating palaces. Birthday parties on the yacht. Girls weekend on the yacht. Casual sunset dinner, you guessed it, on the yacht. While the rest of us are figuring out how to book a cabin on a cruise deal site, they're deciding which Mediterranean port to stop at for gelato. And forget about beach towels and sunscreen. These yachts have butlers, chefs trained at Michelin star restaurants, and a DJ booth for spontaneous dance parties. You think your Spotify playlist slaps? Try listening to it while sipping gold flaked mocktails in international waters. Pegasus, acquired in 2014, is not just a yacht. It is a kingdom on water. This floating palace features interiors that resemble a golden dream with every chair resembling a throne and tables that seem to shine brighter than the sun. The yacht's amenities are legendary, including a spa with 24 karat gold infused oils, a gym more advanced than an Olympic training center, a cinema, and dining areas fit for a king or a small army. The cost of this maritime marvel was a cool $200 million pocket change for the crown prince, the Serene. Originally owned by vodka tycoon Yuri Sheffer, the Serene makes the Pegasus look like a rowboat. This massive yacht, measuring 134 m long, is more luxurious than the entire French Riviera. It hosts up to 24 guests in stateooms that make Buckingham Palace look basic. The Serene is a world of its own with amenities that include a spa, gym, movie theater with a screen visible from space, an onboard helicopter, and a submarine for underwater adventures. Now, let's shift gears. In the world of luxury automobiles, few collections can rival the opulence and diversity of Crown Prince Muhammad bin Salman's car collection. This extraordinary assemblage of vehicles is not just evidence of his immense wealth, but also a reflection of his passion for speed, style, and cuttingedge technology. his daughters. They're not just posing next to the cars, they're basking in the rare opportunity of riding in them. Prince Salman's stunning garage houses a Bugatti Chiron, a supercar that stands as a pinnacle of automotive engineering. With a top speed of 261 mph and the ability to accelerate from 0 to 60 in a mere 2.4 seconds, the Chiron is a marvel of speed and precision. Priced at $3 million, this masterpiece of design and performance is a fitting symbol of the Prince's love for the extraordinary. The Chiron's sleek lines and powerful engine make it a standout in any collection. And for Prince Salman, it represents the ultimate in luxury and performance. Another gem in the Prince's collection is the Lamborghini Aventador. A car known for its aggressive design and exhilarating power. The Aventador's V12 engine delivers a heartpounding driving experience, making it a favorite among those who crave speed and excitement. The McLaren P1 with its hybrid technology and track ready performance showcases Prince Salman's appreciation for innovation and sustainability. This limited production hypercar combines electric and gasoline power to deliver an exhilarating ride. Reflecting the Prince's forward-thinking approach to luxury, adding a touch of classic elegance to the collection is the Rolls-Royce Phantom. A car renowned for its timeless design and unparalleled comfort. The Phantom's handcrafted interiors and smooth ride make it a symbol of luxury and refinement. For Prince Salman, the Phantom is more than just a car. It is an experience of opulence and sophistication. Each detail, from the plush leather seats to the meticulously crafted dashboard, speaks to the artistry and craftsmanship that define Rolls-Royce. The Prince's collection also includes the Ferrari LaFerrari, a hybrid supercar that combines breathtaking performance with cuttingedge technology. He also owns a Mercedes-Benz Gwagon. Moving further into the realm of luxury, Prince Muhammad bin Salman's private jets stand as unparalleled symbols of opulence and sophistication. These airborne palaces are not just about getting from one place to another. They reflect the prince's penchant for the extraordinary. Each jet in his fleet is a masterpiece of engineering and design, offering a glimpse into a world where luxury knows no bounds. Prince Salman's aviation fleet consists of his custom Airbus A380, a flying palace that redefineses the concept of luxury travel. This aircraft, which costs a staggering $450 million, is a marvel of modern engineering and design. Inside, the Airbus A380 is a world of its own. Featuring a concert hall, a Turkish bath, and even a garage for the Prince's cars. The interiors are designed to the Prince's exacting standards. With every detail meticulously crafted to provide the ultimate in comfort and elegance, complementing the Airbus A380, is the Boeing 747. Another masterpiece of aviation that has been transformed into a private haven for the prince. This jumbo jet, typically a symbol of commercial aviation, has been customized to offer the ultimate in airborne luxury. The interiors are a blend of modern design and traditional elegance. With every detail reflecting the prince's refined taste, the Boeing 747 offers a range of amenities designed to provide the utmost comfort and convenience. From spacious lounges to private suites, the jet is equipped to meet the needs of the most discerning traveler. The dining area with its gourmet kitchen allows the prince to enjoy exquisite meals prepared by top chefs, while the entertainment system offers a wide range of options for in-flight entertainment. the rich family history. Now, let's talk about where all this wealth comes from. Because Prince Muhammad bin Salman didn't just stumble upon a treasure chest. He was born into one the size of the Arabian Peninsula. It all starts with oil. Back in the 1930s, Saudi Arabia discovered that its deserts weren't just good for sand. They were sitting on one of the largest oil reserves in the world. The discovery of oil was the result of a partnership between the Saudi government and the California Arabian Standard Oil Company, a precursor to what would become Saudi Aramco. This collaboration was initiated by King Abdul Aziz who recognized the potential of oil to transform his nation. The agreement allowed the American company to explore and extract oil in exchange for a share of the profits providing the kingdom with a muchneeded source of revenue. The oil reserves beneath Saudi Arabia were among the largest in the world and the revenue generated from their extraction catapulted the house of Saud into the ranks of the world's wealthiest families. The newfound wealth enabled the royal family to consolidate their power, invest in the kingdom's infrastructure, and embark on ambitious development projects. Saudi Aramco, the crown jewel of the Saudi economy, stands as a monumental symbol of the kingdom's wealth and influence. Its significance to the Saudi economy cannot be overstated. The company is the backbone of the kingdom's financial stability, generating the lion's share of government revenue. Its operations account for a substantial portion of the country's GDP and its exports are a major contributor to the kingdom's trade balance. The origins of Saudi Aramco can be traced back to the California Arabian Standard Oil Company, which struck oil in the Dharmam Dome near Dhran. This discovery marked the beginning of a partnership that would evolve into Saudi Aramco, a name derived from the Arabian-American Oil Company. Over the decades, Saudi Aramco has expanded its operations, becoming a vertically integrated oil giant with activities spanning exploration, production, refining, distribution, and marketing. Saudi Aramco's significance to the Saudi economy cannot be overstated. The company is the backbone of the kingdom's financial stability, generating the lion's share of government revenue. Its operations account for a substantial portion of the country's GDP, and its exports are a major contributor to the kingdom's trade balance. The revenue generated by Saudi Aramco has funded the kingdom's ambitious development projects from infrastructure to education and healthcare transforming Saudi Arabia into a modern nation. The company's success has provided the House of Saud with a steady stream of income enabling them to maintain their opulent lifestyle and exert influence both domestically and internationally. The royal family's control over Saudi Aramco has allowed them to leverage the company's resources to further their strategic interests both within the kingdom and on the global stage. As the world's largest oil producer, Saudi Aramco's decisions on production levels and pricing have far-reaching implications affecting economies and energy policies worldwide. This influence has enhanced the royal family's standing on the international stage, providing them with a platform to shape global affairs. Beyond the vast oil reserves and the monumental success of Saudi Aramco, the Saudi royal family has strategically diversified its wealth through extensive real estate holdings and a myriad of investments across various sectors. Real estate is a cornerstone of the Saudi royal family's wealth. The House of Saud owns vast tracks of land within Saudi Arabia, including prime real estate in major urban centers such as Riyad, Jedha, and Mecca. These holdings are not merely residential or commercial properties. They include some of the most prestigious and strategically located parcels of land in the kingdom. The value of these properties has soared over the years, driven by the kingdom's rapid urbanization and economic growth. In Riyad, the capital city, the royal family's real estate portfolio includes luxury hotels, shopping malls, and high-end residential complexes. These properties generate substantial rental income and capital appreciation, contributing significantly to the family's wealth. The development of these properties has also played a crucial role in transforming Riyad into a modern metropolis, attracting businesses and tourists from around the world. The royal family's real estate interests extend beyond the borders of Saudi Arabia. They have invested in luxury properties in some of the world's most sought-after locations, including London, Paris, and New York. These international holdings not only diversify their investment portfolio, but also provide a hedge against fluctuations in the oil market. The royal family's global real estate investments are a testament to their strategic foresight and their ability to capitalize on opportunities in the international property market. In addition to real estate, the Saudi royal family has made significant investments in various industries, further enhancing their wealth and influence. They have stakes in major corporations across sectors such as finance, telecommunications, technology, and entertainment. These investments have provided the royal family with additional streams of income and have positioned them as key players in the global business arena. One notable example is the public investment fund, PIF, the sovereign wealth fund of Saudi Arabia, which is closely linked to the royal family. The PIF is like the royal family's piggy bank, except instead of coins, it holds billions in global assets. The PIF owns stakes in everything from Uber to football clubs. If it's profitable, shiny, or famous, chances are the PIF has a piece of it. In its early years, the PIF played a crucial role in the kingdom's economic growth. By investing in key sectors such as energy, transportation, and telecommunications, the fund helped to build the infrastructure necessary for a thriving economy. These investments were aligned with the broader goals of the Saudi government which sought to diversify the economy and reduce its dependence on oil revenues. As the global economy evolved, so too did the PIF strategy. In the 1980s and 1990s, the fund began to expand its focus beyond domestic investments, exploring opportunities in international markets. This shift was driven by a recognition that global diversification could provide greater financial returns and reduce the kingdom's exposure to oil price volatility. The PIF's international investments during this period were primarily in traditional sectors such as real estate and financial services, reflecting a cautious approach to global markets. The 21st century marked a new era for the PIF as the fund embraced a more aggressive and diversified investment strategy. Under the leadership of Crown Prince Muhammad bin Salman, the PIF has been transformed into a global investment powerhouse with a renewed focus on high growth sectors such as technology, entertainment, and renewable energy. This transformation is a key component of vision 2030. The kingdom's ambitious plan to diversify its economy and reduce its reliance on oil. One of the most significant changes in the PIF's strategy has been its focus on technology and innovation by investing in companies like Uber, Lucid Motors, Alphabet, and Microsoft. The fund is positioning Saudi Arabia as a leader in the digital economy. These investments are not just about financial returns. They are part of a broader strategy to foster innovation and technological advancement within the kingdom. By partnering with some of the world's most innovative companies, the PIF is gaining access to cuttingedge technologies and insights that can drive economic growth and diversification. So, what does all this mean for the youngest royals like Princess Nura and Princess Fod? It means their playroom has probably seen more wealth than an entire Wall Street office. These little princesses aren't just rich. They're born into the kind of wealth that makes fictional princesses look underfunded. While most kids play with plastic tiaras, they're toddling past real crown jewels. Their bedtime stories might be read from gold leaf books under crystal chandeliers. And don't be surprised if their first words included something like diversified portfolio. The future of Prince Salman's daughters. So, what lies ahead for Princess Nora and Princess Fa? Aside from diamond tiaras and glittering galas. Well, if their royal relatives are anything to go by, these little princesses are in for a future of big titles and even bigger boardrooms. Several women in the Saudi royal family already hold powerful roles in business, philanthropy, education and government. Think ministers, CEOs, and highlevel advisers with serious influence and enviable handbags. One of them is Princess Reema Bint Bander Al-Saud. Remma has carved a unique path that blends diplomacy, education, sports, and philanthropy. Princess Al Jawara bint Talal Saud and Princess Sora bint Saud al- Saud are two remarkable figures within the house of Saud each making significant contributions to business innovation, fashion and philanthropy. Princess Al Jara Bint Talal Saud is a visionary leader whose work in business innovation has garnered international recognition. By the time Nura and Father are old enough for career talks, they might have to decide between launching a global fashion empire, chairing a royal fund, or maybe even founding a space agency. Because why not? with private tutors, access to the world's best education, and a network that could call a sultan for references. Their LinkedIn profiles are going to be wild. While the little princesses rule the golden halls of Riyad across the pond, there's another little royal making waves with tiaras and headlines. Meet Princess Charlotte of the British royal family. She may be tiny, but her bankability is huge. Like fashion empire, huge. Princess Charlotte is already one of the richest kids in the world. Experts estimate her potential net worth at over $3 billion. Yes, with a B. That's thanks to her influence in the children's fashion world known as the Charlotte effect. If she wears it, it sells out instantly. She's basically a mini influencer with a crown. Her inheritance is no joke either. With royal estates, jewelry, and a cut of the family fortune, Charlotte is set for life and then some. She's also third in line to the British throne, which means she could one day be the ruler of England with a royal wave and a killer wardrobe. So while Princess Nura and Princess Fod are living their best lives in Saudi palaces, Princess Charlotte is doing the same thing British style, different castles, same level of sparkle. While most of us worry about losing Wi-Fi signal, MBS's daughters worry about things like which Picasso looks better in the study or should we take the jet or the yacht this weekend? It's a different world, a shinier, more curated, goldplated world. Sure, they were born into privilege, but hey, if you're going to live a life of excess, you might as well do it with taste. And Da Vinci, a Lambo, and a 400 ft yacht definitely count. As for the rest of us, we'll just be over here googling free virtual museum tours and pretending our scented candle is luxury living. As we reflect on the remarkable journey of these young princesses, it is clear that their influence extends far beyond their years. They stand as a symbol of the modern royal families, blending tradition with contemporary relevance. And their story continues to unfold, promising even greater impact in the future.
